title: Four-state discrimination for a pair of spin qubits via gate reflectometry
abstract: Single-electron spin qubits defined in quantum dots are used as building blocks of a semiconductor-based quantum computer. Readout in a scaled-up version of such a quantum computer is expected to rely on the Pauli Spin Blockade (PSB) mechanism. A desired functionality of PSB readout is that it reveals two bits of information on the two spin qubits that are involved in the process, such that the four computational basis states can be discriminated. In this work, we propose and quantitatively analyze an experimental procedure, based on gate reflectometry, which enables this four-state discrimination in a single measurement. We provide an intuitive recipe to maximize the contrast between the quantum capacitances of the four basis states. Focusing on silicon double quantum dots equipped with a micromagnet, we quantify how amplifier noise and phonon-mediated relaxation influence readout fidelity. Our results highlight a realistic opportunity to mitigate the overhead of readout ancilla qubits in a spin-based quantum computer.
one_liner: Analyzes a gate-reflectometry protocol that can distinguish all four basis states of a two-spin qubit pair in one PSB-based measurement.
why_it_matters: Single-shot four-state spin readout could cut ancilla overhead and simplify scaling of silicon spin-qubit processors.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a gate-reflectometry readout method for a silicon double quantum dot with a micromagnet that resolves the four computational basis states of two single-electron spin qubits in one measurement. The analysis centers on maximizing contrast in state-dependent quantum capacitance under Pauli spin blockade conditions. It quantitatively includes amplifier noise and phonon-mediated relaxation to estimate realistic readout fidelity. The work provides an experimentally grounded recipe for multiplexable, higher-information spin readout in semiconductor hardware.
main_result: It identifies a realistic parameter regime where gate reflectometry can discriminate all four two-qubit basis states in a single PSB measurement.
application_angle: A four-outcome readout primitive is directly relevant to scalable semiconductor quantum processors because it can reduce readout circuitry and ancilla-qubit overhead.

title: The Dirac sea of phase: Unifying phase paradoxes and Talbot revivals in multimode waveguides
abstract: The quantum mechanical description of phase remains a fundamental challenge, with theoretical efforts tracing from the early works of London and Dirac to discrete formalisms. In this work, we extend the action-angle formalism to the Helmholtz-Schrödinger equation by introducing a phase-dependent wavefunction $φ(θ, t)$ residing in the Hardy space $H^2(\mathbb{D})$. This mathematical structure, defined by functions analytic on the unit disk with square-integrable boundary values, naturally ensures the positivity of the energy spectrum while providing a rigorous framework for wave dynamics in photonic systems. We demonstrate that establishing a self-adjoint phase operator requires extending the Hilbert space to $L^2$, a procedure that necessitates the admission of negative energy states. We interpret these states through an analogy with the Dirac sea, where the existence of antiphase or antiphoton modes provides a conceptual framework for understanding the fundamental limits of phase localization and quantum uncertainty. This formalism is applied to light propagation in multimode waveguides characterized by anharmonic refractive index profiles. By mapping modal dispersion to our phase representation, we show that the deviation of propagation constants from linear spacing governs the spatial evolution of the optical field. This approach offers a clear mechanism for the emergence of periodic self-imaging known as the Talbot effect, the generation of fractional revivals, and the formation of complex fractal interference patterns, providing a robust toolkit for the characterization and design of multimode interference devices.
one_liner: Introduces a Hardy-space phase formalism that links quantum phase paradoxes to Talbot revivals and multimode interference in waveguides.
why_it_matters: It offers a design-oriented theoretical framework for predicting and engineering self-imaging, fractional revivals, and interference structure in photonic devices.
detailed_summary: The authors extend the action-angle formalism to the Helmholtz-Schrödinger equation by defining a phase-dependent wavefunction in Hardy space, which preserves a positive energy spectrum for photonic dynamics. They show that making the phase operator self-adjoint requires extension to an L2 space with negative-energy-like states, interpreted as an analogue of a Dirac sea of phase. Mapping modal dispersion in anharmonic multimode waveguides into this representation, they derive how nonuniform propagation-constant spacing drives Talbot self-imaging, fractional revivals, and fractal interference. The framework is positioned as a mathematical tool for analyzing and designing multimode interference behavior.
main_result: The work unifies phase-operator pathologies and multimode Talbot dynamics within a single Hardy-space formalism tied to waveguide modal dispersion.
application_angle: This could inform the design of multimode photonic components for beam shaping, self-imaging, and interference-based optical processing.

title: Quantum information advantage based on Bell inequalities
abstract: Recently, Kretschmer et al. [KGD+25] presented an experimental demonstration of a proposed quantum information advantage protocol. We present an alternate proposal based on a relation derived from parallel-repeated CHSH games. Our memory measure is based on an information measure and is different from [KGD+25], where they count the number of qubits. Our proposal has an efficient verifier and a noise-robust quantum prover which is arguably much more efficient compared to [KGD+25].
one_liner: Proposes a Bell-inequality-based quantum information advantage protocol with an efficient verifier and a noise-robust, lower-overhead quantum prover.
why_it_matters: A more efficient verification protocol strengthens the practical case for near-term demonstrations of quantum information advantage with controllable hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper presents an alternative quantum information advantage protocol derived from relations for parallel-repeated CHSH games. Unlike prior work that measures memory by counting qubits, it uses an information-theoretic memory measure, changing the resource accounting for the prover. The proposed verifier is efficient, and the protocol is argued to tolerate noise while requiring a substantially more efficient quantum prover than earlier proposals. The result is a cleaner route to experimentally test separations grounded in Bell nonlocality.
main_result: It gives a Bell-inequality-based advantage protocol with efficient verification and improved prover efficiency under a noise-robust formulation.
application_angle: The protocol could lower experimental overhead for demonstrating certified quantum advantage on photonic or other nonlocality-capable hardware platforms.

title: Enhanced Rydberg Blockade through RF-tuned Förster Resonance
abstract: Enhancing interactions between Rydberg atoms is a key challenge in contemporary quantum technologies. Stronger interactions enable faster Rydberg gates in digital processors and larger entangled states in analog simulation. Achieving the same interaction strength at lower principal quantum number addresses current constraints in available Rabi frequency and field sensitivity in large scale tweezer or cavity QED experiments. Here, we demonstrate a new technique using AC Stark shifts from a microwave drive to tune into a Förster resonance, thereby modifying the interaction scaling with distance from $1/R^6$ to $1/R^3$. We validate enhanced Rydberg interactions (in strength and range) by probing cavity Rydberg polariton blockade at $n=44$ in $^{87}$Rb, improving from $g^{(2)}(0) = 1.0 (1)$ in the Van-der-Waals regime to $g^{(2)}(0) = 0.38 (1)$ in the dipolar regime on the Förster resonance. Importantly, our technique allows minimal shifts of the original Rydberg state, suppressing detuning errors in gate protocols while maintaining quadratic insensitivity to DC electric fields.
one_liner: Uses microwave AC Stark tuning to reach Förster resonance and boost Rydberg blockade strength and range in cavity polaritons.
why_it_matters: Stronger blockade at lower principal quantum number can enable faster gates and more scalable neutral-atom or cavity-QED quantum devices.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate RF-assisted tuning into a Förster resonance using microwave-induced AC Stark shifts, changing the effective Rydberg interaction from van der Waals-like 1/R^6 to dipolar 1/R^3 scaling. They test the method in 87Rb cavity Rydberg polaritons at n=44, where the on-resonance regime improves photon antibunching from g^(2)(0)=1.0(1) to 0.38(1). The scheme enhances both interaction strength and range while keeping the original Rydberg state nearly unshifted, which suppresses gate detuning errors. It also preserves quadratic insensitivity to DC electric fields, important for stable operation.
main_result: RF-tuned Förster resonance yields substantially stronger and longer-range blockade, evidenced by improved polariton antibunching at relatively low n.
application_angle: This technique is useful for faster neutral-atom gates, stronger photonic nonlinearities, and larger entangled-state generation in tweezer and cavity-QED architectures.

title: Practical implementation of arbitrary nonlocal controlled-unitary gate via indefinite causal order
abstract: Quantum gate teleportation enables the implementation of nonlocal quantum operations without direct interactions between distant nodes. We propose an efficient protocol for implementing arbitrary controlled-unitary (CU) gates acting on two spatially separated parties via indefinite causal order (ICO). By establishing a maximally entanglement between two remote nodes and coherently superposing orders of single-qubit gates, our protocol circumvents the drawback of complex local two-qubit operations. This ICO-based approach enables full programmability of CU gates by adjusting the inherent single-qubit operations, offering advantages over conventional fixed causal-order methods in terms of reduced circuit complexity and improved experimental flexibility. Furthermore, we develop an optical construction to implement the polarization CU gate using a stable and reciprocal Sagnac interferometer. Our work establishes a practical framework for scalable distributed quantum computation with flexible operations.
one_liner: Proposes a practical scheme for arbitrary remote controlled-unitary gates using indefinite causal order and an optical Sagnac implementation.
why_it_matters: Programmable nonlocal gates with simpler local operations are valuable for distributed quantum computing and networked quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a protocol for implementing arbitrary controlled-unitary gates between spatially separated parties by combining shared entanglement with a coherent superposition of gate orders. By exploiting indefinite causal order, it avoids complex local two-qubit operations and keeps programmability in single-qubit controls. The paper also details an optical realization for polarization-encoded CU gates in a reciprocal Sagnac interferometer, emphasizing stability and practicality. The architecture targets flexible remote gate synthesis rather than fixed-operation network links.
platform_or_system: polarization photonics in a Sagnac interferometer
main_result: It shows that arbitrary nonlocal CU gates can be implemented with indefinite causal order using only entanglement plus tunable single-qubit operations.
application_angle: The protocol is relevant to scalable quantum networks because it reduces local gate complexity for remote logic across distributed nodes.

title: A Bipartite Quantum Key Distribution Protocol Based on Indefinite Causal Order
abstract: We propose a bipartite quantum key distribution (QKD) protocol based on causal nonseparability: the presence of a resource -- a process matrix -- that does not correspond to any definite causal order between two parties. In our protocol, Alice and Bob perform local operations arranged in a ``causal-order guessing game,'' whereby each round yields an 85.35\% probability of matching bits when the communication is undisturbed. This raw matching probability (or equivalently, a $\sim14.65\%$ error rate) is amenable to standard forward error-correction strategies. We further discuss the practical construction of the QKD protocol using indefinite causal order, where several different scenarios are deeply analyzed.
one_liner: Introduces a bipartite QKD protocol that uses indefinite causal order as the cryptographic resource in a causal-order guessing game.
why_it_matters: It expands QKD design beyond standard channel models and suggests a new route to secure communication using causally nonseparable processes.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a two-party QKD scheme in which Alice and Bob exploit a causally nonseparable process matrix and extract raw key bits from a causal-order guessing game. In the ideal undisturbed setting, each round gives an 85.35% bit-match probability, corresponding to a raw error rate near 14.65%, which the authors note is compatible with standard forward error correction. The manuscript further discusses practical constructions and analyzes several implementation scenarios based on indefinite causal order. The protocol positions causal nonseparability itself as the operative communication resource.
main_result: It defines a bipartite QKD protocol based on causal nonseparability and quantifies an achievable raw key correlation of 85.35% per round.
application_angle: If implementable, the scheme could open a new class of quantum communication protocols where security primitives are built from indefinite causal order rather than fixed channel structure.

title: A Realistic Framework for Quantum Sensing under Finite Resources
abstract: Quantum-enhanced sensing is commonly benchmarked using the quantum Fisher information (QFI), often interpreted as a direct indicator of achievable precision. However, this quantity acquires operational meaning only within a fully specified inference framework that consistently incorporates state preparation, measurement design, resource accounting, estimator construction, prior information, and finite data effects. Here we establish a realistic end-to-end framework for quantum sensing under finite resources and identify general principles required for operationally meaningful performance assessment. A central conceptual point is that the relevant unit of estimation is not a single detection event but the inference data set required to construct a consistent estimator. We apply this approach to several paradigmatic sensing strategies frequently cited in the literature. Revisiting phase estimation with NOON states within a Bayesian framework under equal total photon resources, we explicitly construct optimal estimators and show that such schemes offer no performance advantage over repeated classical interferometry for global phase estimation with finite prior width. The apparent Heisenberg-like scaling arises predominantly from prior constraints rather than from information gained in the measurement, which is operationally negligible in the resource-normalized sense considered here. We further analyse Holland-Burnett interferometry and homodyne detection with squeezed states, demonstrating how estimator construction and repetition number determine the attainable precision and when QFI provides a reliable diagnostic. Our results clarify the conditions under which nonclassical resources lead to genuine metrological advantages and provide a practical methodology for designing and evaluating quantum sensing protocols under realistic experimental constraints.
one_liner: This paper builds an end-to-end finite-resource framework showing when quantum sensing claims remain operationally valid in realistic inference settings.
why_it_matters: It reframes metrology benchmarks around estimators, priors, and finite data, helping experiments avoid overstating quantum advantage from QFI alone.
detailed_summary: The work formulates quantum sensing as a complete inference pipeline including state preparation, measurement choice, total resource accounting, prior information, estimator construction, and finite sample effects. Using this framework, it re-examines phase estimation with NOON states and finds no practical gain over repeated classical interferometry for global phase estimation under equal total photon budgets and finite prior width. It further analyzes Holland-Burnett interferometry and squeezed-state homodyne sensing to identify when QFI is a faithful indicator and when estimator and repetition choices dominate achievable precision. A key technical message is that the operational unit is the full dataset needed for a consistent estimator, not a single detection event.
main_result: It shows that finite-resource, resource-normalized Bayesian analysis can remove apparent Heisenberg-like advantages, especially for global phase estimation with NOON states.
application_angle: The framework gives experimentalists a practical method to design and benchmark sensing protocols under realistic photon budgets, priors, and finite-data constraints.

title: Mid-Infrared Modulation of Quantum Emitters in Hexagonal Boron Nitride
abstract: Single photon emitters (SPEs) are promising building blocks for practical devices in quantum technologies. Traditionally, these systems are excited using off-resonant visible light through their phonon transitions, yet this process remains poorly understood. Here, we explore the interaction of mid-infrared (MIR) excitation on the properties of SPEs in hexagonal boron nitride. Notably, we present a reversible, non-destructive method to enhance emission from blue SPEs using MIR co-excitation. By resonantly driving defect-localized in-plane infrared-active optical phonon modes near 7.3 um, the MIR field modulates carrier dynamics through a phonon-assisted recombination. This unique feature, not observed previously for defects in solids, is a promising reservoir in a growing toolkit to modulate quantum emitters at room temperature for their use in practical quantum technologies.
one_liner: Mid-infrared co-excitation resonantly drives hBN defect phonons to reversibly boost emission from room-temperature single-photon emitters.
why_it_matters: It introduces a non-destructive knob for modulating solid-state quantum emitters through infrared-active phonons, expanding practical control options for integrated photonics.
detailed_summary: The authors study single-photon emitters in hexagonal boron nitride under combined visible and mid-infrared excitation and observe reversible enhancement of blue-emitter output. The effect is linked to resonant driving of defect-localized in-plane infrared-active optical phonon modes near 7.3 um, which modifies carrier dynamics through phonon-assisted recombination. This provides a distinct control pathway not previously reported for solid-state defects and remains non-destructive under operation. The result is especially relevant because the modulation occurs at room temperature, where practical devices are most attractive.
main_result: Resonant MIR excitation near 7.3 um reversibly enhances emission from blue hBN single-photon emitters via phonon-assisted recombination dynamics.
application_angle: Infrared phonon control could become a device-level tuning mechanism for brighter, room-temperature quantum light sources in photonic chips and sensors.

title: The robustness of composite pulses elucidated by classical mechanics. II. The role of initial state imperfection
abstract: In nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), Composite Pulses (CPs) are widely used to correct for pulse imperfections, e.g., RF field inhomogeneity and resonance offset. Although robust pulse sequences have been developed throughout the years, the imperfection of the initial state has not been widely discussed in the literature as an additional systematic error. In previous work, we developed a classical canonical framework to perform stability analysis and used this as a measure of CP robustness. In that work, a single initial condition was allowed to evolve under various pulse imperfections. The current work extends this approach to $2D$ distributions of initial conditions on the Bloch Sphere; the objective is to minimize the area in order to preserve coherence, while maximizing population inversion of the entire distribution. As a case study, we investigate Levitt's $90(x)180(y)90(x)$ pulse sequence, when there is a spread in initial conditions. The canonical framework enables us to assess the robustness of Levitt's pulse sequence, and we find that it is maintained to a great extent even when considering a spread of initial conditions. Nevertheless, by conducting a numerical optimization, we have identified several variants of Levitt's pulse sequence that produce a larger coherent population inversion when there is a spread in initial conditions.
one_liner: A classical canonical analysis shows composite pulses remain robust even with initial-state spread, and optimized variants improve coherent inversion.
why_it_matters: It addresses a neglected experimental error source, initial-state imperfection, and offers better pulse designs for robust spin control.
detailed_summary: Extending earlier stability analysis, the paper propagates two-dimensional distributions of initial Bloch-sphere states rather than a single initial condition to quantify robustness against state preparation errors. The objective is to minimize distribution area to preserve coherence while maximizing inversion across the whole ensemble. Applied to Levitt's 90(x)180(y)90(x) sequence, the analysis shows substantial robustness persists even with spread in initial conditions. Numerical optimization then identifies modified sequences that deliver stronger coherent population inversion for imperfect initial-state ensembles.
main_result: Levitt's composite pulse remains largely robust to initial-state spread, and optimized variants can outperform it in coherent population inversion.
application_angle: The approach can improve calibration of control pulses in spin-based quantum platforms where imperfect initialization is a real systematic limitation.

title: Microwave response of electrically driven spins in a three-qubit quantum processor
abstract: In electric dipole spin resonance (EDSR), a single spin is electrically driven in the field gradient produced by a micromagnet. While EDSR has enabled high fidelity gate operations in many devices, there are reports of unexpected non-linearities in the Rabi frequency as a function of microwave drive amplitude. We carefully measure the response of Loss-DiVincenzo (LD) single spin qubits to resonant drives as well as simultaneous resonant and off-resonant drives, as would be encountered in a realistic quantum processor. With the microwave amplitude carefully calibrated, we find that the Rabi frequency scales linearly with drive amplitude, even when all three spins are driven simultaneously. We also determine that heating-induced resonance frequency shifts from off-resonant drives are comparable to typical temporal drifts. Our results indicate that the previously observed nonlinear response is not a general feature of LD spin qubits.
one_liner: Careful calibration of a three-spin processor finds EDSR Rabi frequency stays linear with drive amplitude, even during simultaneous multi-qubit driving.
why_it_matters: It clarifies that reported nonlinearities are not intrinsic to Loss-DiVincenzo spin qubits, simplifying control models for scalable operation.
detailed_summary: The study characterizes microwave-driven Loss-DiVincenzo single-spin qubits in a three-qubit processor under both resonant and mixed resonant/off-resonant drive conditions typical of real control schedules. With carefully calibrated microwave amplitudes, the measured Rabi frequency scales linearly with drive strength, including when all three spins are driven simultaneously. The work also quantifies heating-induced resonance shifts from off-resonant tones and finds them comparable to ordinary temporal drift levels. These measurements argue that previously observed nonlinear drive response is not a generic feature of this qubit modality.
main_result: In a three-qubit device, electrically driven spins exhibit linear Rabi scaling with microwave amplitude, while off-resonant-drive shifts remain modest and drift-like.
application_angle: This supports more predictable calibration and parallel-drive strategies for semiconductor spin-qubit processors moving toward larger arrays.

title: Gate Optimization via Efficient Two-Qubit Benchmarking for NV Centers in Diamond
abstract: High-fidelity gate implementation requires sophisticated control pulses that steer the quantum system to undergo the desired transformation. Quantum Optimal Control allows to derive these control pulses in an open-loop fashion based on numerical simulations. However, their precision can be limited by incomplete knowledge of the system. Closed-loop optimization overcomes this limitation by incorporating feedback from measurements, provided a suitable and efficient measure of the gate performance can be defined. In this article, we present an efficient method to evaluate the performance of a two-qubit gate by preparation and measurement of only two quantum states, enabling experimental closed-loop optimization with a metric previously believed to be limited to open-loop control. We tailor the approach to nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond and, through numerical simulations, demonstrate how the method can optimize a two-qubit gate while reducing the number of required measurements by two orders of magnitude compared to standard process tomography under realistic experimental settings.
one_liner: A two-state benchmarking method enables closed-loop optimization of two-qubit NV-center gates with far fewer measurements than tomography.
why_it_matters: It makes experimental feedback optimization of entangling gates much more practical in solid-state qubits with limited measurement throughput.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces an efficient performance metric for two-qubit gates that requires preparation and measurement of only two states, rather than full process reconstruction. This metric is tailored to nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond and is compatible with closed-loop optimization, where experimental feedback corrects model inaccuracies that limit open-loop optimal control. Numerical simulations under realistic conditions show the method can optimize a two-qubit gate while cutting required measurements by roughly two orders of magnitude versus standard process tomography. The result directly addresses a major bottleneck in experimental gate tuning.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a two-state gate-evaluation scheme that supports closed-loop optimization of NV two-qubit gates with about 100x fewer measurements than process tomography.
application_angle: Lower-overhead benchmarking can accelerate routine calibration of solid-state quantum processors and reduce experimental time for high-fidelity gate tuning.

title: An Accurate Vector Magnetometer via Zeeman Rabi Oscillations
abstract: Accurate magnetic field direction sensing in compact platforms is critical in applications spanning magnetic navigation, space science, and biomedical imaging. We demonstrate a single-optical-axis vector optically pumped magnetometer based on Rabi oscillations between Zeeman sublevels driven by a series of resonant radiofrequency (RF) polarization ellipses (PEs). A calibration protocol based on controlled rotations of the DC magnetic field determines the spatial orientation of each PE. We develop a detailed theoretical model describing the angular dependence of the Rabi frequencies, incorporating key systematics including RF Stark shifts and Bloch-Siegert shifts. We also account for an RF-based heading-error systematic affecting Rabi-frequency measurements arising from the nonlinear Zeeman effect. Simultaneous Larmor measurements yield the magnitude of the magnetic field, enabling integrated vector-scalar measurements. The magnetometer achieves deadzone-free vector operation with 80 $μ$rad mean angular accuracy and angular noise densities as low as 8 $μ$rad$/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$, offering a pathway towards miniaturized sensors without requiring 3D optical access or sensor rotations.
one_liner: A single-optical-axis optically pumped magnetometer achieves deadzone-free vector sensing using Zeeman Rabi oscillations and RF polarization ellipses.
why_it_matters: It delivers high-accuracy vector magnetometry without 3D optical access or sensor rotation, which is valuable for compact field-deployable instruments.
detailed_summary: The device measures magnetic-field direction from Rabi oscillations between Zeeman sublevels driven by a sequence of resonant RF polarization ellipses, while Larmor precession provides field magnitude. A calibration method based on controlled DC-field rotations determines the spatial orientation of each RF ellipse, and the model explicitly includes RF Stark shifts, Bloch-Siegert shifts, and a nonlinear-Zeeman heading-error systematic. The demonstrated performance reaches about 80 urad mean angular accuracy with angular noise densities down to 8 urad/sqrt(Hz). Importantly, the architecture avoids dead zones and needs only a single optical axis.
main_result: The magnetometer achieves deadzone-free vector-scalar field measurement with roughly 80 urad mean angular accuracy and 8 urad/sqrt(Hz) angular noise density.
application_angle: This architecture is well suited to miniaturized navigation, space, and biomedical magnetic-field sensors where compact geometry and orientation-independent operation matter.

title: Quantum-limited estimation of the difference between photonic momenta via spatially resolved two-photon interference
abstract: We present a quantum sensing protocol for three-dimensional estimation of the difference between the momenta of two photons based on spatially resolved interferometric sampling measurements. The protocol attains ultimate quantum precision in the simultaneous estimation of the components of the relative momentum for any values of the parameters already with $\sim 2000$ sampling measurements and a bias below $1\%$. These results identify 3D spatially resolved two-photon interference as an efficient tool for multi-parameter quantum sensing, with potential applications in high-precision 3D localization, refractometry, and near-field calibration in free-space quantum technologies.
one_liner: A spatially resolved Hong-Ou-Mandel-style protocol estimates 3D relative photon momentum at the quantum limit with only about 2000 samples.
why_it_matters: It turns two-photon interference into a practical multiparameter sensor for 3D localization, refractometry, and alignment tasks in free-space quantum optics.
detailed_summary: The work proposes a three-dimensional estimator for the relative momentum vector between two photons using spatially resolved interferometric coincidence measurements. The protocol reaches the quantum Cramer-Rao limit for simultaneous estimation of all three components across the full parameter range, with reported bias below 1% using roughly 2000 samples. The key technical point is that position-resolved two-photon interference retains enough information to saturate ultimate precision bounds without adaptive tuning to specific operating points.
main_result: The authors show quantum-limited simultaneous estimation of all three components of relative photonic momentum with low bias and modest measurement counts.
application_angle: This could enable precise optical alignment, 3D localization, refractive-index metrology, and calibration in free-space quantum communication links.

title: Heterogeneously Integrated Diamond-on-Lithium Niobate Quantum Photonic Platform
abstract: Diamond photonics has enabled efficient interfaces for quantum memories and is predicted to be a critical component of quantum networks. However, scalable network architectures require spatial, temporal, and spectral control of photons, which relies on nonlinear and electro-optic functionalities that diamond alone cannot provide. Here, we demonstrate heterogeneous integration of a thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) platform, which has strong chi-2 nonlinearity and electro-optic effects, with thin diamond films. We demonstrate high-Q diamond photonic crystal cavities (Q factors exceeding 5x10^4 at 735 nm) that are lithographically aligned with TFLN photonic backbone and critically coupled to it. This allows us to realize low-loss diamond-TFLN "escalators" (loss ~1 dB/coupler) that support efficient light transfer between them. At cryogenic temperatures (5K), we can collect photons emitted from silicon vacancies (SiVs) embedded within the diamond structure via the TFLN photonic circuit. This approach establishes a scalable route toward integrated photonic circuits for practical quantum networking and other technologies.
one_liner: A diamond-on-thin-film-lithium-niobate chip combines high-Q color-center cavities with low-loss electro-optic photonic routing for scalable quantum networking.
why_it_matters: It integrates quantum emitters with a mature nonlinear control platform, addressing a core bottleneck in building networkable quantum photonic hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates heterogeneous integration of thin diamond films with thin-film lithium niobate, joining emitter-friendly diamond nanophotonics with a chi-2 and electro-optic photonic backbone. It reports diamond photonic crystal cavities with Q exceeding 5x10^4 at 735 nm, lithographically aligned and critically coupled to the lithium-niobate circuitry through about 1 dB per coupler transfer structures. At 5 K, photons from embedded silicon-vacancy centers are collected through the TFLN circuit, showing that quantum emitters can be interfaced directly with a scalable control and routing layer.
main_result: The team realizes cryogenic photon collection from SiV centers through a critically coupled diamond-TFLN photonic circuit with high-Q cavities and low interlayer coupling loss.
application_angle: The architecture is directly relevant to chip-scale quantum repeaters, memories, and frequency- or phase-controlled nodes for practical quantum networks.

title: Communication-constrained nonlocal correlations
abstract: Identifying the physical grounds distinguishing quantum theory from broader probabilistic frameworks remains an open challenge. Communication-based proposals -- most notably the principles of impossibility of superluminal signaling and information causality (IC) -- highlight the role of communication in ruling out unphysical theories and offer an operational rationale on why quantum predictions prevail over these alternative models. Nevertheless, most such developments rely on communicating parts optimizing over specific tasks, such as communication complexity problems and random access codes (RAC). In this work, we systematically extend this communication-based approach. We characterize the class of communication tasks relevant for this context, and employ the general information-theoretic framework to derive new operational constraints preventing such unphysical behaviors. Remarkably, our results reveal a broad family of previously undetected implausible behaviors, independent of any particular encoding or decoding strategy, reinforcing the role of communication as a fundamental lens through which physically meaningful theories can be identified.
one_liner: A general information-theoretic framework identifies new classes of nonphysical nonlocal correlations under communication constraints.
why_it_matters: It strengthens the operational criteria used to separate quantum-allowed correlations from broader probabilistic models relevant to secure and device-independent protocols.
detailed_summary: Rather than optimizing over specific random access codes or communication-complexity games, the paper characterizes a broader class of communication tasks that can test whether nonlocal correlations are physically admissible. Within that general framework, it derives new operational constraints that exclude families of supra-quantum behaviors missed by earlier task-specific analyses. The results are theoretical, but technically important because they provide encoding- and decoding-independent tests for implausible correlations.
main_result: The authors derive new communication-based constraints that rule out previously undetected supra-quantum nonlocal behaviors without relying on specific task encodings.
application_angle: These constraints can sharpen certification methods for device-independent quantum communication and networked nonlocality experiments.

title: Electromagnetically induced transparency and population repump readout of Rydberg states of Cs atoms in a J-scheme
abstract: Rydberg atom electrometry offers traceable electric field measurements over many decades of radio frequencies in a single device. Miniaturization of these sensors is primarily limited by requirements of the lasers used. Here we demonstrate a three-photon sensing scheme using a J-shaped energy level coupling that can be achieved using external cavity diode lasers, without the need for a doubling crystal or tapered amplifier. In the low laser power regime, we demonstrate a full-width at half-maximum linewidth of 1.3 MHz. We demonstrate that for RF field electrometry using conventional heterodyne techniques, we can detect 4.7 GHz at a sensitivity of 27 μV m-1 Hz-1/2, comparable to that of two-photon detection schemes which require the use of a tapered amplifier. We also investigate a modified scheme where the probe laser is locked to a different hyperfine state, thus measuring the two-photon electromagnetically induced transparency in the other two lasers via the change in population of this separate state due to repumping. In this scheme we find the sensitivity for a 4.7 GHz field to be 39 μV m-1 Hz-1/2, and demonstrate that the amplitude scaling with probe power offers a different saturation profile than the linked J-scheme counterpart.
one_liner: A Cs Rydberg J-scheme enables RF electrometry with only diode lasers while preserving MHz linewidths and competitive sensitivity.
why_it_matters: It lowers the laser-system complexity for atomic electric-field sensors, making compact and deployable Rydberg electrometers more realistic.
detailed_summary: The authors implement a three-photon J-shaped excitation scheme for cesium Rydberg sensing that avoids components such as doubling crystals and tapered amplifiers. In the low-power regime they obtain a 1.3 MHz full-width at half-maximum linewidth, and for 4.7 GHz RF detection they report 27 microvolt per meter per root hertz sensitivity with conventional heterodyne readout. They also study a repump-based variant that reads out two-photon EIT through population transfer in another hyperfine state, reaching 39 microvolt per meter per root hertz and exhibiting a distinct probe-power saturation behavior.
main_result: The work demonstrates diode-laser-only Rydberg RF electrometry at 4.7 GHz with sensitivities competitive with more complex two-photon schemes.
application_angle: This simplifies the optical stack for traceable broadband electric-field sensors and supports miniaturized atomic RF metrology hardware.

title: Metriq: A Collaborative Platform for Benchmarking Quantum Computers
abstract: The fragmented landscape of quantum computer benchmarks, characterized by system-specific tools and inconsistent evaluation methodologies, hinders reliable cross-platform performance assessment. We introduce Metriq, an open-source collaborative platform for reproducible cross-platform quantum benchmarking that integrates benchmark definition and execution, data collection, and public presentation into a unified workflow. The Metriq benchmark suite spans both system-level metrics that characterize fundamental device properties such as entanglement quality, gate performance, and circuit speed, as well as application-inspired protocols that assess performance on quantum machine learning, optimization, and quantum simulation tasks. Benchmarks are chosen to scale with processor size, and the framework incorporates cost and resource estimation to support practical evaluation. Using Metriq, we collect and publicly release results from more than ten quantum computers across multiple hardware vendors, enabling systematic cross-platform comparison. The resulting curated dataset also reveals the practical strengths and limitations of individual benchmarks, creating a feedback loop that informs the ongoing refinement of the suite. To summarize performance across the benchmark suite, we introduce the Metriq Score, a composite index aggregating benchmark outcomes. We further present cross-benchmark analyses enabled by the shared dataset and their correlations with hardware calibration metrics. Through open development and data sharing, Metriq provides a practical foundation for reproducible benchmarking of quantum computers as hardware and benchmarking methods continue to evolve.
one_liner: Metriq is an open platform that standardizes cross-vendor quantum-computer benchmarking and aggregates results into a shared public scorecard.
why_it_matters: It addresses the lack of reproducible, apples-to-apples hardware evaluation needed for procurement, roadmap decisions, and system engineering.
detailed_summary: Metriq combines benchmark specification, execution, result collection, and public presentation in one reproducible workflow for quantum hardware evaluation. Its suite spans system metrics such as gate quality, entanglement, and circuit speed, plus application-inspired tasks in machine learning, optimization, and simulation, with scaling and resource-cost estimates built in. The authors report public results from more than ten devices across multiple vendors and introduce a composite Metriq Score together with cross-benchmark analyses linked to calibration data.
main_result: The project delivers a reproducible open benchmarking stack and dataset spanning more than ten quantum computers from multiple vendors.
application_angle: It enables more credible hardware comparison, vendor selection, and performance tracking for organizations deploying or evaluating quantum processors.

title: Exploring pathways towards quantum advantage in quantum chemistry: the case of a molecule with half-Möbius topology
abstract: We report quantum chemistry calculations performed on superconducting quantum processors for a molecule exhibiting the half-Möbius electronic topology originally introduced by Rončević et al. Using SqDRIFT, a randomized sample-based Krylov quantum diagonalization algorithm, we achieve reliable quantum simulations on active spaces corresponding to 36 orbitals (72 qubits) and extend previous studies up to 50 orbitals (100 qubits). We demonstrate that a systematic increase of active space sizes, which has a concrete impact on the accuracy of the electronic structure description, is achievable with state-of-the-art quantum processors, thus offering a promising path towards practically relevant quantum-assisted electronic-structure calculations.
one_liner: Superconducting processors run randomized Krylov quantum chemistry for a half-Mobius molecule using active spaces up to 50 orbitals and 100 qubits.
why_it_matters: It shows a concrete scaling path for useful quantum chemistry workloads on current superconducting hardware rather than only toy molecular instances.
detailed_summary: Using the SqDRIFT randomized sample-based Krylov quantum diagonalization method, the study performs electronic-structure calculations for a molecule with half-Mobius topology on superconducting quantum processors. The experiments achieve reliable simulations for active spaces of 36 orbitals, corresponding to 72 qubits, and extend prior studies to 50 orbitals or 100 qubits. A central technical claim is that increasing active-space size in a controlled way improves chemistry fidelity while remaining executable on state-of-the-art hardware.
main_result: The authors demonstrate quantum-assisted chemistry calculations on superconducting hardware for active spaces up to 50 orbitals, equivalent to 100 qubits.
application_angle: This points toward practically relevant quantum chemistry workflows where near-term processors tackle larger active spaces for materials and molecular design.

title: Scalable Postselection of Quantum Resources
abstract: The large overhead imposed by quantum error correction is a critical challenge to the realization of quantum computers, and motivates searching for alternative error correcting codes and fault-tolerant circuit constructions. Postselection is a powerful tool that builds large programs out of probabilistically generated sub-circuits, and has been shown to increase the threshold of quantum error correction based on fusing fixed-size resource states or concatenated codes. In this work, we present an approach to lower the overhead of quantum computing using scalable postselection, based on directly postselecting sub-circuits with a size extensive in the code distance using decoder soft information. We introduce a metric, the partial gap, that estimates what the logical gap of a resource state will be after it is consumed, and show that postselection based on the partial gap leads to scalable improvements in the logical error rate. In the specific context of implementing logical gates via teleportation through a cluster state, we demonstrate that scalable postselection provides a $4\times$ reduction in the overhead per logical gate, at the same logical error probability.
one_liner: Decoder-informed scalable postselection cuts logical-gate overhead by 4x while holding logical error probability fixed.
why_it_matters: It shows postselection can scale with code distance and materially reduce fault-tolerant resource costs for practical quantum computing.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces scalable postselection for large sub-circuits, using decoder soft information rather than only fixed-size resource checks. Its core metric, the partial gap, estimates the logical gap a resource state will retain after consumption, enabling acceptance decisions before full use. In a teleportation-through-cluster-state setting, the authors show this strategy improves logical error suppression in a way that scales with code distance. For the same target logical error probability, they report a 4x reduction in overhead per logical gate.
main_result: Scalable postselection based on the partial gap achieves a 4x reduction in per-gate overhead at fixed logical error probability.
application_angle: Lowering overhead in fault-tolerant logical operations directly improves the viability of large-scale quantum processors and networked resource-state architectures.

title: Approximate QCAs in one dimension using approximate algebras
abstract: Quantum cellular automata (QCAs) are automorphisms of tensor product algebras that preserve locality, with local quantum circuits as a simple example. We study approximate QCAs, where the locality condition is only satisfied up to a small error, as occurs for local quantum dynamics on the lattice. A priori, approximate QCAs could exhibit genuinely new behavior, failing to be well-approximated by any exact QCA. We show this does not occur in one dimension: every approximate QCA on a finite circle can be rounded to a strict QCA with approximately the same action on local operators, so these systems are classified by the same index as in the exact case. Previous work considered the case of the infinite line, by using global methods not amenable to finite systems. Our new approach proceeds locally and now applies to finite systems, including circles or homomorphisms from sub-intervals. We extract exact local boundary algebras from the approximate QCA restricted to local patches, then glue these to form a strict QCA. The key technical ingredient is a robust notion of the intersection of two subalgebras: when the projections onto two subalgebras approximately commute, we construct an exact subalgebra that serves as a stable proxy for their intersection. This construction uses a recent theorem of Kitaev on the rigidity of approximate $C^*$-algebras.
one_liner: Approximate 1D quantum cellular automata on finite circles can be rounded to exact QCAs with the same index structure.
why_it_matters: It removes a key ambiguity in noisy local dynamics by showing approximate 1D QCAs do not realize new phases beyond exact QCAs.
detailed_summary: This work proves that any approximate QCA in one dimension on a finite circle can be converted into a strict QCA with nearly identical action on local operators. The construction is local: exact boundary algebras are extracted from approximate local patches and then glued into a global automorphism. A central technical tool is a robust notion of subalgebra intersection when approximate projections nearly commute, leveraging rigidity results for approximate C*-algebras. The result extends classification by QCA index from exact to approximate finite 1D settings.
main_result: Every approximate 1D finite-system QCA can be rounded to a strict QCA, preserving the same classification index up to approximation.
application_angle: This gives a stable mathematical foundation for engineered local quantum dynamics, useful when implementing or certifying approximate lattice evolutions in hardware.

title: Efficient training of photonic quantum generative models
abstract: The topic of generative learning has gained traction within the field of quantum machine learning, in particular with the advent of train-on-classical, deploy-on-quantum methods. This approach exploits the properties of intermediate-complexity circuits whose training can be simulated classically efficiently, but that generally require quantum hardware for the corresponding sampling problem. Quantum linear optics possess similar properties, which allows us to propose an efficient training procedure for photon-native quantum generative models based on the maximum mean discrepancy, where the deployment of the model corresponds to the task of boson sampling. We provide numerical results, propose datasets, and we also explore how initialization strategies and ansatz choice affect the training.
one_liner: A photon-native generative model can be trained efficiently on classical hardware and deployed as a boson-sampling device.
why_it_matters: It links practical learning workflows to linear-optical hardware, making photonic quantum generative modeling more experimentally accessible.
detailed_summary: The authors develop an efficient training method for photonic quantum generative models in a train-on-classical, deploy-on-quantum regime. The objective is based on maximum mean discrepancy, while the deployed sampler corresponds to a boson-sampling task implemented with linear optics. Numerical studies examine datasets, initialization strategies, and ansatz choices to characterize trainability and model quality. The approach exploits intermediate-complexity photonic circuits that remain classically trainable but quantum-native at sampling time.
main_result: The paper presents an efficient classical training pipeline for photonic generative models whose inference stage is realized by boson-sampling hardware.
application_angle: It offers a practical route to use near-term photonic processors for learned distribution generation without requiring fully quantum training loops.

title: Large Language Model-Assisted Superconducting Qubit Experiments
abstract: Superconducting circuits have demonstrated significant potential in quantum information processing and quantum sensing. Implementing novel control and measurement sequences for superconducting qubits is often a complex and time-consuming process, requiring extensive expertise in both the underlying physics and the specific hardware and software. In this work, we introduce a framework that leverages a large language model (LLM) to automate qubit control and measurement. Specifically, our framework conducts experiments by generating and invoking schema-less tools on demand via a knowledge base on instrumental usage and experimental procedures. We showcase this framework with two experiments: an autonomous resonator characterization and a direct reproduction of a quantum non-demolition (QND) characterization of a superconducting qubit from literature. This framework enables rapid deployment of standard control-and-measurement protocols and facilitates implementation of novel experimental procedures, offering a more flexible and user-friendly paradigm for controlling complex quantum hardware.
one_liner: An LLM-driven framework autonomously runs superconducting-qubit characterization and reproduces a published QND experiment.
why_it_matters: Automating control-sequence design and instrument orchestration could significantly reduce the expertise and time needed to operate quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: This paper introduces a framework in which a large language model generates and invokes schema-less tools using a knowledge base of instrument usage and experimental procedures. The system is validated on autonomous resonator characterization and on reproducing a quantum non-demolition characterization protocol from the literature. Rather than hard-coding fixed workflows, the agent composes control and measurement actions dynamically for the target hardware stack. The result is a flexible automation layer for superconducting-qubit experiments.
main_result: The framework autonomously executed resonator characterization and reproduced a literature QND characterization on superconducting hardware.
application_angle: This can shorten experimental turnaround, lower operator burden, and accelerate development of new qubit calibration, sensing, and measurement protocols.

title: Quantum Sensing of Birefringence Beyond the Classical Limit with a Hyper-Entangled SU(1,1) Interferometer
abstract: Quantum interferometric sensing plays a crucial role in a wide range of applications, including quantum metrology, quantum imaging, and quantum lithography, where minute phase shifts carry valuable physical information. The strength of quantum sensing lies in surpassing classical sensitivity limits, particularly through the use of quantum entanglement and squeezing to suppress optical shot noise. Birefringence sensing is crucial for various applications, as it provides detailed information about the material's structure, stress, composition, and environmental conditions. We present an interferometric scheme for detecting unknown small birefringence beyond the shot-noise limit of sensitivity that leverages the hyper-entanglement within a pair of polarized nonlinear SU(1,1) interferometers, coupled by the birefringence. Specifically, two pairs of crossed-polarization nonlinear media, both generate and measure two-mode quantum light that is squeezed and polarization-entangled. We present a complete theoretical analysis of the interferometer's sensitivity to small birefringence under realistic conditions of gain and internal loss, illuminating the potential for enhancement of the sensitivity by 3-15dB in practical, real-world experiments (the exact achievable enhancement is governed solely by the loss).
one_liner: A hyper-entangled SU(1,1) interferometer is predicted to sense small birefringence 3-15 dB beyond shot-noise under realistic loss.
why_it_matters: It proposes a concrete quantum-enhanced birefringence sensor with practical gains relevant to materials analysis and precision optical metrology.
detailed_summary: The scheme uses two polarized nonlinear SU(1,1) interferometers coupled by an unknown birefringent element, generating and reading out squeezed, polarization-entangled two-mode light. The authors provide a full sensitivity analysis for small birefringence including realistic gain and internal loss. Their model predicts sub-shot-noise performance with enhancement set primarily by loss, yielding 3-15 dB improvement in practical regimes. The architecture is tailored to birefringence estimation rather than generic phase sensing.
main_result: The proposed interferometer achieves predicted birefringence sensitivity 3-15 dB beyond the classical shot-noise limit under realistic loss assumptions.
application_angle: Quantum-enhanced birefringence readout is directly relevant to stress mapping, material characterization, and precision optical diagnostics.

title: Experimental demonstration of optimal measurement for unambiguously discriminating asymmetric qudit states
abstract: Identification of nonorthogonal quantum states without error is crucial for various applications in quantum information technology, as well as the foundations of quantum physics. Theoretical studies have proposed measurements that maximize the success probability of unambiguously discriminating quantum states. However, these methods are not always experimentally feasible, which has led most demonstrations to focus on equiprobable symmetric states. Here, we establish a projective measurement scheme that optimally discriminates multiple asymmetric qudit states. We experimentally demonstrate this optimal projective measurement using a photonic orbital angular momentum state, where asymmetric qudit states are encoded in the Laguerre-Gaussian modes of a heralded single-photon state. Our results have broad applications in high-dimensional quantum state-based quantum information processing, including quantum key distribution and quantum sensing.
one_liner: Heralded single photons carrying OAM qudits realize an optimal projective measurement for unambiguous discrimination of asymmetric states.
why_it_matters: It expands experimentally feasible high-dimensional state discrimination beyond symmetric, equiprobable cases important for realistic quantum protocols.
detailed_summary: The work establishes a projective measurement scheme that optimally performs unambiguous discrimination for multiple asymmetric qudit states, a regime usually avoided experimentally. The demonstration uses heralded single photons with information encoded in Laguerre-Gaussian orbital-angular-momentum modes. By implementing the optimal projective measurement, the experiment shows that nonorthogonal high-dimensional asymmetric states can be identified without error when conclusive outcomes occur. This goes beyond the usual focus on symmetric, equiprobable state sets.
platform_or_system: photonic OAM qudits in heralded single photons
main_result: The experiment demonstrates optimal unambiguous discrimination of asymmetric qudit states using projective measurements on photonic OAM modes.
application_angle: Better high-dimensional state discrimination can improve quantum key distribution, photonic information processing, and sensing protocols using nonorthogonal state sets.

title: Genuinely entangled subspaces and strongly nonlocal unextendible biseparable bases in four-partite systems
abstract: A set of orthogonal pure states is an unextendible biseparable basis (UBB), which means that its complementary subspace contains only genuinely entangled states. UBBs thus serve as an effective tool for constructing genuinely entangled subspaces. If every state within such a subspace exhibits distillable entanglement across all bipartitions, it becomes particularly advantageous for applications in quantum information. In this paper, we mainly conduct research on the 4-qudit quantum systems, where the local dimension $d$ is not less than 3. We present an approach for constructing UBB and prove that the UBB established in this way is strongly nonlocal. We build several genuinely entangled subspaces and demonstrate the distillability of the genuinely entangled subspaces across all bipartitions. In addition, we also describe the specific orthonormal basis for some genuinely entangled subspaces. These results will not only contribute to the development of quantum nonlocality theory, but also provide a crucial theoretical foundation for practical quantum information processing tasks.
one_liner: Constructs strongly nonlocal unextendible biseparable bases in four-partite qudit systems, yielding genuinely entangled subspaces distillable across every bipartition.
why_it_matters: It gives a systematic route to multipartite entanglement resources with certified nonlocal structure and broad utility for quantum networking and information protocols.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a construction of unextendible biseparable bases in four-partite qudit systems with local dimension d >= 3 and proves that the resulting bases are strongly nonlocal. The complementary subspaces are shown to be genuinely entangled subspaces, and the authors establish distillability across all bipartitions, strengthening their usefulness as entanglement resources. They also provide explicit orthonormal bases for some of these subspaces, making the construction concrete rather than purely existential.
main_result: A family of strongly nonlocal UBBs is constructed in four-partite qudit systems, generating genuinely entangled subspaces that are distillable across every bipartition.
application_angle: Such explicitly engineered multipartite entangled subspaces can inform robust resource-state design for quantum communication, secret sharing, and distributed quantum processing.

title: Recent advances in Ultralong-range Rydberg molecules
abstract: Rydberg molecule, formed by one or more Rydberg atoms, exhibits remarkable properties, including an exceptionally large spatial extent, rich rovibrational level structures, permanent electric dipole moments, and a pronounced sensitivity to external fields. Based on the underlying binding mechanisms, Rydberg molecules can be divided into three categories, the ground-Rydberg molecule that is bound via a low-energy electron-atom scattering interaction between ground atom and Rydberg electron, the Rydberg-Rydberg molecule that is bound via a long-range electrostatic interaction between Rydberg atoms, and the ion-Rydberg molecule that is bound via single- or multi-polar interactions between Rydberg atom and ion. This review focuses on recent theoretical and experimental advances in diatomic Rydberg molecules, covering their formation and binding mechanisms, potential energy curves, experimental observations, and spectroscopic properties, with the aim of providing a comprehensive overview of the current state and future prospects of this rapidly developing field.
one_liner: A review of recent theory and experiments on ultralong-range Rydberg molecules, covering binding mechanisms, spectroscopy, and prospects for quantum control.
why_it_matters: Rydberg molecules offer extreme size, large dipoles, and field sensitivity, making them promising ingredients for quantum simulation, sensing, and neutral-atom hardware.
detailed_summary: This review surveys three main classes of diatomic Rydberg molecules: ground-Rydberg, Rydberg-Rydberg, and ion-Rydberg complexes. It summarizes the relevant binding mechanisms, potential-energy curves, rovibrational structure, spectroscopic signatures, and recent experimental observations. By organizing both theoretical and experimental progress, it clarifies how these exotic molecular states can be prepared, characterized, and exploited in emerging quantum platforms.
main_result: The article consolidates recent advances in the formation, spectroscopy, and control of diatomic ultralong-range Rydberg molecules across multiple binding regimes.
application_angle: The review maps how highly polarizable, field-sensitive Rydberg molecules could support quantum sensing, tunable interactions, and new modalities in neutral-atom quantum hardware.

title: Reconfigurable Superconducting Quantum Circuits Enabled by Micro-Scale Liquid-Metal Interconnects
abstract: Modular architectures are a promising route toward scalable superconducting quantum processors, but finite fabrication yield and the lack of high quality temporary interconnects impose fundamental limitations on system size. Here, we demonstrate chip-scale liquid-metal interconnects that show promise for plug-and-play superconducting quantum circuits by enabling non-destructive module replacement while maintaining high microwave performance. Using gallium-based liquid metals, we realize high-quality inter-module signal and ground interconnects, comparable in performance to conventional coplanar waveguide resonators. We illustrate consistent device characteristics across three thermal cycles between room temperature and 15 mK, as well as the ability to reform superconducting connections following module replacement. A width-dependent resonance frequency shift reveals a significant kinetic inductance fraction, which we attribute to the presence of $β$-phase tantalum as confirmed by X-ray characterization. Finally, we investigate power-dependent loss mechanisms and observe high-power dissipative nonlinearities qualitatively consistent with a readout-power heating model. These results establish liquid metals as viable chip-scale interconnects for reconfigurable, modular superconducting quantum systems.
one_liner: Demonstrates microscale liquid-metal interconnects that preserve high microwave performance while enabling replaceable modular superconducting quantum circuits.
why_it_matters: Temporary low-loss interconnects address yield and serviceability bottlenecks in scaling superconducting processors toward modular, maintainable architectures.
detailed_summary: The authors implement gallium-based chip-scale liquid-metal signal and ground interconnects for superconducting modules and benchmark them against conventional coplanar-waveguide resonators. The devices retain consistent characteristics over three thermal cycles between room temperature and 15 mK and can reform superconducting connections after module replacement. They also identify a width-dependent resonance shift linked to significant kinetic inductance, attributed to beta-phase tantalum, and study power-dependent loss showing high-power dissipative nonlinearities consistent with readout heating.
platform_or_system: modular superconducting microwave circuits with liquid-metal links
main_result: Microscale gallium-based liquid-metal interconnects can provide reconfigurable, non-destructive module connections with microwave performance comparable to standard superconducting resonators.
application_angle: This directly supports plug-and-play maintenance, higher effective fabrication yield, and scalable modular packaging for superconducting quantum processors.

title: Metrology for Quantum Hardware Standardization -- Charting a Pathway: A Strategic Review
abstract: Advances in quantum mechanics have long underpinned metrology by enabling practical realizations of units through quantum effects. With the 2019 SI revision, traceability is anchored in defined fundamental constants, reinforcing the quantum-mechanical basis of modern standards. In parallel, quantum technologies are transitioning from laboratory science to engineering and early industrial deployment, bringing familiar pressures for integration, reliability, cost reduction, supply-chain formation, and standardization. The direction of benefit is thus reversing: metrology and precision measurement are becoming enabling infrastructure for the industrialization of quantum technologies. Against this backdrop, this paper surveys the metrology and precision-measurement capabilities required across representative quantum-computing modalities and identifies where electrical and related metrology can contribute to the development, characterization, and reliable operation of quantum hardware. We then discuss cross-cutting measurement needs and standardization opportunities that recur across platforms, and note how similar frameworks can extend to emerging quantum-sensing applications.
one_liner: A strategic review outlining the metrology, characterization, and standards needed to industrialize quantum hardware across computing modalities.
why_it_matters: Reliable measurement frameworks and traceability are prerequisites for manufacturable, interoperable, and certifiable quantum technologies.
detailed_summary: The paper reviews how quantum-era metrology underpins the transition of quantum technologies from laboratory prototypes to engineered systems. It identifies platform-specific and cross-cutting measurement needs for characterization, control, reliability, and supply-chain development across representative quantum-computing modalities. The discussion emphasizes where electrical and related metrology can enable benchmarking, interoperability, and standards development, with extensions to quantum sensing.
main_result: The review defines a roadmap for measurement capabilities and standardization practices needed to support dependable, industrial-scale quantum hardware.
application_angle: It is practically relevant because standards and traceable measurements are essential for device qualification, vendor comparison, and reproducible deployment of quantum systems.

title: Enhancing light-matter coupling for exploring chaos in the quantum Rabi model
abstract: Accessing chaos in the quantum Rabi model (QRM) usually requires operating far from resonance, combined with ultra- or deep-strong light-matter coupling. This makes direct experiments challenging. In this manuscript, we propose a solution to this challenge by employing an anti-squeezing transformation to the bosonic field. Specifically, we demonstrate that this transformation maps a weakly coupled, two-photon driven Jaynes-Cummings model (JCM) to an effective deep-strong-coupling QRM in the squeezed-light frame. Using out-of-time-order correlator, Husimi distribution, and linear entanglement entropy, we numerically probe chaos in this coupling-enhanced platform and observe the similar chaotic phenomena as in the ideal QRM. We also find the coupling-enhanced model can drive the system deeper into the chaotic regime. This establishes coupling-enhanced method as a practical approach to study QRM chaos without requiring intrinsic ultra-strong coupling.
one_liner: Shows that anti-squeezing can map a weakly coupled driven Jaynes-Cummings system into an effective deep-strong-coupling quantum Rabi model for chaos studies.
why_it_matters: It offers an experimentally accessible route to probe quantum Rabi chaos without requiring intrinsically ultra-strong light-matter coupling hardware.
detailed_summary: The work applies an anti-squeezing transformation to the bosonic mode and demonstrates that a weakly coupled, two-photon driven Jaynes-Cummings model maps to an effective deep-strong-coupling quantum Rabi model in the squeezed frame. The authors characterize chaotic dynamics using out-of-time-order correlators, Husimi distributions, and linear entanglement entropy. Numerics show chaos closely matching the ideal QRM and indicate that the coupling-enhanced scheme can reach deeper chaotic regimes than the native weak-coupling system.
platform_or_system: driven cavity-QED Jaynes-Cummings platform with squeezed bosonic mode
main_result: A squeezing-based transformation enables effective deep-strong-coupling QRM dynamics and reproduces hallmark signatures of quantum chaos in a weak-coupling photonic platform.
application_angle: The approach lowers hardware demands for exploring nonintegrable light-matter dynamics, making chaos-based studies more feasible in existing cavity and circuit QED experiments.

title: Meta-cavity Quantum Electrodynamics
abstract: Cavity quantum electrodynamics (cQED) harnesses light-matter interactions to produce nonclassical light states. However, a fundamental challenge lies in simultaneously achieving Purcell enhancement and tailored wavefront control within a single cavity, due to conflicting resonator requirements. Here, we overcome this limitation by demonstrating triggered single-photon emission with customizable wavefronts from semiconductor quantum dots embedded in geometric-phase metacavities. These monolithic devices - only 200 nm thick - deliver Purcell-enhanced emission alongside spin-momentum-locked radiation, vortex beams, and holographic patterns. The meta-atom lattice provides high-Q optical confinement, while spatially modulated orientations enable efficient outcoupling of photons with designed states. This work establishes a new paradigm for intrinsically multiplexing metasurface-based wavefront shaping with cQED, enabling high-performance quantum light sources from subwavelength-scale monolithic platforms.
one_liner: Introduces metacavity QED devices that combine Purcell-enhanced single-photon emission with programmable wavefronts such as vortex and holographic beams.
why_it_matters: It merges quantum-light generation and wavefront engineering in one monolithic platform, reducing complexity for deployable photonic quantum sources.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates semiconductor quantum dots embedded in geometric-phase metacavities only 200 nm thick, achieving triggered single-photon emission with tailored output states. The devices simultaneously provide high-Q confinement for Purcell enhancement and metasurface-enabled outcoupling for spin-momentum locking, vortex beams, and holographic emission patterns. This resolves the usual tradeoff between strong cavity enhancement and wavefront control by integrating both functions into a single monolithic nanophotonic structure.
main_result: Monolithic 200 nm-thick metacavities produce Purcell-enhanced triggered single photons with customizable wavefronts including spin-locked, vortex, and holographic modes.
application_angle: Such compact sources are directly relevant to integrated quantum photonics, where brightness, mode control, and on-chip manufacturability are critical for communications and scalable optical systems.

title: Efficient Qubit Simulation of Hybrid Oscillator-Qubit Quantum Computation
abstract: We introduce a framework for simulating hybrid oscillator-qubit quantum processors on qubit-only systems through position encoding. By encoding continuous-variable position and momentum wave functions into qubit amplitudes, our method efficiently simulates all Gaussian and conditional Gaussian operations -- encompassing the phase-space instruction set (beam splitter, single-qubit rotation, conditional displacement) and extending to squeezing, conditional squeezing, conditional rotation, and conditional beam splitter -- using $O\!\left(\log^2\!\left(Γ+ \log(1/ε)\right)\right)$ qubit gates per hybrid gate, where $Γ$ is the Fock-level bound and $ε$ is the target precision. This polylogarithmic per-gate complexity represents an exponential improvement over Fock basis encoding approaches, which require exponential quantum or classical resources in the number of qubits per mode. We provide rigorous numerical characterization of quantum Fourier transform errors for Fock-bounded states, enabling precise resource estimation for practical implementations. This work establishes that hybrid oscillator-qubit algorithms can be implemented on qubit processors with polynomial overhead, providing new insights into the computational power trade-offs between discrete-variable and hybrid continuous-discrete-variable quantum computing.
one_liner: A position-encoding method simulates hybrid oscillator-qubit gates on qubit-only hardware with polylogarithmic gate cost.
why_it_matters: It suggests CV-hybrid algorithms can run on standard qubit processors without the exponential overhead typical of Fock-space encodings.
detailed_summary: The paper maps continuous-variable position and momentum wavefunctions into qubit amplitudes, enabling qubit-only simulation of hybrid oscillator-qubit processors. It covers Gaussian and conditional Gaussian primitives including beam splitters, squeezing, conditional squeezing, conditional rotation, and conditional beam splitter operations. The claimed per-gate complexity scales as O(log^2(Gamma + log(1/epsilon))), where Gamma is the Fock cutoff and epsilon the target precision, which is exponentially better than standard Fock-basis encodings in the number of qubits per mode. The authors also quantify quantum Fourier transform errors for Fock-bounded states to support resource estimates.
main_result: The work shows that a broad hybrid CV-qubit gate set can be simulated on qubit-only hardware with polylogarithmic overhead per hybrid gate.
application_angle: This lowers the barrier to testing photonic or bosonic-hybrid algorithms on existing qubit platforms before dedicated hybrid hardware is available.

title: Fictitious Copy Quantum Error Mitigation
abstract: Errors are arguably the most pressing challenge impeding practical applications of quantum computers, which has instigated vigorous research on the development of quantum error mitigation (QEM) techniques. Existing QEM methods suppress errors with a varying degree of efficacy but importantly demand significant additional quantum and classical computational resources. In this work, we present Fictitious Copy Quantum Error Mitigation (FCQEM) method which corrects quantum errors without requiring any additional quantum resources and purely relies on using classical postprocessing of a joint probability distribution to correct expectation values. The joint probability distribution can be measured ``fictitiously'' by sampling one copy of noisy quantum circuit twice, or classically squaring probabilities from simply one copy. We show that FCQEM can recover eigenvalues even if exact eigenstates are not prepared. Furthermore, our technique can benefit other noise mitigation techniques with no additional quantum resources, which is demonstrated by combining FCQEM with the Quantum Computed Moments (QCM) method. FCQEM can compensate for noise that is pathological to QCM, and QCM allows for FCQEM to recover the ground state energy with a larger variety of trial states. We show that our technique can find the exact ground state energy of molecular and spin models under simulated noise models as well as experiments on a Rigetti 84-qubit superconducting quantum processor. The reported FCQEM method is general purpose for the current generation of quantum devices and is applicable to any problem that measures eigenvalues of operators on sharply peaked distributions.
one_liner: FCQEM mitigates quantum errors through classical post-processing alone and is demonstrated on simulations and a Rigetti 84-qubit device.
why_it_matters: A no-extra-quantum-resource mitigation method is attractive for near-term hardware where circuit depth, shots, and ancillary qubits are tightly constrained.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce Fictitious Copy Quantum Error Mitigation, which estimates corrected expectation values from a joint probability distribution built by resampling or squaring single-copy measurement statistics.
