The method can recover eigenvalues even when the prepared state is not an exact eigenstate, and it can be combined with Quantum Computed Moments to improve robustness against noise types that degrade QCM alone. They validate the approach on molecular and spin-model problems under simulated noise and on a Rigetti 84-qubit superconducting processor. The central advantage is that the protocol adds no quantum hardware overhead, shifting the burden entirely to classical post-processing.
main_result: FCQEM recovers exact or strongly improved eigenvalue estimates without additional quantum resources, including experimental validation on a large superconducting processor.
application_angle: The technique can extend the useful accuracy window of current quantum processors for chemistry, materials, and eigenvalue estimation workloads.

title: Verified delegated quantum computation requires techniques beyond cut-and-choose
abstract: Delegated quantum computation enables a client with limited quantum capabilities to outsource computations to a more powerful quantum server while preserving correctness and privacy. Verification is crucial in this setting to ensure that the untrusted quantum server performs the computation honestly and returns correct results. A common verification method is the quantum cut-and-choose technique. Inspired by classical verification methods for two-party computation, the client uses the majority of the delegated rounds to test the server's honesty, while keeping the remaining ones for the actual computation. Combining this technique with other methods, such as quantum error correction, could help achieve negligible cheating probabilities for the server; however, such methods can impose significant overheads making implementations unfeasible for the near-term future. In this work, we investigate whether cut-and-choose can yield efficient and secure verifiable quantum computation without additional costly techniques. We find that verifiable delegated quantum computation protocols relying solely on cut-and-choose techniques cannot be secure and efficient at the same time.
one_liner: This work proves cut-and-choose alone cannot make delegated quantum computation both efficient and securely verifiable.
why_it_matters: It sharply narrows the design space for practical verified quantum cloud protocols and indicates extra mechanisms are unavoidable.
detailed_summary: The paper studies verifiable delegated quantum computation where a limited client outsources work to an untrusted quantum server. It analyzes whether repeating delegated rounds and using most of them as tests, in the spirit of quantum cut-and-choose, can by itself yield both low overhead and strong soundness. The authors show that protocols relying only on cut-and-choose cannot simultaneously achieve efficiency and security, implying that additional ingredients such as error correction or other verification structures are necessary. This is a complexity and protocol-design limitation result rather than a hardware demonstration.
main_result: The main result is an impossibility-style conclusion that cut-and-choose-only verification cannot be both efficient and secure for delegated quantum computation.
application_angle: For quantum cloud services, the result guides architects toward stronger verification stacks instead of lightweight cut-and-choose-only schemes that will not scale securely.

title: Chip-Integrated Broadband Multi-Photon Source for Wavelength-Multiplexed Quantum Networks
abstract: Quantum networks based on wavelength-multiplexed entanglement enable parallel distribution of quantum correlations, increasing channel capacity for secure communication and distributed quantum information processing. However, broadband integrated sources capable of generating multipartite entanglement beyond photon pairs remain scarce. Here we report on-chip generation of telecom-band four-photon entanglement in a periodically poled thin-film lithium niobate on insulator (LNOI) waveguide. Type-0 spontaneous parametric down-conversion provides a phase-matching bandwidth exceeding 200 nm, enabling spectrally separable generation of multi-photon entanglement across the telecom band. The generated photons are encoded in time bins for robust fiber compatibility, and a coherent interface enabling reversible conversion between time-bin and polarization degrees of freedom allows complete quantum state tomography. We measure two-photon entanglement with a brightness of 6.7 MHz/mW/nm and a fidelity of $0.874 \pm 0.002$. At a pump power of 0.08 mW, the four-photon state exhibits a fourfold coincidence rate of 1 Hz and a fidelity of $0.74 \pm 0.01$, representing a threefold improvement over previous integrated platforms. Our results establish LNOI as a scalable platform for broadband multi-photon entanglement and provide a practical route toward dense wavelength-multiplexed quantum networks.
one_liner: An LNOI chip generates broadband telecom-band four-photon entanglement suitable for wavelength-multiplexed quantum networking.
why_it_matters: Integrated multi-photon sources with broad bandwidth and telecom compatibility are a key missing component for scalable dense quantum network links.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate on-chip telecom-band four-photon entanglement in periodically poled thin-film lithium niobate on insulator waveguides using type-0 SPDC with more than 200 nm phase-matching bandwidth. The photons are encoded in time bins for fiber robustness, and a coherent time-bin/polarization interface enables full state tomography. They report two-photon entanglement brightness of 6.7 MHz/mW/nm with fidelity 0.874 plus a four-photon state fidelity of 0.74 at a fourfold coincidence rate of 1 Hz using 0.08 mW pump power. The four-photon performance is described as a threefold improvement over prior integrated platforms.
main_result: The chip produces broadband, telecom-compatible four-photon entanglement on an integrated platform with improved fidelity-rate performance over earlier integrated sources.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to dense wavelength-division quantum networks that need parallel entanglement distribution from compact chip-scale sources.

title: Enhanced security in Quantum Token protocols using Hybrid Spin-Photon Interfaces
abstract: Quantum token protocols enable unforgeable quantum tokens promising unconditional security beyond classical cryptographic assumptions. We show here that the three stages of the Quantum token protocols involving the preparation, storage and verification can be made more secure when involving spin-photon interfaces that leverage high fidelity hybrid tripartite (spin-photon-spin) entanglement, Bell state measurements and highly coherent spin quantum memories. Further we describe the physical implementation of various stages of the protocol using the hybrid electron and nuclear spins in diamond interfaced with time-bin photons.
one_liner: Hybrid spin-photon interfaces are proposed to harden quantum token protocols through high-fidelity entanglement, Bell measurements, and long-lived memories.
why_it_matters: It connects a cryptographic primitive to a realistic solid-state photonic platform with better security prospects for preparation, storage, and verification.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes how quantum token protocols can be strengthened by using hybrid spin-photon interfaces across token preparation, storage, and verification stages. The proposed implementation relies on tripartite spin-photon-spin entanglement, Bell-state measurements, and coherent spin memories. As a concrete platform, it focuses on hybrid electron and nuclear spins in diamond coupled to time-bin photonic qubits. The contribution is primarily an architecture and protocol-security proposal rather than an experimental benchmark study.
platform_or_system: diamond spin-photon interface with time-bin photons
main_result: The work proposes that diamond-based hybrid spin-photon hardware can enhance the security of quantum token protocols across all protocol stages.
application_angle: If realized, it would support physically grounded unforgeable quantum credential systems with integrated storage and photonic verification channels.

title: Sensing Low-Frequency Field with Rydberg Atoms via Quantum Weak Measurement
abstract: Recently, Rydberg atom has emerged as an attractive choice to realize quantum sensing of low-frequency electric field. The progress so far has mostly utilized the intensity and phase changes in probe laser and the corresponding detection mechanism still remains classical. Nevertheless, external field acting on the Rydberg state can induce the polarization variation of probe laser in the Rydberg electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) system embedded in realistic multi-state atoms. We experimentally observe this phenomenon and realize signal extraction by appropriately utilizing the polarization degrees of freedom. Based on such a mechanism, we further design and implement a quantum weak measurement scheme, which clearly suppresses the technical noise and leads to considerable improvement of performance. Evaluation of the sensitivities across different post-selection angles demonstrates that the weak measurement results agree well with the theoretical model predictions. The advantages of our method are analyzed from multiple aspects, including characterizing the responses over different frequencies and comparing the responses of the weak measurement scheme and the traditional transmission-based method. After accounting for the screening effect of a measured ratio 17\% where the $^\text{87}$Rb atoms experience a substantially reduced field inside the glass cell, the performance reaches 33 $μ\text{V}~\text{cm}^\text{-1}~\text{Hz}^\text{-1/2}$ in sensitivity and 1.0 $μ\text{V/cm}$ in minimal detectable field for an integration time of 1000 s, as perceived by the atoms.
one_liner: Rydberg-EIT weak measurement improves low-frequency electric-field sensing by exploiting probe polarization rather than only transmission.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical path to better atom-based RF and LF electrometry by suppressing technical noise with a quantum-enhanced readout layer.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a realistic multilevel Rydberg EIT system in 87Rb to sense low-frequency electric fields through field-induced probe polarization changes. The authors implement a weak-measurement readout based on polarization post-selection and compare it against conventional transmission-based detection across frequencies and post-selection angles. After correcting for a measured 17% screening factor inside the vapor cell, they report a sensitivity of 33 microvolts per centimeter per root hertz and a minimum detectable field of 1.0 microvolt per centimeter for 1000 s integration. The data agree well with the theoretical weak-measurement model and indicate reduced technical noise.
main_result: The main result is a low-frequency electric-field sensor based on Rydberg atoms and weak measurement that achieves 33 microvolts/cm/Hz^1/2 sensitivity and 1.0 microvolt/cm detectability.
application_angle: This can improve compact atom-based electrometry for low-frequency field detection in metrology, electronics diagnostics, and electromagnetic environment monitoring.

title: Quantum optical impurity models in interacting waveguide QED
abstract: We consider a generic model for interacting waveguide QED systems, where photons in a coupled-cavity array localize around atomic impurities while simultaneously interacting through local Kerr nonlinearities. This scenario appears naturally in nanophotonic crystals, circuit QED lattices, and ultracold atomic systems and is governed by the competition between attractive Jaynes-Cummings-mediated binding and intrinsic photon-photon repulsion. We analyze how this interplay affects the formation of localized few-photon bound states and determine the resulting many-body ground states for large periodic arrays of impurities and different filling factors. With the help of large-scale numerical simulations and approximate analytical models, we identify a rich phase diagram featuring Mott-like insulating states as well as superfluid phases with long-range correlations, which are mediated by an unbound, but strongly interacting photonic fluid.
one_liner: Interacting waveguide QED with atomic impurities yields localized few-photon states and many-body phases from bound and unbound photonic modes.
why_it_matters: It connects realistic nanophotonic and circuit-QED platforms to controllable strongly correlated photon phases relevant for quantum simulation and nonlinear photonics.
detailed_summary: The paper studies photons in a coupled-cavity waveguide interacting with atomic impurities and local Kerr nonlinearities, where Jaynes-Cummings attraction competes with photon-photon repulsion. Using large-scale numerics plus approximate analytics, it maps how few-photon localized bound states form and evolve in periodic impurity arrays at different fillings. The resulting phase diagram includes Mott-like insulating regimes and superfluid phases with long-range correlations mediated by an unbound but strongly interacting photonic fluid.
main_result: It identifies the conditions under which impurity-bound photons and extended interacting photonic fluids produce distinct many-body insulating and superfluid phases.
application_angle: The model is directly relevant to nanophotonic crystals, circuit-QED lattices, and cold-atom photonic simulators aimed at engineered quantum many-body light.

title: System-bath model for quantum chemistry
abstract: We propose an approximate mapping of a molecular Hamiltonian to a Hamiltonian of qubits, which allows for high accuracy quantum chemistry calculations of vertical excitation energies of some molecules. The mapping is based on separating of a very small active space of only two orbitals and on modeling the electronic excitations in the remaining orbitals by a set of qubits or, equivalently, by a set of oscillators. This approach is inspired by the Random Phase Approximation (RPA), in which the excitations of electron gas are described by bosonic degrees of freedom. As a result, the Hamiltonian of the molecule is reduced to that of a system-bath model. The "system" part of the Hamiltonian describes the two molecular orbitals -- the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) -- which are populated by two electrons. Two qubits are sufficient to encode the Hamiltonian of such a system. The "bath" consists of oscillators or, equivalently, of two level systems with each of them corresponding to an electron excitation from a doubly occupied orbital below the Fermi level to an empty orbital above the Fermi level. We hope that this mapping can inspire new approaches and algorithms aimed at calculating excitation energies of molecules on near term quantum computers.
one_liner: A HOMO-LUMO system-bath mapping compresses molecular excitation problems into two active qubits coupled to effective bath modes.
why_it_matters: The reduction targets excitation-energy estimation on near-term quantum processors by shrinking chemistry problems while retaining key environmental excitations.
detailed_summary: The authors propose an approximate molecular Hamiltonian mapping where only the HOMO-LUMO active space is treated explicitly, requiring two qubits for two electrons in two orbitals. Excitations involving the remaining orbitals are represented as oscillator or qubit bath modes inspired by random-phase-approximation physics. This turns the electronic-structure problem for vertical excitation energies into a compact system-bath model suited to near-term quantum algorithms.
main_result: It demonstrates an approximate qubit-and-bath representation that can capture molecular vertical excitation energies with a minimal active-space core.
application_angle: The approach could lower hardware and circuit requirements for useful excited-state chemistry calculations on early fault-limited quantum devices.

title: Evolution of Photonic Quantum Machine Learning under Noise
authors: A. M. A. S. D. Alagiyawanna
abstract: Photonic Quantum Machine Learning (PQML) is an emerging approach that integrates photonic quantum computing technologies with machine learning techniques to enable scalable and energy-efficient quantum information processing. Photonic systems offer advantages such as room-temperature operation, high-speed signal processing, and the ability to represent information in high-dimensional Hilbert spaces. However, noise remains a major challenge affecting the performance, reliability, and scalability of PQML implementations. This review provides a systematic analysis of noise sources in photonic quantum machine learning systems. We discuss photonic quantum computing architectures and examine key quantum machine learning algorithms implemented on photonic platforms, including Variational Quantum Circuits, Quantum Neural Networks, and Quantum Support Vector Machines. The paper categorizes major noise mechanisms and analyzes their impact on learning performance, training stability, and convergence behavior. Furthermore, we review both traditional and advanced noise characterization techniques and survey recent strategies for noise mitigation in photonic quantum systems. Finally, we highlight recent experimental advances and discuss future research directions for developing robust and scalable PQML systems under realistic noise conditions.
one_liner: This review maps how noise sources shape performance, trainability, and mitigation strategies in photonic quantum machine learning.
why_it_matters: Robust PQML needs hardware-aware noise models and mitigation methods before photonic processors can support reliable learning tasks at scale.
detailed_summary: The paper surveys photonic quantum computing architectures used for quantum machine learning and analyzes how realistic noise affects algorithms such as variational circuits, quantum neural networks, and quantum support vector machines. It classifies key noise mechanisms and links them to degraded accuracy, unstable training, and altered convergence. The review also covers characterization methods, mitigation strategies, and recent experiments, framing a hardware-conscious roadmap for scalable PQML.
main_result: It synthesizes the main noise channels, diagnostics, and mitigation approaches that govern practical photonic quantum machine-learning performance.
application_angle: The analysis is useful for designing photonic ML systems that remain trainable and reliable under realistic device imperfections and deployment constraints.

title: Narrowband heralded single photons via Bragg grating inscription in germanium-doped photonic crystal fiber
abstract: We present a fiber-based source of narrowband heralded single photons in the telecoms C-band. Photon pairs were generated by spontaneous four-wave mixing in photonic crystal fiber (PCF) with a germanium-doped region incorporated into its core for enhanced photosensitivity. A fiber Bragg grating (FBG) with a bandwidth of 0.2 nm and contrast of 17.5 dB was UV-written into the PCF to reflect a sub-nanometre slice of the photon-pair spectrum. This allowed narrowband photons to be heralded at the proximal end of the fiber by detection events after the distal end. We present photon counting data with a coincidence-to-accidental ratio of up to 70. Our source demonstrates a viable route to fiber-integrated narrowband heralded single photon sources suitable for coupling to quantum memories and interfacing heterogeneous qubit types.
one_liner: A UV-written Bragg grating inside Ge-doped photonic crystal fiber produces narrowband telecom heralded single photons with high coincidence quality.
why_it_matters: Fiber-integrated narrowband heralded photons are valuable for linking telecom networks to quantum memories and heterogeneous quantum nodes.
detailed_summary: Photon pairs are generated by spontaneous four-wave mixing in a germanium-doped photonic crystal fiber whose enhanced photosensitivity enables direct UV inscription of a fiber Bragg grating. The grating reflects a 0.2 nm spectral slice with 17.5 dB contrast, allowing narrowband photons to be heralded at the proximal fiber end. The experiment reports coincidence-to-accidental ratios up to 70, demonstrating a fully fiber-based source in the telecom C-band.
main_result: The work demonstrates a fiber-integrated narrowband telecom heralded single-photon source using an inscribed Bragg grating for in-fiber spectral filtering.
application_angle: This architecture is well suited for deployable quantum-network links, especially where narrowband telecom photons must interface efficiently with quantum memories.

title: Temporal limitations and digital data processing in continuous variable measurements of non-Gaussian states
abstract: Non-Gaussian quantum states and operations are essential tools for multiple quantum information protocols exploiting light as information career. In this context, a key role is played by schemes operating with continuous wave light, in which non-Gaussian states are obtained by photon subtraction/addition and eventually reconstructed by quantum state tomography. In these configurations, the temporal resolution of the homodyne detection and the digital data processing critically affect our ability to faithfully reconstruct the produced non-Gaussian states. In this work, we apply digital data processing to experimental data to study how the temporal performances of the detection chain affect the acquisition and treatment of tomographic data. This allows understanding how these features impact the quality of quantum states observed by non-ideal detection chains. By doing so, we discuss the actual constraints on the acquisition and reconstruction of non-Gaussian states by taking into account the limitations of realistic experimental resources.
one_liner: Digital post-processing and detector timing limits are quantified for continuous-variable tomography of non-Gaussian optical states.
why_it_matters: Accurate reconstruction of photon-subtracted or added states depends on realistic acquisition-chain limits, which directly impacts CV quantum information experiments.
detailed_summary: The study analyzes how finite temporal resolution in homodyne detection and subsequent digital processing affect tomography of non-Gaussian states produced in continuous-wave optical setups. Using experimental data, it evaluates how non-ideal acquisition chains distort the observed state and the reconstructed statistics. The work translates hardware timing and signal-processing limitations into concrete constraints on faithful reconstruction of photon-subtracted or photon-added states.
main_result: It establishes how detector bandwidth and digital processing jointly limit the acquisition and reconstruction fidelity of non-Gaussian continuous-variable states.
application_angle: The results provide practical design rules for experimental CV platforms that need reliable non-Gaussian state verification for communication and information-processing protocols.

title: Resolving Transient Electron-Phonon Coupling with Time-Resolved Spontaneous Raman Spectroscopy
abstract: Understanding the interaction of charge carriers with lattice vibrations in the quasi-equilibrium regime is crucial for semiconductor functionality. However, the structural signatures of these interactions are often too subtle for conventional ultrafast techniques to detect. We developed a time-resolved spontaneous Raman technique based on time-correlated single-photon counting to track the spectral response following photoexcitation, providing sub-wavenumber spectral resolution and a few-hundred-picosecond temporal resolution. Unlike traditional pump-probe schemes, our method utilizes a modulated continuous-wave probe to maintain high spectral resolution, enabling detection of low-frequency Raman shifts down to 10 cm$^{-1}$. Applied to lightly boron-doped silicon, we resolve intra-valence band and inter-valence band electronic transitions. A coupled-mode analysis of transient phonon asymmetry, resulting from interference with the inter-valence band transitions, reveals electron-phonon coupling parameters that directly relate to carrier recombination. By capturing these subtle dynamical shifts, we demonstrate that this platform offers a powerful probe for investigating electron-phonon interactions in long-lived excited states.
one_liner: Time-resolved spontaneous Raman spectroscopy resolves subtle transient electron-phonon coupling in photoexcited boron-doped silicon.
why_it_matters: The method adds high spectral precision to ultrafast materials probes, enabling direct extraction of coupling parameters tied to carrier recombination.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a time-resolved spontaneous Raman technique based on time-correlated single-photon counting and a modulated continuous-wave probe, achieving sub-wavenumber spectral resolution with few-hundred-picosecond timing. Applied to lightly boron-doped silicon, the method resolves both intra-valence-band and inter-valence-band electronic transitions after photoexcitation. A coupled-mode analysis of transient phonon line-shape asymmetry extracts electron-phonon coupling parameters linked to carrier recombination dynamics.
platform_or_system: Time-resolved Raman spectroscopy in doped silicon
main_result: It demonstrates a Raman platform that can directly track weak transient spectral shifts and quantify electron-phonon coupling in long-lived excited semiconductor states.
application_angle: The technique is relevant for semiconductor diagnostics and materials metrology where subtle nonequilibrium coupling signatures matter for device optimization.

title: Capacity of Entanglement and Replica Backreaction in RST Gravity
abstract: We compute the capacity of entanglement in two dimensional dilaton gravity in a setting where Hawking radiation, backreaction, and islands can be treated analytically. Our focus is the eternal black hole of the Russo Susskind Thorlacius model coupled to N conformal matter fields. Unlike previous gravitational computations, which were mostly carried out in JT gravity, the RST model forces one to deal with a genuinely dynamical conformal factor and with the global constraints of the replica construction. The main technical step is therefore to solve the replica deformation on the orbifold globally at first order near n=1, including the homogeneous sector fixed by single valuedness and by the requirement of a fixed microcanonical state. For a single interval we obtain a time independent generalized capacity, parallel to the generalized entropy. For two intervals, even in the late time factorization regime, the global solution generates an interaction term between replica fixed points; after Lorentzian continuation this produces a time dependent capacity on the two QES saddle, despite the corresponding entropy plateau. We discuss the regime of validity of the resulting expressions and explain how the large size of the two QES capacity implies a highly non uniform saddle competition near n=1, providing a concrete mechanism for sharp features of the capacity at the Page transition.
one_liner: Analytic RST-gravity calculations reveal time-dependent capacity of entanglement from replica backreaction even when entropy has plateaued.
why_it_matters: It sharpens how gravitational replica saddles behave near the Page transition, informing quantum-information diagnostics beyond entropy alone.
detailed_summary: The paper computes the capacity of entanglement in the two-dimensional Russo-Susskind-Thorlacius gravity model with Hawking radiation and backreaction treated analytically. A key technical step is solving the first-order replica deformation globally near n=1, including homogeneous modes fixed by single-valuedness and a microcanonical constraint. For a single interval, the generalized capacity is time independent, but for two intervals the global replica solution induces interactions between replica fixed points that generate a time-dependent capacity after Lorentzian continuation. The analysis also argues that large two-QES capacity drives strongly nonuniform saddle competition near the Page transition.
main_result: They derive analytic expressions showing that two-interval entanglement capacity can remain strongly time dependent even when the corresponding entropy is on a late-time plateau.
application_angle: The work provides a sharper information-theoretic probe of black-hole dynamics and replica backreaction that could guide future quantum-simulation or analog-gravity studies.

title: A Hybrid Quantum-Classical Framework for Financial Volatility Forecasting Based on Quantum Circuit Born Machines
abstract: Accurate forecasting of financial market volatility is crucial for risk management, option pricing, and portfolio optimization. Traditional econometric models and classical machine learning methods face challenges in handling the inherent non-linear and non-stationary characteristics of financial time series. In recent years, the rapid development of quantum computing has provided a new paradigm for solving complex optimization and sampling problems. This paper proposes a novel hybrid quantum-classical computing framework aimed at combining the powerful representation capabilities of classical neural networks with the unique advantages of quantum models. For the specific task of financial market volatility forecasting, we designed and implemented a hybrid model based on this framework, which combines a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network with a Quantum Circuit Born Machine (QCBM). The LSTM is responsible for extracting complex dynamic features from historical time series data, while the QCBM serves as a learnable prior module, providing the model with a high-quality prior distribution to guide the forecasting process. We evaluated the model on two real financial datasets consisting of 5-minute high-frequency data from the Shanghai Stock Exchange (SSE) Composite Index and CSI 300 Index. Experimental results show that, compared to a purely classical LSTM baseline model, our hybrid quantum-classical model demonstrates significant advantages across multiple key metrics, including Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and QLIKE loss, proving the great potential of quantum computing in enhancing the capabilities of financial forecasting models. More broadly, the proposed hybrid framework offers a flexible architecture that may be adapted to other machine learning tasks involving high-dimensional, complex, or non-linear data distributions.
one_liner: A hybrid LSTM plus quantum circuit Born machine improves high-frequency financial volatility forecasts over a classical LSTM baseline.
why_it_matters: It is a concrete application of near-term hybrid quantum machine learning to a noisy real-world prediction task with measurable accuracy gains.
detailed_summary: The authors combine an LSTM feature extractor with a quantum circuit Born machine that acts as a learnable prior for volatility forecasting. They test the framework on 5-minute Shanghai Stock Exchange Composite Index and CSI 300 data, comparing against a purely classical LSTM model. Reported improvements appear across MSE, RMSE, and QLIKE metrics, indicating that the quantum prior helps model nonlinear and nonstationary market structure. The architecture is presented as a general hybrid pipeline for complex probabilistic learning tasks.
main_result: The hybrid quantum-classical model outperforms a classical LSTM baseline on two real high-frequency financial datasets using multiple forecasting metrics.
application_angle: It suggests a deployable path for quantum-enhanced probabilistic modeling in finance and other domains with high-dimensional, hard-to-model temporal data.

title: Experimental Characterization of Biological Tissue Dielectric Properties through THz Time-Domain Spectroscopy
abstract: Terahertz (THz) radiation provides a non-ionizing, highly sensitive probe of the dielectric properties of biological tissues. In this study, we present a comprehensive experimental characterization of dielectric properties using pork skin tissue, a widely used surrogate for human tissue, as a biological sample. Measurements are conducted employing THz time-domain spectroscopy in the 0.1-11 THz frequency range with photoconductive antennas for both signal generation and detection. Frequency-dependent refractive indices, absorption, and complex permittivity are extracted from transmitted time-domain signals. Our results confirm strong absorption and low transmittance at low THz frequencies due to water content, while highlighting frequency-dependent dispersion and narrowband transmission features at higher frequencies. This work provides one of the first extended-frequency datasets of biological tissue dielectric properties, supporting realistic channel modeling for the design and development of intra-body nanosensor networks in the THz band.
one_liner: THz time-domain spectroscopy maps pork-skin dielectric properties from 0.1 to 11 THz for bioelectromagnetic modeling.
why_it_matters: The dataset supports realistic THz propagation models for intra-body links and biosensing systems using non-ionizing radiation.
detailed_summary: Using photoconductive antennas for generation and detection, the study measures transmitted THz pulses through pork skin over 0.1-11 THz. From the time-domain data, the authors extract refractive index, absorption, and complex permittivity as frequency-dependent material parameters. The results show strong low-frequency absorption and low transmittance linked to water content, together with dispersive behavior and narrowband transmission features at higher frequencies. The paper positions the measurements as an extended-frequency reference dataset for biological-tissue THz channel modeling.
platform_or_system: THz time-domain spectroscopy of pork skin
main_result: They experimentally obtain broadband dielectric-property data for biological tissue, including refractive index, absorption, and complex permittivity across 0.1-11 THz.
application_angle: These measurements are directly useful for designing THz biosensors, medical imaging approaches, and intra-body nanosensor communication channels.

title: An Introduction to the Foundations and Interpretations of Quantum Mechanics
abstract: This article surveys key conceptual and interpretational developments in quantum mechanics, tracing the theory from its foundational postulates to contemporary discussions of measurement, nonlocality, and the emergence of classicality. Beginning with the structure of Hilbert space and the postulates governing state evolution and measurement, the epistemic stance of the Copenhagen interpretation and its modern reformulations are examined. The Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen argument, Bell's theorem, and Hardy's paradox are then discussed as probes of locality and realism, alongside the deterministic but explicitly nonlocal de Broglie-Bohm theory. The measurement problem and the implications of contextuality are analyzed in relation to objective collapse models, which introduce new physical dynamics to account for definite outcomes. Finally, the role of decoherence in the suppression of interference and the emergence of classical behavior is explored, together with the interpretational frameworks of many-worlds and consistent histories. This material aims to provide a coherent introductory overview of how different interpretations address the central concern of what quantum mechanics tells us about the nature of physical reality.
one_liner: A compact survey reviews quantum foundations, measurement, nonlocality, decoherence, and major interpretational frameworks.
why_it_matters: It consolidates the conceptual landscape behind quantum technologies, though it is primarily pedagogical rather than a new experimental advance.
detailed_summary: This article reviews the formal postulates of quantum mechanics and then surveys major interpretational responses to measurement, realism, and classical emergence. It covers Copenhagen-style views, EPR and Bell arguments, Hardy's paradox, de Broglie-Bohm theory, contextuality, objective-collapse models, decoherence, many-worlds, and consistent histories. The work is structured as an introduction rather than an experimental or hardware contribution. Its value lies in clarifying the conceptual assumptions that underpin quantum-information and quantum-device research.
main_result: The paper synthesizes the main interpretational and conceptual frameworks used to discuss measurement, nonlocality, and the emergence of classical behavior in quantum theory.
application_angle: It can serve as background material for researchers and students entering quantum technology fields who need a clear map of foundational assumptions and debates.

title: Quantum-preserving telecom conversion of atomic biphotons
abstract: We experimentally demonstrate telecom frequency conversion of atomic biphotons using a diamond-type atomic ensemble. By spectrally engineering heralded photons and optimizing the atomic converter, efficient conversion is achieved while preserving the temporal waveform and nonclassical antibunching behavior. The converted photons retain strong quantum correlations and well-defined wavepackets, demonstrating preservation of dynamical quantum properties beyond photon-statistics-based benchmarks. The measured performance agrees with a microscopic model that captures the spectral acceptance and parameter dependence of the converter. These results establish a practical interface between atomic photon sources and telecom fiber networks, enabling quantum interference and distributed quantum communication.
one_liner: Atomic biphotons are converted to telecom wavelengths while preserving waveform, antibunching, and strong quantum correlations.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates a practical quantum interface between narrowband atomic photon sources and low-loss fiber telecom networks.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a diamond-type atomic ensemble to frequency-convert heralded atomic biphotons into the telecom band. By spectrally engineering the input photons and optimizing the atomic converter, the authors preserve the temporal envelope, antibunching behavior, and strong pair correlations after conversion. A microscopic model reproduces the observed spectral acceptance and parameter dependence of the process. The result goes beyond simple count-statistics benchmarks by showing preservation of dynamical quantum wavepacket properties.
main_result: They experimentally achieve telecom-band conversion of atomic biphotons while retaining nonclassical correlations and the original temporal wavepacket structure.
application_angle: This interface is directly relevant for long-distance quantum networking, enabling atomic quantum memories and sources to connect efficiently through standard telecom fiber.

title: Crosstalk in Multi-Qubit Fluxonium Architectures with Transmon Couplers
abstract: In recent years, several architectures have been proposed for implementing two-qubit operations on fluxonium superconducting qubits. A particularly promising approach, which was demonstrated experimentally by Refs. [1,2], employs a transmon superconducting qubit as a tunable coupler between the fluxonium qubits. These experiments have shown that the transmon coupler enables fast, high-fidelity two-qubit operations while suppressing unwanted ZZ crosstalk between the fluxonium qubits. In this work, we numerically study the scalability of this architecture. We find that, when trivially scaling this architecture, crosstalk from spectator qubits limits the gate fidelity to below 90%. We show that these spectator errors can be reduced to below $10^{-4}$ by reducing the coupling strength and by dynamically tuning transmons that are not used for a two-qubit operation to an off position. We further investigate the resilience of the operation to direct capacitive coupling between the transmon couplers and to microwave crosstalk.
one_liner: Numerical scaling analysis shows spectator crosstalk can severely limit fluxonium gates unless idle transmon couplers are tuned off.
why_it_matters: It identifies a concrete control strategy to preserve high-fidelity two-qubit gates in scalable fluxonium architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper studies multi-qubit fluxonium processors that use tunable transmon qubits as couplers for two-qubit gates. Simulations indicate that naive scaling introduces spectator-qubit crosstalk that can drive gate fidelity below 90 percent. The authors show that reducing coupling strength and dynamically parking unused transmon couplers in an off state can suppress spectator errors below 1e-4. They also analyze robustness to direct capacitive coupling between couplers and to microwave crosstalk.
platform_or_system: fluxonium qubits with tunable transmon couplers
main_result: The study finds that scalable high-fidelity operation is feasible if inactive transmon couplers are dynamically turned off and couplings are carefully reduced.
application_angle: The results provide actionable architecture and control guidance for building larger superconducting quantum processors based on fluxonium qubits.

title: Direct Laser Writing of Ferromagnetic Nickel Utilizing the Principle of Sensitized Triplet-Triplet Annihilation Upconversion
abstract: Direct laser writing of ferromagnetic microstructures is of great interest for sensing and data storage in compact three-dimensional architectures. However, reliable direct laser writing of metallic and even more so ferromagnetic materials remains a major challenge. Here, we present a novel photoresist suitable to direct laser write ferromagnetic nickel based on sensitized triplet-triplet annihilation upconversion. By combining an in-situ photochemical deoxygenation process with a sensitized triplet-triplet annihilation upconversion process as well as a photoreduction of Ni2+ ions, the deposition of metallic nickel is enabled under ambient conditions. Using this approach, nickel structures are fabricated as a proof of concept. Scanning electron microscopy and EDX analysis confirm the spatially confined deposition of nickel, while magnetic characterization by vibrating sample magnetometry and scanning NV magnetometry demonstrate the ferromagnetic nature of the printed structures. This work presents a major step forward in extending the possibilities of direct laser writing to metallic and ferromagnetic materials.
one_liner: A triplet-triplet-annihilation photoresist enables ambient direct laser writing of ferromagnetic nickel microstructures.
why_it_matters: It extends 3D laser microfabrication to magnetic metals, opening compact architectures for microsensors, magnetic devices, and data storage.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a photoresist that combines in-situ photochemical deoxygenation, sensitized triplet-triplet annihilation upconversion, and photoreduction of Ni2+ to deposit metallic nickel under ambient conditions. As a proof of concept, the authors print spatially confined nickel microstructures and verify composition with SEM and EDX. Ferromagnetism is confirmed by vibrating-sample magnetometry and scanning NV magnetometry, showing that the written features are not just metallic but magnetically functional.
main_result: The authors demonstrate ambient-condition direct laser writing of ferromagnetic nickel structures with verified magnetic response.
application_angle: This approach could enable 3D-integrated magnetic microsensors, on-chip field sources, and dense magnetic memory geometries that are hard to realize with planar fabrication.

title: Quantum control of the environment in open quantum systems enables rapid qubit reset
abstract: Qubit reset is crucial in quantum technology and is typically achieved by coupling the qubit to a dissipative environment. However, the achievable speed and fidelity are limited by qubit-environment entanglement. We use exact tensor-network simulations and a time-dependent variational approach to investigate these effects for transmon qubits with a time-dependent system-environment coupling. We show that they are due to the formation of a polaron state and how this can be reversed using a time-dependent coupling. Coupling protocols are identified which achieve reset with an excited-state population of $10^{-6}$ in $10$ ns. A related paper [C. Ortega-Taberner, E. O'Neill and P. R. Eastham, arXiv:XXXX.XXXX] addresses the complementary case of control via a time-dependent Hamiltonian. Our work shows how the dynamics of the environment of an open quantum system can be controlled to design effective quantum processes in non-Markovian systems.
one_liner: Controlling qubit-environment coupling in time enables ultrafast transmon reset despite non-Markovian polaron formation.
why_it_matters: Fast, high-fidelity reset is a core bottleneck for superconducting processors, and this work shows environment control can push reset to 10 ns at 10^-6 error.
detailed_summary: Using exact tensor-network simulations and a time-dependent variational method, the authors study reset of a transmon coupled to a dissipative environment with tunable coupling strength. They identify qubit-environment entanglement as polaron formation that limits conventional reset performance beyond Born-Markov assumptions. Time-dependent coupling protocols are then designed to reverse this buildup of correlations and achieve an excited-state population of 10^-6 in 10 ns.
platform_or_system: Transmon qubit coupled to a dissipative bath
main_result: Time-dependent control of system-environment coupling enables transmon reset to 10^-6 excited-state population within 10 ns.
application_angle: The result is directly relevant to shortening idle time and increasing cycle speed in superconducting quantum processors where reset overhead limits throughput.

title: Qubit reset beyond the Born-Markov approximation: optimal driving to overcome polaron formation
abstract: Qubits are typically reset into a known state by coupling them to a low-temperature environment. When treated in the Born-Markov approximation such couplings produce exponential relaxation to equilibrium, giving high reset fidelities limited only by temperature. We investigate qubit reset beyond this approximation, using numerically exact tensor network methods and the time-dependent variational principle, focussing on a spin-boson model describing a transmon qubit coupled to a resistor. Beyond the Born-Markov approximation the reset fidelity becomes limited by the buildup of system-environment correlations which corresponds to the formation of a polaron. We implement numerical optimal control to find time-dependent qubit Hamiltonians which overcome this limitation by steering the dynamics of the correlated system-environment state. The optimal controls becomes more effective when the environment is filtered to span a smaller spectral range, and remain effective when the multilevel nature of the transmon is considered. A related paper [C. Ortega-Taberner, E. O'Neill and P. R. Eastham, arXiv:XXXX.XXXX] addresses the complementary case of control via a time-dependent system-environment coupling. Our results show how limitations on reset speed and fidelity can be overcome, and how time-dependent driving can steer system-environment correlations and reverse polaron formation.
one_liner: Optimal time-dependent driving can undo polaron-induced reset limits and accelerate transmon initialization beyond Born-Markov theory.
why_it_matters: It shows hardware-efficient reset can be improved by pulse design even in strongly correlated open-system regimes relevant to real superconducting devices.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze qubit reset in a spin-boson model for a transmon coupled to a resistor using numerically exact tensor-network methods. They show that beyond the Born-Markov approximation, reset fidelity is constrained by system-environment correlations associated with polaron formation. Numerical optimal control of the qubit Hamiltonian steers the joint state to reverse these correlations, with stronger performance when the environment is spectrally filtered and with robustness retained in multilevel transmon models.
main_result: Optimal control pulses on the qubit Hamiltonian overcome non-Markovian polaron limits and improve reset speed and fidelity.
application_angle: The method offers a realistic route to better active reset protocols for superconducting qubits without requiring idealized weak-coupling assumptions.

title: A complete classification of 2d symmetry protected states with symmetric entanglers
abstract: We consider symmetry protected topological states of 2d quantum spin systems, with a finite symmetry group $G$. It has been conjectured that such states are classified by the cohomology group $H^3(G,U(1))$, but the completeness of this classfication is an open problem. We restrict ourselves to symmetry protected topological states that can be prepared from a product state by a symmetric entangler. For this class of states, we prove that the classification by $H^3(G,U(1))$ is complete.
one_liner: This work proves the H^3(G,U(1)) classification is complete for 2D SPT states preparable by symmetric entanglers.
why_it_matters: A complete classification sharpens the design rules for symmetry-protected resource states used in quantum many-body control and information processing.
detailed_summary: The paper studies two-dimensional symmetry-protected topological states of quantum spin systems with finite symmetry group G, restricted to states generated from product states by symmetric entanglers. Within this physically motivated preparation class, the authors prove that classification by the cohomology group H^3(G,U(1)) is complete. The result resolves a key completeness question for this subclass of 2D SPT states and gives a rigorous boundary on what distinct phases can occur.
main_result: For 2D SPT states reachable by symmetric entanglers, the authors prove that H^3(G,U(1)) gives the full classification.
application_angle: The theorem provides a firmer theoretical basis for engineering and validating structured entangled states that could serve as protected resources in quantum devices.

title: Learning from Radio using Variational Quantum RF Sensing
abstract: In modern wireless networks, radio channels serve a dual role. Whilst their primary function is to carry bits of information from a transmitter to a receiver, the intrinsic sensitivity of transmitted signals to the physical structure of the environment makes the channel a powerful source of knowledge about the world. In this paper, we consider an agent that learns about its environment using a quantum sensing probe, optimised using a quantum circuit, which interacts with the radio-frequency (RF) electromagnetic field. We use data obtained from a ray-tracer to train the quantum circuit and learning model and we provide extensive experiments under realistic conditions on a localisation task. We show that using quantum sensors to learn from radio signals can enable intelligent systems that require no channel measurements at deployment, remain sensitive to weak and obstructed RF signals, and can learn about the world despite operating with strictly less information than classical baselines.
one_liner: A variational quantum RF sensor learns environment structure from radio propagation and improves localization under weak or blocked signals.
why_it_matters: It points to quantum-enhanced RF sensing for wireless localization without deployment-time channel measurements, a practical edge in cluttered environments.
detailed_summary: The paper considers an agent that probes radio-frequency electromagnetic fields with a quantum sensor whose operation is optimized by a variational quantum circuit. Training data come from a ray-tracer, and the learned model is evaluated on localization tasks under realistic wireless conditions. The reported advantage is that the quantum sensing approach can infer environmental information without channel measurements at deployment, while remaining sensitive to weak and obstructed RF signals and operating with less information than classical baselines.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a quantum-RF-learning framework that performs localization effectively under realistic weak-signal and obstruction scenarios.
application_angle: This could matter for low-overhead indoor positioning, spectrum-aware robotics, and sensing in environments where classical channel estimation is costly or unreliable.

title: Quantum batteries and time dilation
abstract: Is spacetime fundamental or can it be derived through quantum interactions? We propose here a way to describe time dilation solely from quantum mechanics. First we start by observing that any operational notion of time must imply some sort of regular motion and, crucially, some sort of memory. Thus the clock model we use here is a simple example of a quantum memory: a charging battery. We describe here the charging of such batteries with quantum open dynamics. The expected value of these batteries grow linearly in time like $\varphi t$. The open dynamics is dependent on an auxiliar state $σ$. Therefore, with a different auxiliar state we have a different $\varphi$. We can describe time dilation and thus a metric this way. We exemplify with a black hole metric.
one_liner: Open-dynamics charging of quantum batteries is used as an operational clock model to encode time dilation via state-dependent rates.
why_it_matters: It proposes a quantum-informational route to effective spacetime notions, linking timing, memory, and open-system dynamics in communication-relevant settings.
detailed_summary: The authors model a clock as a quantum memory in the form of a charging battery evolving under open dynamics. In their framework, the battery's expected energy grows linearly in time with a rate phi that depends on an auxiliary state sigma. Different auxiliary states therefore define different effective clock rates, which the paper interprets as time dilation and uses to construct a metric, including an illustrative black-hole example.
main_result: The work shows how state-dependent quantum battery charging rates can reproduce an operational notion of time dilation and metric structure.
application_angle: If developed further, the framework could inform quantum-network timing and relativistic synchronization models where clocks are treated as physical quantum resources.

title: A fully solution-processed organic microcavity laser in the strong light-matter coupling regime
abstract: Solid-state semiconductor lasers underpin technologies from telecommunications and data storage to sensing, medical diagnostics, and emerging quantum communication. Polaritons-hybrid exciton-photon states have further extended this reach, enabling room-temperature quantum effects such as low-threshold lasing and single-photon nonlinearities. Organic semiconductors are ideal for polaritonics due to their large exciton binding energy, strong optical nonlinearities, and straightforward processing, making them attractive for both classical and quantum photonics. While solution-processed organic films have been widely explored, their optical cavities have almost always been fabricated using vacuum deposition, limiting the realization of truly scalable and low-cost devices. Here, we report the first organic laser microcavities fabricated entirely by solution processing, which operate in the strong coupling regimeThe resulting platform can be driven reliably to high excitation densities, where we observe a reversible, interaction-driven redistribution of the polariton condensate, revealing a distinct polariton lasing behaviour in organic microcavities. Together, the fabrication approach and the observed lasing dynamics establish a route toward scalable polaritonic and quantum photonic technologies and provide new opportunities for studying nonlinear polariton physics in organic systems.
one_liner: An all-solution-processed organic microcavity reaches strong coupling and shows reversible polariton lasing dynamics at high excitation density.
why_it_matters: It removes vacuum-deposition bottlenecks for organic polaritonic devices, strengthening the path to scalable low-cost photonic and quantum-light sources.
detailed_summary: The work reports the first organic laser microcavities fabricated entirely by solution processing that still operate in the strong light-matter coupling regime. Under high excitation, the authors observe a reversible interaction-driven redistribution of the polariton condensate, consistent with distinct polariton lasing behavior rather than conventional photon lasing. The result combines manufacturable cavity fabrication with nonlinear polariton dynamics in an organic semiconductor platform relevant to room-temperature photonics.
main_result: A fully solution-processed organic microcavity was shown to support strong coupling and reversible polariton-lasing dynamics at high pump density.
application_angle: The fabrication route is compatible with scalable, low-cost production of organic polaritonic components for integrated photonics and prospective quantum optical devices.

title: Frozen mode in coupled single-mode waveguides with gratings
abstract: We present a systematic methodology for designing slow-light photonic integrated circuits with a frozen mode based on a special kind of exceptional point of degeneracy (EPD) of order three named stationary inflection points (SIPs). This is realized through three-way coupled waveguides with lateral gratings operating at telecommunication wavelengths. We provide two designs and analyze sensitivity to geometric perturbations. We have fabricated a periodic waveguide with integrated taper loads and demonstrate reasonable agreement with full-wave simulations. These findings confirm the feasibility of integrating SIP-based delay functionalities in standard silicon photonic platforms.
one_liner: Coupled grating waveguides are designed and fabricated to realize a frozen mode slow-light effect via a third-order stationary inflection point.
why_it_matters: Frozen-mode delay elements on standard silicon photonics could enable compact buffers, stronger light-matter interaction, and more sensitive integrated photonic functions.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a design methodology for photonic integrated circuits that realize a frozen mode using a third-order exceptional-point-of-degeneracy known as a stationary inflection point. The implementation uses three-way coupled single-mode waveguides with lateral gratings at telecom wavelengths, and the authors study tolerance to geometric perturbations. A fabricated periodic waveguide with taper loads shows reasonable agreement with full-wave simulations, supporting the practicality of the concept on silicon photonic platforms.
main_result: The authors designed and experimentally validated a silicon-photonic waveguide structure exhibiting SIP-based frozen-mode slow-light behavior.
application_angle: Such structures can serve as integrated delay lines or field-enhancement elements for telecom photonic circuits, modulators, and on-chip sensing.

title: CHSH inequality always holds in bipartite qutrits with spin-1 observables
abstract: We resolve a conjecture of Hanotel and Loubenets concerning CHSH inequality in bipartite qutrits. It states that nonseparable pure states of two qutrits do not violate the CHSH inequality when each party is restricted to spin-1 observables. We prove a stronger result that \emph{all} bipartite states on $\mathbb{C}^3 \otimes \mathbb{C}^3$ satisfy the CHSH inequality under spin-1 measurements, regardless of whether the state is pure or mixed.
one_liner: The paper proves that bipartite qutrit states never violate CHSH when both parties are restricted to spin-1 observables.
why_it_matters: It sharply constrains what nonclassical correlations are experimentally accessible in spin-1 qutrit tests and guides measurement design for higher-dimensional platforms.
detailed_summary: This work resolves a conjecture about Bell nonlocality in two-qutrit systems measured with spin-1 observables. It proves not only that nonseparable pure states fail to violate CHSH under this restriction, but that all states on C^3 tensor C^3, including mixed states, satisfy the CHSH inequality. The result is a measurement-specific no-go theorem that clarifies the operational limits of spin-1 tests in qutrit experiments.
platform_or_system: bipartite qutrit system with spin-1 measurements
main_result: All bipartite qutrit states were proven to obey the CHSH inequality when local measurements are restricted to spin-1 observables.
application_angle: The theorem helps experimental groups avoid unproductive CHSH configurations and motivates alternative measurement sets for high-dimensional quantum communication tests.

title: Machine learning the arrow of time in solid-state spins
abstract: Understanding the emergence of the thermodynamic arrow of time in microscopic systems is of fundamental importance, particularly given that unitary evolution preserves time-reversal symmetry. While projective measurements introduce temporal irreversibility, identifying this asymmetry from single evolution trajectories in the presence of stochastic fluctuations presents a considerable challenge. Here, we harness machine learning to identify the arrow of time from individual trajectories generated by a programmable ten-qubit quantum processor based on a nitrogen-vacancy center in diamond. We implement quantum circuits that realize unitary evolutions where heat flows from hotter to colder subsystems and their time-reversed counterparts. Projective measurements inserted in these processes induce entropy production, and their outcomes constitute the evolution trajectory. We demonstrate that an unsupervised clustering algorithm autonomously divides the experimental trajectories into two distinct groups without prior knowledge, while a convolutional neural network identifies the temporal direction of these trajectories with approximately 92% accuracy. In addition, we show that a diffusion-based generative model reproduces essential signatures of directional energy flow and entropy production. Our results establish machine learning as a powerful tool for uncovering underlying physical processes from complex experimental data, advancing the interface between quantum thermodynamics and artificial intelligence.
one_liner: Machine-learning models identify time direction from single-shot trajectories measured on a ten-qubit NV-center quantum processor.
why_it_matters: It shows that AI can recover thermodynamic irreversibility directly from noisy hardware data, opening practical analysis tools for quantum processors and spin devices.
detailed_summary: Using a programmable ten-qubit nitrogen-vacancy-center processor, the authors generate forward and time-reversed quantum trajectories involving heat flow between hotter and colder subsystems. Projective measurements introduce entropy production, and those measurement records are used as input to unsupervised clustering, a CNN classifier, and a diffusion-based generative model. The CNN identifies the temporal direction with about 92% accuracy, while the generative model reproduces signatures of directional energy flow and entropy production.
main_result: Experimental single-trajectory data from a ten-qubit NV platform allowed unsupervised separation of forward and reverse processes and CNN-based arrow-of-time classification at roughly 92% accuracy.
application_angle: The methods provide a practical diagnostics layer for extracting hidden thermodynamic structure from experimental quantum-hardware data and benchmarking nonequilibrium control protocols.

title: Development of an Extensible Unified Control System Using the STARS Framework and Common Commands for Detector Control
abstract: Two Fresnel zone plates zooming optics have been successfully developed and installed at the AR-NE1A beamline of the Photon Factory at the high energy accelerator research organization (KEK) in Japan. To ensure the reliable and versatile operation of this optical instrumentation, a dedicated control architecture has been implemented based on the simple transmission and retrieval system (STARS) framework, incorporating the newly proposed STARS common commands for detector control (CCDC) -- a detector-specific data acquisition (DAQ) state and command system. This system serves as both a practical control system for zooming optics and a demonstration model for modular extensibility using the STARS framework and inter-operability among detector systems enabled by the CCDC command set. The system has been commissioned, and its performance has been verified at the AR NE1A beamline. The control architecture affords enhanced configurational flexibility for optical components and provides an interface appropriate for both routine users and advanced experimental protocols.
one_liner: A modular STARS-based control stack with common detector commands was commissioned for Fresnel-zone-plate zoom optics at KEK beamlines.
why_it_matters: Standardized detector and optics control improves reliability, interoperability, and experimental throughput for complex photon-science instrumentation.
detailed_summary: The paper describes an extensible control architecture for two Fresnel zone plate zooming optics installed at the AR-NE1A beamline of the Photon Factory. It combines the STARS framework with newly proposed common commands for detector control, defining a detector-specific DAQ state and command system that supports interoperability. Commissioning at the beamline verified operation and demonstrated configurational flexibility for both routine experiments and more advanced protocols.
platform_or_system: synchrotron beamline optics and detector control system
main_result: A unified STARS-based control system with common detector commands was implemented and validated on operating zoom-optics instrumentation at a synchrotron beamline.
application_angle: The architecture is directly useful for deploying maintainable, interoperable control software across beamline optics and detector systems in precision measurement facilities.

title: Practical Methods for Distance-Adaptive Continuous-Variable Quantum Key Distribution
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) is a promising quantum-safe alternative to classical asymmetric cryptography that enables two authenticated parties to establish a shared secret over a potentially eavesdropped quantum channel. A key step in CV-QKD post-processing is information reconciliation, which leverages forward error correction (FEC) techniques to extract identical bit strings from noisy correlated data. In this work, we analyze the strict limitations on operating distance that are imposed by constant-rate FEC, severely limiting the practicability of CV-QKD systems in deployed optical networks. To overcome the distance limitations, we evaluate three strategies: (i) tuning modulation variance, (ii) adding controlled amounts of trusted detector loss, and (iii) the use of rate-adaptive FEC. All approaches are validated experimentally, compared in terms of performance, and we discuss implementation aspects. Our results show that while methods (i) and (ii) extend the operational distance of constant-rate FEC without the need for additional hardware components, they incur a significant penalty in secret key rate (SKR). In contrast, rate-adaptive FEC enables CV-QKD operation with performance close to the asymptotic SKR over a wide range of distances, provided that the reconciliation efficiency is chosen appropriately.
one_liner: Three distance-adaptation methods for CV-QKD are experimentally compared, with rate-adaptive FEC giving the broadest reach near asymptotic performance.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical deployment bottleneck in CV-QKD by showing how to maintain operation over varying link distances without severe key-rate loss.
detailed_summary: The study analyzes how constant-rate forward-error correction sharply limits the operating distance of continuous-variable QKD systems in realistic networks. It experimentally evaluates three mitigation strategies: modulation-variance tuning, adding trusted detector loss, and rate-adaptive FEC. While the first two extend reach at a notable secret-key-rate penalty, rate-adaptive FEC maintains operation across a wide distance range with performance close to the asymptotic secret key rate when reconciliation efficiency is properly chosen.
main_result: Rate-adaptive FEC was shown experimentally to deliver distance-adaptive CV-QKD operation with performance close to asymptotic secret key rate across a broad range of channel lengths.
application_angle: The results directly support deployable CV-QKD systems on changing optical-network links where channel loss varies and fixed-rate reconciliation is inadequate.

title: Remote engineering of particle-like topologies to visualise entanglement dynamics
abstract: Skyrmions are a particle-like topology with a quantised skyrmion number, realised across condensed matter and photonic platforms alike. In quantum photonics, they constitute an emerging resource, promising robust quantum information encoding, so far realised as single photon and bi-photon entangled states. Here we report the first visualisation of tripartite entanglement dynamics through topological structure using spin-skyrmion entangled states, where the topology of a single photon is remotely controlled through the spin of its entangled partner. We visualise our tripartite state theoretically by introducing the notion of a topological Bloch sphere that completely captures the entanglement and topolological features of the state. By leveraging this state, we realise the first quantum multiskyrmions, comprising multiple localised skyrmions within a single structure, that emulate signatures of their magnetic counterparts. We verify this experimentally and show that traversing our topological sphere reveals entanglement-driven particle-like motion of the localised topological structures. These dynamics unveil a physical manifestation of tripartite entanglement correlations which we illustrate by example of GHZ-like states, enabling a visualisation of multiple Bell states encoded within our system. Our work opens exciting possibilities for quantum sensing by mapping complex quantum channel features onto topological observables of multipartite states and offers a promising avenue for harnessing quantum topologies for multi-level encoding quantum communication schemes.
one_liner: Tripartite spin-skyrmion photonic states let one photon remotely steer topological textures on its entangled partner.
why_it_matters: It links multipartite entanglement to directly visualizable topological observables, suggesting robust high-dimensional encoding and sensing primitives.
detailed_summary: The work introduces spin-skyrmion entangled states in which the topology carried by one photon is remotely controlled via the spin state of its entangled partner, yielding a photonic realization of tripartite entanglement dynamics. The authors define a topological Bloch sphere that jointly parameterizes the state's entanglement structure and skyrmionic topology. They further realize quantum multiskyrmions with multiple localized skyrmions in a single optical structure and experimentally verify entanglement-driven motion of these localized topological features. The framework also maps GHZ-like correlations and multiple Bell-state signatures onto observable topological dynamics.
main_result: They experimentally demonstrate remote control and visualization of multipartite entanglement through photonic skyrmion topology, including multiskyrmion structures and entanglement-driven motion.
application_angle: Topological observables could provide robust readout channels for quantum sensing and support multi-level photonic encoding for quantum communication.

title: Full-channel wavefront manipulation of surface waves with chirality-assisted geometric-phase metasurface
abstract: Owing to their localized field enhancement and subwavelength resolution, surface waves (SWs) offer broad application potential in communications, sensing, and photonics via on-chip wavefront manipulation. This makes multi-channel SW wavefront manipulation highly desirable. However, conventional metasurfaces for SW wavefront shaping, relying on geometric and propagation phase mechanisms, typically exhibit similar functionalities for co- or cross-polarized output channels under different circularly polarized (CP) incidences, thereby limiting the development of high-capacity on-chip integrated devices. Here, by introducing the chirality-assisted phase as an additional phase control mechanism, we effectively decouple both co- and cross-polarized output channels, enabling independent SW wavefront shaping in four distinct channels. We numerically and experimentally demonstrate two metasurfaces in the microwave range: a four-channel SW meta-deflector and a four-channel SW metadevice that simultaneously produces a focused SW beam, a SW Bessel beam, and two deflected SW beams in different directions. Therefore, chirality-assisted geometric-phase metasurfaces provide a versatile platform for multi-channel SW wavefront engineering, offering significant potential for high-capacity on-chip communication and integrated photonic systems.
one_liner: A chirality-assisted metasurface independently programs four surface-wave channels under different circular-polarization inputs.
why_it_matters: Independent control of four surface-wave channels increases functionality and channel density for integrated sensing and communication hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper adds chirality-assisted phase to geometric-phase metasurface design, allowing co- and cross-polarized surface-wave output channels to be decoupled rather than constrained to similar responses. In the microwave regime, the authors numerically and experimentally demonstrate a four-channel surface-wave deflector and a multifunctional device that simultaneously generates a focused beam, a Bessel beam, and two deflected beams. This achieves independent wavefront engineering across four distinct channels from circularly polarized inputs. The approach directly targets compact on-chip surface-wave control with higher channel capacity than conventional metasurfaces.
main_result: They experimentally realize four independently addressable surface-wave channels using chirality-assisted geometric-phase metasurfaces.
application_angle: The design is relevant for high-capacity on-chip sensing, routing, and integrated photonic or communication components that need multiple simultaneous surface-wave functions.

title: Temporal-Mode Engineering for Multiplexed Microwave Photons and Mode-Selective Quantum State Transfer
abstract: Quantum communication between distant superconducting qubits on separate chips using itinerant microwave photons has been studied to realize distributed quantum information processing. To enhance information capacity and fault tolerance in quantum networks, it is beneficial to encode a large quantity of quantum information using auxiliary degrees of freedom of these photons. In this work, we experimentally investigate the use of temporal modes of photon wave packets. Through the photon-shaping technique with a fixed-frequency transmon qubit, we generate single microwave photons in four orthogonal temporal modes propagating along a waveguide. We demonstrate mode-selective absorption across orthogonal modes via the time-reversed process of emission, achieving absorption efficiencies exceeding 0.89 for mode-matched cases, while remaining below 0.13 for orthogonal modes. Photons rejected by a given receiver mode can remain mutually orthogonal, enabling selective absorption at subsequent receivers in future multi-node architectures. These results highlight the feasibility of temporal-mode engineering for constructing a higher-dimensional orthogonal basis for multiplexed quantum networks.
one_liner: Orthogonal temporal modes of microwave single photons are generated and selectively absorbed for multiplexed superconducting quantum links.
why_it_matters: Temporal-mode selectivity adds a scalable encoding dimension for distributed superconducting processors and quantum network multiplexing.
detailed_summary: Using a fixed-frequency transmon and photon-shaping control, the experiment generates itinerant single microwave photons in four orthogonal temporal modes propagating through a waveguide. A time-reversed absorption process is then used to demonstrate mode-selective capture, with efficiencies above 0.89 for matched modes and below 0.13 for orthogonal modes. The rejected photons remain mutually orthogonal, preserving the possibility of subsequent routing or capture by downstream receivers. This establishes temporal modes as a practical higher-dimensional basis for microwave quantum networking.
main_result: The authors demonstrate four orthogonal temporal-mode microwave photons and mode-selective quantum state transfer with high matched-mode absorption and strong orthogonality rejection.
application_angle: Temporal-mode multiplexing can increase channel capacity and routing flexibility in chip-to-chip superconducting quantum communication networks.

title: Quantum-logic spectroscopy of forbidden vibrational transitions in single nitrogen molecular ions
abstract: Electric-dipole forbidden spectroscopic transitions in atoms form the basis of many advanced implementations of quantum computers, atomic clocks and quantum sensors. Coherently addressing such transitions in molecules which are among the most ubiquitous and versatile quantum objects has remained a long-standing challenge owing to their complex energy-level structure. Here, we report the search, observation and coherent manipulation of electric-quadrupole rotational-vibrational transitions in single trapped molecules using a quantum-logic-spectroscopy protocol. We identified individual hyperfine-Zeeman-rotational components of the fundamental vibrational transition of the nitrogen molecular ion, N$_2^+$, and performed coherent population transfer between energy levels. Our work opens up new perspectives for precision molecular spectroscopy, for high-fidelity qubits encoded in the rotational-vibrational motion of molecules, for precise infrared molecular clocks and for searches for new physics
one_liner: Quantum-logic spectroscopy accesses and coherently drives forbidden vibrational transitions in a single trapped N2+ molecular ion.
why_it_matters: It opens precision control of molecular forbidden transitions for clocks, sensing, molecular qubits, and tests of fundamental physics.
detailed_summary: The study uses a quantum-logic-spectroscopy protocol to search for, identify, and coherently manipulate electric-quadrupole rovibrational transitions in single trapped nitrogen molecular ions. Individual hyperfine-Zeeman-rotational components of the fundamental vibrational transition in N2+ are resolved, and coherent population transfer between molecular levels is demonstrated. Accessing these electric-dipole-forbidden transitions overcomes a long-standing control challenge caused by molecular level complexity.
