The result extends atom-style precision metrology and coherent control techniques into molecular platforms.
main_result: They observe and coherently drive forbidden rovibrational transitions in single N2+ ions using quantum-logic spectroscopy.
application_angle: The capability supports infrared molecular clocks, high-resolution molecular sensors, and molecular-state encodings for future quantum hardware.

title: Entanglement distribution among distinct mechanical nodes in a quantum network
abstract: We propose two schemes to achieve remote entanglement distribution between two mechanical nodes with a significant frequency mismatch, based on optomechanical systems. The first scheme utilizes the physical mechanism to redistribute the quantum entanglement initially established in a dispersively-coupled optomechanical system with a megahertz mechanical resonance to a distant optomechanical system which embodies the tripleresonant interaction induced by the scattering of gigahertz mechanical phonon. We also provide a fast optical pulse protocol to realize the long-distance entanglement distribution from the optomechanical system supporting the gigahertz mechanical mode to the megahertz mechanical mode included in a distant optomechanical system. Specifically, these two schemes respectively demonstrate the megahertz-to-gigahertz and gigahertz-tomegahertz entanglement distribution in the quantum network of optical photons and phonons. This work may facilitate the application of various mechanical systems in hybrid quantum network-based quantum technologies and fundamental physical research.
one_liner: Two optomechanical protocols distribute entanglement between remote mechanical nodes with strongly mismatched MHz and GHz resonances.
why_it_matters: Bridging disparate mechanical frequencies is a key step toward heterogeneous quantum networks that combine specialized phononic nodes.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes two remote entanglement-distribution schemes for optomechanical nodes with large mechanical frequency mismatch. One redistributes entanglement from a dispersively coupled MHz optomechanical system to a distant triply resonant system supporting GHz phonon scattering, while the other uses fast optical pulses for the reverse GHz-to-MHz direction. Together, the schemes enable both MHz-to-GHz and GHz-to-MHz entanglement distribution mediated by optical and mechanical degrees of freedom. This targets hybrid network architectures where different mechanical platforms play complementary roles.
main_result: They propose bidirectional entanglement-distribution protocols linking remote mechanical resonators across megahertz and gigahertz frequency scales.
application_angle: Such frequency-bridging protocols could connect otherwise incompatible mechanical subsystems in long-distance hybrid quantum communication and transduction networks.

title: Optical quantum teleportation with known amplitude distorting factors of teleported qubits
abstract: We develop a quantum teleportation protocol of an unknown optical single rail qubit using a hybrid quantum channel composed of continuous variable (CV) states of certain parity. The quantum channel is characterized by two parameters: a squeezing parameter of single-mode squeezed vacuum (SMSV) state and the beam splitter (BS) parameter used to implement it. The CV part of the hybrid state belongs to Alice, while discrete variable (DV) half is controlled by Bob. The third parameter of the protocol is a parameter of the beam splitter, used to mix the CV components of the hybrid quantum state with unknown optical single-rail qubit. Even though the number of measurement results Alice sends may increase, Bob can obtain the original qubit half the time with an appropriate choice of parameter values. In almost half the remaining cases, Bob obtains the original qubit with distorted amplitudes, and both participants know the value of the distortion factors. This means that as the amount of classical information transmitted by Alice increases, they both gain greater access to partial information about the unitary transformations that the teleported qubits undergo, allowing Bob to continue using them or attempt to recover them to improve the protocol's efficiency. The proposed method is a generalization of quantum teleportation with a nonlocal photon used as a quantum channel and unknown single-rail optical qubit.
one_liner: A hybrid CV-DV teleportation scheme for single-rail optical qubits tracks and exploits known amplitude distortions at the receiver.
why_it_matters: Knowing the teleportation-induced distortion expands usable outcomes and may improve efficiency in practical optical quantum communication.
detailed_summary: The protocol teleports an unknown optical single-rail qubit through a hybrid channel combining continuous-variable parity states with a discrete-variable mode. Its operation is governed by the squeezed-vacuum, beam-splitter, and mixing parameters, which determine the distribution of Alice's measurement outcomes and Bob's recovered state. With suitable parameter choices, Bob receives the original qubit in about half the cases, while many remaining outcomes yield qubits with known amplitude-distorting factors rather than fully unusable states. Because both parties know the distortion values, the receiver can either continue computation with the transformed state or apply recovery strategies.
main_result: The authors show that single-rail optical qubits can be teleported with a substantial fraction of outcomes either exact or carrying known amplitude distortions that remain actionable.
application_angle: The scheme can raise effective throughput in optical quantum links by converting some nominal teleportation failures into correctable or directly usable transmitted states.

title: Self-testing with untrusted random number generators
abstract: Self-testing--the attractive possibility to infer the underlying physics of a quantum device in a black-box scenario--has gained increased traction in recent years, with applications to device-independent quantum information processing. Thus far, self-testing has been done under the assumption that the settings for the requisite Bell test are chosen freely and independently of the device tested in the experiment. That is, the random number generator used to generate the settings has been assumed to have no correlations with the device tested. Here, we extend self-testing protocols beyond the independence assumption. Surprisingly, we show that all pure bipartite partially entangled states can be self-tested provided that the random number generator obeys a residual randomness constraint strictly weaker than the independence assumption. This in itself provides a semi-device-independent certification of independence between the randomness source and the device.
one_liner: Shows self-testing of all pure bipartite partially entangled states even when the Bell-setting RNG is not fully independent.
why_it_matters: It relaxes a core assumption behind device-independent certification, making black-box validation more realistic for experimental quantum networks and photonic Bell tests.
detailed_summary: The paper extends quantum self-testing beyond the standard assumption that measurement settings are chosen by a random source fully independent of the devices under test. It proves that every pure bipartite partially entangled state remains self-testable under a weaker residual-randomness condition on the setting generator. Technically, this yields a semi-device-independent certification not only of the shared state but also of the degree of independence between the randomness source and the measured devices. The result is directly relevant to Bell-type photonic experiments where strict measurement independence is difficult to guarantee.
platform_or_system: Bell-test quantum devices with imperfect RNGs
main_result: All pure bipartite partially entangled states can be self-tested even when the setting RNG is correlated with the device, provided a weaker residual-randomness constraint holds.
application_angle: This could make device-independent photonic certification and quantum communication protocols more robust to realistic imperfections in random setting generation.

title: Efficient and accurate two-qubit-gate operation in a high-connectivity transmon lattice utilizing a tunable coupling to a shared mode
abstract: Increasing connectivity and decreasing qubit-state delocalization without compromising the speed and accuracy of elementary gate operations are topical challenges in the development of large-scale superconducting quantum computers. In this theoretical work, we study a special honeycomb qubit lattice where each qubit inside a unit cell is coupled to every other one via two dedicated tunable couplers and a common central element. This results in an effective multi-mode interaction enabling tunable, on-demand, all-to-all connectivity between each qubit pair within the unit cell. We provide a thorough analysis of the unit cell, including a proposal for a novel and efficient conditional-Z gate scheme which takes advantage of the effective multi-mode coupling. We develop an experimentally viable pulse protocol for a single-step gate implementation which considerably improves the gate speed compared to the previous two-qubit-gate realizations suggested for architectures utilizing a center mode. We also show numerical results on how the presence of spectator qubits affects the average two-qubit-gate fidelity, and analyse how the multi-mode coupling structure mitigates the delocalization-induced crosstalk during simultaneous single-qubit gates within the unit cell. We also provide analytical estimates for the errors caused by relaxation and dephasing during a two-qubit-gate operation, including noise terms for the multi-mode coupling structure. Our multi-mode coupling architecture results in a good balance between increased connectivity and available parallelism, especially when several interacting unit cells form a quantum processing unit. We anticipate that the obtained results pave the way towards high-connectivity quantum processors with efficient and low-overhead quantum algorithms.
one_liner: Proposes a fast, high-fidelity CZ gate in a highly connected transmon unit cell using tunable couplers and a shared central mode.
why_it_matters: It addresses the hardware bottleneck of scaling superconducting processors by balancing strong connectivity, gate speed, and reduced crosstalk in a practical lattice design.
detailed_summary: This theoretical work analyzes a honeycomb superconducting architecture in which qubits within a unit cell interact through two tunable couplers plus a common central element, producing effective multimode interactions. The authors design a single-step conditional-Z pulse protocol that improves gate speed relative to earlier center-mode-mediated schemes while remaining experimentally plausible. They also quantify spectator-qubit effects on average two-qubit fidelity, study simultaneous single-qubit operations, and derive analytical relaxation and dephasing error estimates for the multimode setting. The architecture is positioned as a high-connectivity building block with useful parallelism for larger processors.
main_result: A single-step CZ gate scheme leveraging multimode coupling is shown to enable efficient and accurate two-qubit operations in a densely connected transmon unit cell.
application_angle: The design is relevant for scalable superconducting QPUs that need richer qubit connectivity without paying a prohibitive penalty in crosstalk or control overhead.

title: High fidelity photon-photon gates by scattering off a two-level quantum emitter
abstract: We present a scheme for implementing a high-fidelity non-linear phase shift on a photonic state. The scheme is based on repeated scattering off a two-level quantum emitter embedded in a chiral or one-sided waveguide. The waveguide is equipped with elements inducing second-order dispersion and temporal phase shifts, which effectively form a harmonic trap and confine the photon pulses to a Gaussian shape. The same quantum emitter can be used for each scattering, and thus, only one quantum emitter is needed in this scheme. To illustrate the application of our scheme for photonic quantum computing and quantum communication, we analyze the implementation of a control-Z gate and a deterministic Bell-state analyzer for photonic qubits. Through numerical optimization, we show that we can reach a control-Z gate fidelity of $\mathcal{F} \sim 99.2\%$ ($\mathcal{F} \sim 96\%$) and a success probability of $P_s \sim 99.6 \%$ ($P_s\sim 98 \%$) for a Bell-state measurement with $N=17$ ($N=5$) scatterings.
one_liner: Repeated scattering from one two-level emitter yields near-deterministic high-fidelity photonic CZ gates and Bell-state measurements.
why_it_matters: It offers a concrete route to strong optical nonlinearities with a single emitter, a key requirement for scalable photonic quantum computing and network nodes.
detailed_summary: The scheme uses repeated scattering of photonic pulses from a single two-level emitter embedded in a chiral or one-sided waveguide, with engineered second-order dispersion and temporal phase shifts to keep the pulses in a Gaussian harmonic-trap-like mode. Numerical optimization shows that the repeated interaction can implement a nonlinear phase shift strong enough for photonic logic. For 17 scatterings, the authors report a CZ-gate fidelity around 99.2% and Bell-state-measurement success probability around 99.6%, with lower-resource versions still performing strongly. The proposal is technically notable because one emitter is reused throughout, reducing hardware complexity.
platform_or_system: single emitter in a chiral waveguide
main_result: A single two-level emitter with repeated photon scattering can realize photonic CZ gates and deterministic Bell-state analysis with fidelities and success probabilities approaching 99%.
application_angle: This is directly useful for photonic processors and quantum repeater nodes that need compact, deterministic photon-photon interactions and Bell measurements.

title: Photonic nanojets as emergent free-space power flux funnels
abstract: A reduced local field model derived from full-wave electromagnetic simulations shows that photonic nanojet formation corresponds to an emergent mesoscopic funnel of propagating power flux sustained by an effective free-space transverse mode structure. This interpretation moves beyond purely geometric-optics or interference-based explanations by identifying a self-consistent redistribution of phase gradients and effective longitudinal wavenumber near the nanojet waist. The model quantitatively captures characteristic nanojet morphology, including the formation and local structure of the jet waist. It also yields a geometry-independent lower bound on the nanojet waist, linking transverse confinement to the effective axial wavenumber through an explicit trade-off. The model establishes a direct connection between full-wave Maxwell fields and a reduced free-space oscillator description, yielding new physical insight into nanojet confinement and suggesting design principles for nanojet-assisted imaging, lithography, and subwavelength field localization.
one_liner: Reframes photonic nanojets as emergent free-space power-flux funnels with a reduced model that predicts waist formation and confinement limits.
why_it_matters: It provides design rules for subwavelength optical focusing relevant to free-space photonic links, imaging, lithography, and field localization.
detailed_summary: Using a reduced local-field model derived from full-wave electromagnetic simulations, the paper interprets nanojet formation as a mesoscopic funneling of propagating power flux supported by an effective transverse free-space mode structure. This goes beyond geometric-optics and simple interference pictures by explicitly linking phase-gradient redistribution and effective longitudinal wavenumber near the jet waist. The model reproduces characteristic waist morphology and derives a geometry-independent lower bound on waist size through a confinement versus axial-wavenumber trade-off. It therefore connects full Maxwell solutions to a simpler oscillator-style description with practical predictive value.
main_result: The authors derive a reduced model that quantitatively captures nanojet waist structure and establishes a lower bound on confinement tied to effective axial wavenumber.
application_angle: The result can guide optical component design for nanojet-assisted imaging, lithography, and tightly localized free-space photonic coupling structures.

title: Entanglement distillation based on Hamiltonian dynamics
abstract: Efficient entanglement distillation is a central task in quantum information science and future quantum networks. At the core of distillation protocols are the quantum error correction and detection schemes which enhance the fidelity of entangled pairs. Conventional protocols focus on digital systems, which typically require complicated compiled circuits, high-fidelity multi-qubit operations and delicate pulse-level control that impose high demands on near-term hardware. Crucially, the leading physical platforms for quantum networks, trapped ions and neutral atoms, are governed by native many-body Hamiltonians inherently suited for analog, continuous-time evolution. Adopting these natural dynamics is simpler than engineering digital logic via delicate pulse-level control. Motivated by this experimental reality, we seek to leverage the intrinsic analog capabilities for efficient entanglement distillation. In this work, we introduce the Hamiltonian entanglement distillation protocol, which exploits the intrinsic information scrambling generated by random time evolution under native Hamiltonians. We establish a quantitative connection between output fidelity and Out-of-Time-Order Correlators, showing that efficient scrambling directly implies good distillation performance. Since generic Hamiltonians are naturally efficient scramblers, the capability for distillation is ubiquitous: almost all Hamiltonians in the Hilbert space suffice for high-fidelity distillation. Numerical simulations of representative Rydberg-atom and trapped-ion systems further confirm that robust performance could be achieved using only short-range interactions and evolution times feasible in current experiments. By avoiding the complexity of digital circuit control, our approach substantially relaxes experimental requirements, providing a scalable route to entanglement engineering on current analog quantum platforms.
one_liner: Introduces entanglement distillation driven by native Hamiltonian scrambling instead of compiled digital circuits.
why_it_matters: It lowers control complexity for near-term quantum network hardware by exploiting analog many-body dynamics already available in trapped-ion and neutral-atom systems.
detailed_summary: The work proposes Hamiltonian entanglement distillation, where random time evolution under native many-body Hamiltonians replaces circuit-heavy digital distillation routines. A central theoretical result is a quantitative link between distillation fidelity and out-of-time-order correlators, showing that efficient scrambling implies strong distillation performance. The authors argue that good distillation is generic across Hilbert space and validate the idea numerically for representative Rydberg and trapped-ion systems with short-range interactions and experimentally feasible evolution times. This makes distillation look more native to analog platforms than previously assumed.
main_result: Native Hamiltonian evolution in analog many-body systems can perform high-fidelity entanglement distillation without requiring complex digital gate sequences.
application_angle: The protocol is promising for quantum repeaters and network nodes built from analog hardware where pulse-level digital control is expensive or error-prone.

title: Feasibility of satellite-augmented global quantum repeater networks
abstract: A large scale quantum network requires the distribution of high-fidelity end-to-end entanglement. To overcome the range limitations inherent to terrestrial fiber, a leading architecture has emerged: satellite-based sources transmitting entanglement to quantum repeaters on the ground. By bridging the gap between abstract analytical frameworks and computationally heavy numerical simulations, this paper provides the first quantitative answer to the question of such a network's achievable performance with current and near-term space technology, while accounting for entanglement swapping and purification. This is achieved by integrating a detailed physical model of a satellite-to-ground link into an analytical entanglement resource estimation framework for quantum repeaters, enabling an optimization of the end-to-end entanglement rate. Our analysis, performed across leading quantum hardware platforms, shows that Low Earth Orbit satellite constellations combined with quantum repeaters employing Neutral Atom or Nitrogen and Silicon Vacancy qubits, could enable a global quantum network, distributing entanglement over distances up to 20,000 km, sufficient for connecting any two points on Earth. This work highlights the major bottlenecks in space and quantum hardware technologies, which need to be addressed, thereby guiding informed investments necessary for enabling a large scale quantum network.
one_liner: Quantifies how satellite links plus ground repeaters could distribute entanglement globally over up to 20,000 km with near-term hardware.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete systems-level benchmark for building a planet-scale quantum internet and identifies the hardware bottlenecks that matter most.
detailed_summary: The paper combines a detailed physical model of satellite-to-ground entanglement distribution with an analytical repeater-resource estimation framework that includes swapping and purification. This enables end-to-end optimization of entanglement rates for realistic hybrid satellite-repeater architectures rather than relying solely on abstract bounds or heavy numerics. Across candidate hardware platforms, the analysis finds that Low Earth Orbit constellations paired with neutral-atom or nitrogen/silicon-vacancy-based repeaters could support links up to roughly 20,000 km. The study also isolates the most important performance bottlenecks in both space and node hardware.
main_result: Low Earth Orbit satellite constellations combined with suitable ground repeaters are projected to enable global entanglement distribution over distances sufficient to connect any two points on Earth.
application_angle: This directly informs architecture choices and investment priorities for practical long-distance quantum communication infrastructure.

title: Calibration of electric fields in low-frequency off-resonant Rydberg receivers
abstract: We present results on Rydberg atom-based electric field sensing in the range of 1 kHz - 300 MHz, using a three-photon Rydberg excitation scheme and a transverse electromagnetic (TEM) line waveguide to apply low-frequency rf fields to the cell. Measurements of low-frequency screening in quartz and sapphire vapor cells show excellent agreement with a phenomenological model of the effective vapor cell material properties based on an electrical 2-port measurement of the TEM line. We achieve a best noise-equivalent field of 106(4) $\mathrm{\frac{μV}{m \sqrt{Hz}}}$ at 300 MHz and characterize noise-equivalent fields in the ultra-low to very-low frequency (ULF-VLF) band.
one_liner: Rydberg-atom receivers are calibrated for 1 kHz-300 MHz electric-field sensing with sub-mV/m/sqrt(Hz) sensitivity at 300 MHz.
why_it_matters: It advances traceable, broadband atom-based RF metrology and clarifies how vapor-cell materials screen low-frequency fields in practical sensors.
detailed_summary: The work measures electric fields from 1 kHz to 300 MHz using a three-photon Rydberg excitation scheme and a TEM-line waveguide that applies calibrated low-frequency RF fields to the vapor cell. Screening in quartz and sapphire cells is characterized and matched to a phenomenological effective-material model informed by electrical two-port measurements of the line. The authors report a best noise-equivalent field of 106(4) uV/m/sqrt(Hz) at 300 MHz and map performance across the ULF-VLF regime.
main_result: The authors calibrate low-frequency off-resonant Rydberg receivers and achieve a best noise-equivalent field of 106(4) uV/m/sqrt(Hz) at 300 MHz.
application_angle: The method supports deployable broadband electric-field probes for RF sensing, calibration, and low-frequency electromagnetic monitoring.

title: Quantum Hypergraph States: A Review
abstract: Quantum hypergraph states extend the well-studied class of graph states by taking into account multi-qubit interactions through hyperedges. They provide a powerful framework to represent a family of quantum states with genuine multipartite entanglement. In this review, we provide a compact overview of the formal structure, entanglement characteristics, and operational relevance of hypergraph states in quantum information theory. We begin by introducing their mathematical foundations and generalizations of the stabilizer formalism. A central focus is placed on their entanglement properties, including the classification under local unitary (LU) and stochastic local operations with classical communication (SLOCC), the quantification of multipartite entanglement, and detection techniques via entanglement witnesses. We also explore other nonclassical features of hypergraph states, such as contextuality and genuine multipartite nonlocality, derived from stabilizer-based Bell-type inequalities. Additional attention is given to the role of hypergraph states in error correction, and as a computational resource in measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC), and to their non-stabilizer character - quantified via resource-theoretic measures of quantum magic. Finally we review their generalization to higher dimensions, i.e. to qudits and continuous variables.
one_liner: This review organizes the theory and operational roles of quantum hypergraph states as multipartite resources beyond ordinary graph states.
why_it_matters: It consolidates a useful state class for entanglement, nonlocality, MBQC, and error-correction research relevant to future quantum architectures.
detailed_summary: The review surveys the formal structure of hypergraph states, including generalized stabilizer descriptions and extensions beyond standard graph states through multi-qubit hyperedges. It summarizes LU and SLOCC classifications, entanglement witnesses, contextuality, Bell-type nonlocality tests, and resource-theoretic notions such as quantum magic. It also covers applications to error correction, measurement-based computation, and generalizations to qudits and continuous variables.
main_result: The paper provides a compact technical synthesis of hypergraph-state structure, nonclassical properties, and operational uses across quantum information tasks.
application_angle: It gives hardware and algorithm designers a clearer map of when hypergraph states may serve as useful resources for scalable computation and protected information processing.

title: Hybridlane: A Software Development Kit for Hybrid Continuous-Discrete Variable Quantum Computing
abstract: Hybrid quantum computing systems that combine discrete-variable qubits with continuous-variable qumodes offer promising advantages for quantum simulation, error correction, and sensing applications. However, existing quantum software frameworks lack native support for expressing and manipulating hybrid circuits, forcing developers to work with fragmented toolchains or rely on simulation-coupled representations that limit scalability. We present Hybridlane, an open-source software development kit providing a unified frontend for hybrid continuous-discrete variable quantum computing. Hybridlane introduces automatic wire type inference to distinguish qubits from qumodes without manual annotations, enabling compile-time validation of circuit correctness. By decoupling gate semantics from matrix representations, Hybridlane can describe wide and deep circuits with minimal memory consumption and without requiring simulation. The framework implements a comprehensive library of hybrid gates and decompositions following established instruction set architectures, while remaining compatible with PennyLane's extensive qubit algorithm library. Furthermore, it supports multiple backends including classical simulation with Bosonic Qiskit and hardware compilation to Sandia National Laboratories' QSCOUT ion trap. We demonstrate Hybridlane's capabilities through bosonic quantum phase estimation and ion trap calibration workflows.
one_liner: Hybridlane introduces a unified SDK for circuits mixing qubits and qumodes, with validation, decompositions, and hardware/backend support.
why_it_matters: It lowers a practical software barrier for hybrid CV-DV quantum workflows in simulation, sensing, and hardware compilation.
detailed_summary: Hybridlane provides a frontend for hybrid continuous-variable and discrete-variable quantum computing with automatic wire-type inference and compile-time circuit checks. By separating gate semantics from explicit matrix representations, it can express large hybrid circuits without simulation-heavy memory costs. The package includes hybrid gate libraries and decompositions, interoperates with PennyLane, and targets both Bosonic Qiskit simulation and Sandia's QSCOUT ion-trap hardware. Demonstrations include bosonic phase estimation and ion-trap calibration workflows.
main_result: The authors deliver an open-source SDK that natively represents, validates, and compiles hybrid CV-DV circuits across simulation and trapped-ion hardware backends.
application_angle: This can accelerate development of hybrid protocols for quantum sensing, bosonic algorithms, and hardware calibration without fragmented toolchains.

title: Universality of Classically Trainable, Quantum-Deployed Boson-Sampling Generative Models
abstract: Recent work on the instantaneous quantum polynomial-time (IQP) quantum-circuit Born machine (QCBM) highlights a promising paradigm for generative modeling: train classically, deploy quantumly. In this setting, the training objective can be evaluated efficiently on a classical computer, while sampling from the resulting model may still be classically intractable. Furthermore, in the IQP-QCBM framework, extending the model family with ancillary qubits has been proven to yield universality. This paper asks whether similar results hold for linear-optical generative models. To this end, we introduce the Boson Sampling Born Machine (BSBM). Our analysis retraces analogous steps as were found for IQP-QCBMs with twists. Using recent results that enable classical approximation of broad classes of expectation values in linear optics, we show that BSBMs can be trained classically for wide families of loss functions. Next, we argue that "basic" BSBMs are not universal generative models, and that universality can be achieved by expanding the model while preserving efficient classical training and sampling hardness. In our approach, we introduce and analyze the role of constant-function postprocessing, generalizing the construction for IQP-QCBMs, which under suitable conditions can lead to universality while preserving the hardness of classically simulating the models. We showcase a family of BSBMs, characterized by a single hyperparameter, that allows for a monotonic increase in expressivity toward universality while retaining the capacity to represent ostensibly hard distributions. Furthermore, we discuss the possible modalities for the efficient classical training, in the sense of efficient estimation of gradients of the loss function.
one_liner: A boson-sampling Born machine is shown to support classical training while reaching universal generative modeling through an expanded architecture.
why_it_matters: It sharpens the case for photonic generative models that remain trainable in practice yet retain hard-to-simulate quantum sampling behavior.
detailed_summary: The paper defines the Boson Sampling Born Machine as a linear-optical analogue of classically trainable, quantum-deployed Born models. It argues that basic BSBMs are not universal, then shows universality can be recovered by extending the model class while preserving efficient classical evaluation of broad training objectives and retaining sampling hardness. A one-parameter family is presented that interpolates toward universality, and the authors discuss efficient gradient-estimation modalities for training.
main_result: The main result is that expanded boson-sampling Born machines can be both classically trainable for broad losses and universal as generative models while keeping hard quantum sampling structure.
application_angle: This provides a concrete route toward useful photonic quantum machine-learning models that may be trainable on classical resources and deployed on optical hardware.

title: Mitigating crosstalk errors for simultaneous single-qubit gates on a superconducting quantum processor
abstract: Single-qubit gates on superconducting quantum processors are typically implemented using microwave pulses applied through dedicated control lines. However, these microwave pulses may also drive other qubits due to crosstalk arising from capacitive coupling and wavefunction overlap in systems with closely spaced transition frequencies. Crosstalk and frequency crowding increase errors during simultaneous single-qubit operations relative to isolated gates, thus forming a major bottleneck for scaling superconducting quantum processors. In this work, we combine model-based qubit frequency optimization with pulse shaping to demonstrate crosstalk error mitigation in single-qubit gates on a 49-qubit superconducting quantum processor. We introduce and experimentally verify an analytical model of simultaneous single-qubit gate error caused by microwave crosstalk that depends on a given pulse shape. By employing a model-based optimization strategy of qubit frequencies, we minimize the crosstalk-induced error across the processor and achieve a mean simultaneous single-qubit gate fidelity of 99.96% for a 16-ns gate duration, approaching the mean individual gate fidelity. To further reduce the simultaneous error and required qubit frequency bandwidth on high-crosstalk qubit pairs, we introduce a crosstalk transition suppression (CTS) pulse shaping technique that minimizes the spectral energy around transitions inducing leakage and crosstalk errors. Finally, we combine CTS with model-based frequency optimization across the device and experimentally show a systematic reduction in the required qubit frequency bandwidth for high-fidelity simultaneous gates, supported by simulations of systems with up to 1000 qubits. By alleviating constraints on qubit frequency bandwidth for parallel single-qubit operations, this work represents an important step for scaling towards larger quantum processors.
one_liner: A 49-qubit superconducting processor reaches 99.96% mean simultaneous single-qubit gate fidelity using frequency optimization and crosstalk-suppressing pulses.
why_it_matters: It addresses a central scaling bottleneck in superconducting hardware by making parallel control more accurate and less bandwidth-hungry.
detailed_summary: The authors build and experimentally validate an analytical error model for simultaneous single-qubit gates under microwave crosstalk on a 49-qubit superconducting chip. Using model-based qubit-frequency allocation, they achieve a mean simultaneous gate fidelity of 99.96% for 16 ns gates, close to isolated-gate performance. They further introduce crosstalk transition suppression pulse shaping to reduce spectral weight near harmful transitions, lowering bandwidth demands for high-fidelity parallel control. Simulations indicate the approach remains relevant up to 1000-qubit-scale systems.
main_result: The work demonstrates near-individual-fidelity simultaneous single-qubit gates and reduced frequency-bandwidth requirements through combined frequency optimization and CTS pulse shaping.
application_angle: Improved parallel gate operation directly supports larger superconducting processors with less frequency crowding and better control scalability.

title: Rhenium as a material platform for long-lived transmon qubits
abstract: Dielectric loss at the interfaces of superconducting films has long been recognized as limiting the performance of state-of-the-art superconducting circuits. Notably, the presence of a native oxide layer on the film is hypothesized to contribute to dielectric loss at the metal-air interface. Here, we explore rhenium as a candidate for the film, motivated by its remarkable property to suppress native oxide formation. We demonstrate rhenium on sapphire as a promising material platform for superconducting circuits through the realization of transmons with mean relaxation times $T_1$ up to 407 microseconds at 5 GHz. Our transmons are supplemented with a loss characterization study, in which we separate the dominant loss mechanisms and construct a loss budget that agrees with our $T_1$ measurements. Further characterization may establish rhenium as a leading candidate for maximizing decoherence time.
one_liner: Transmons made from rhenium on sapphire reach mean T1 values up to 407 microseconds, highlighting a low-loss materials platform.
why_it_matters: It points to a practical route for reducing interface dielectric loss by suppressing native oxide formation in superconducting qubits.
detailed_summary: This study explores rhenium films on sapphire as a superconducting-circuit material motivated by rhenium's suppressed native-oxide formation. The authors fabricate transmons with mean relaxation times up to 407 us at 5 GHz and pair device data with a loss-budget analysis that separates dominant dissipation channels. The measured performance is consistent with the constructed loss model, supporting the claim that interface loss is reduced on this platform.
main_result: The paper demonstrates rhenium-based transmons with mean T1 up to 407 microseconds and a loss budget consistent with reduced interface dielectric loss.
application_angle: If the materials stack proves reproducible, it could improve coherence in superconducting processors without requiring major architectural changes.

title: Exact quantum scars of frustrated hardcore bosons for cross-platform realizations
abstract: Quantum many-body scars are nonthermal states exhibiting persistent revivals in an otherwise ergodic, nonintegrable quantum system. Existing examples of exact quantum scars, however, have not yet been amenable to direct experimental demonstration. Here we show that a minimal model of hardcore bosons hopping on a $π$-flux ladder is sufficient to give rise to an exact scar due to kinetic frustration. The simplicity of this model makes it suitable for multiple existing quantum simulation platforms, which we illustrate with proposals for cold atom Bose-Hubbard simulators and polar molecule or Rydberg atom tweezer arrays. In these platforms, the scar lifetime can be extended by tuning experimentally accessible parameters, like the Hubbard interaction or a Floquet drive. Finally, we introduce a practical heuristic based on the energy distribution of eigenstates for systematically predicting and optimizing quantum many-body scar lifetimes. Their cross-platform realizability and long lifetimes make them well-suited for benchmarking coherence and exploring nonergodic dynamics in current and near-term quantum devices.
one_liner: A pi-flux hardcore-boson ladder hosts exact many-body scar states in a minimal model suited to several current quantum simulators.
why_it_matters: It offers an experimentally accessible scar benchmark across atoms, Rydbergs, and molecules, with knobs to prolong revivals and probe nonergodic dynamics.
detailed_summary: The paper identifies exact quantum many-body scars in a minimal model of hardcore bosons hopping on a frustrated pi-flux ladder, where kinetic frustration stabilizes nonthermal eigenstates inside an otherwise ergodic spectrum. It maps the model onto realistic Bose-Hubbard, polar-molecule, and Rydberg-tweezer implementations, emphasizing experimentally tunable controls such as interaction strength and Floquet driving to extend scar lifetimes. The authors also propose an eigenstate-energy-distribution heuristic to predict and optimize revival quality in practical devices.
platform_or_system: Frustrated hardcore bosons on a pi-flux ladder
main_result: The authors show that a simple pi-flux ladder model supports exact scars with long-lived revivals and can be realized across multiple existing quantum-simulation platforms.
application_angle: The model provides a near-term hardware benchmark for coherence, control calibration, and controlled studies of nonergodic dynamics on analog quantum simulators.

title: Generative modeling with Gaussian Boson Sampling: classically trainable Bosonic Born Machines
abstract: Quantum generative modeling has emerged as a promising application of quantum computers, aiming to model complex probability distributions beyond the reach of classical methods. In practice, however, training such models often requires costly gradient estimation performed directly on the quantum hardware. Crucially, for certain structured quantum circuits, expectation values of local observables can be efficiently evaluated on a classical computer, enabling classical training without calls to the quantum hardware in the optimization loop. In these models, sampling from the resulting circuits can still be classically hard, so inference must be performed on a quantum device, yielding a potential computational advantage. In this work, we introduce a photonic quantum generative model built on parametrized Gaussian Boson Sampling circuits. The training is based on the efficient classical evaluation of expectation values enabled by the Gaussian structure of the state, allowing scalable optimization of the model parameters through the maximum mean discrepancy loss function. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach through numerical experiments on photonic systems with up to 805 modes and over a million trainable parameters, highlighting its scalability and suitability for near-term photonic quantum devices.
one_liner: A Gaussian-boson-sampling Born machine enables classical training while retaining quantum sampling for inference on large photonic circuits.
why_it_matters: It narrows the trainability bottleneck in photonic generative models by moving optimization off hardware while preserving a path to quantum advantage at inference.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a bosonic Born machine based on parametrized Gaussian Boson Sampling circuits, where the Gaussian structure allows efficient classical evaluation of local observables during training. Model parameters are optimized with a maximum mean discrepancy objective, avoiding repeated hardware-in-the-loop gradient estimation. Numerical demonstrations scale to 805 optical modes and more than one million trainable parameters, while inference still relies on sampling from the photonic quantum device.
main_result: The paper demonstrates scalable classical training of a photonic quantum generative model on GBS circuits up to 805 modes and over 10^6 parameters.
application_angle: This approach is practical for near-term photonic processors because expensive optimization can be done classically, leaving only hard sampling tasks to the quantum hardware.

title: Kraus map closed-form solution for general master equation dynamics
abstract: The Kraus representation of quantum channels allows for a precise emulation of the complex dynamics that take place on quantum processors, whether for benchmarking algorithms, predicting the performance of error correction and mitigation, or in the myriad other uses of compiled digital sequences. Nonetheless, starting from first principles to obtain continuous quantum master equations involves various approximations such as weak coupling to the environment. Further, converting these equations to Kraus operators cannot generally be obtained in closed-form due to the complicated commutator structure of the problem. In our work, we bridge this gap by providing a general closed form formulation for arbitrarily strong driving while remaining linear in the dissipator. The Kraus solution is expressed as a Riemann sum where higher terms can converge quickly to high precision, which we demonstrate numerically. Such a formulation is highly relevant to quantum computing and gate-based models, where effective models are highly sought for large rotation gate angles, even under the influence of underlying non-trivial noise mechanisms.
one_liner: A closed-form Kraus-map construction converts general driven master-equation dynamics into quantum-channel representations with fast converging sums.
why_it_matters: It gives hardware researchers a more direct way to model noisy gates under strong driving, improving simulation, benchmarking, and error-analysis workflows.
detailed_summary: The authors derive a general closed-form Kraus representation for dynamics generated by master equations under arbitrarily strong driving while remaining linear in the dissipative part. The solution is expressed as a Riemann-sum expansion whose higher-order terms can converge rapidly to high precision. This bridges the gap between first-principles open-system dynamics and channel-level models needed for digital quantum computing, especially beyond weak-drive approximations.
main_result: The main result is a closed-form Kraus-map formulation for broad master-equation dynamics that remains applicable under strong control driving.
application_angle: The method can improve realistic gate-noise compilation, emulator fidelity, and evaluation of error-correction or mitigation protocols on quantum processors.

title: Infinite Distance Extrapolation: How error mitigation can enhance quantum error correction
abstract: Quantum error mitigation (QEM) and quantum error correction (QEC) are two research areas that are often considered as distinct entities, and the problem of combining the two approaches in a non-trivial way has only recently started to be explored. In this paper, we explore a paradigm at the intersection of the two, based on the error mitigation technique of Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE), that uses the distance of an error correcting code as a noise parameter. This is distinct from some alternative approaches, as QEC is here used as a subroutine inside the QEM framework, while other proposals use QEM as a subroutine inside QEC experiments. Intuitively, we exploit the fact that a reduction in the physical noise level is analogous to an increase in the code distance, as both of them result in a decrease in the logical error rate. As such, the extrapolation to zero noise in the case of ZNE becomes comparable to the extrapolation to infinite distance in the case of this method. We describe how to calculate expectation values from a fault-tolerant computation, and we gain some analytical intuition for our ansatz choice. We explore the performance of the considered method to reduce the errors in a range of expectation values for a realistic circuit-level noise model and realistic device imperfections on the rotated surface code, and we particularly show that the performance of the method holds even in the case of non-stabiliser input states.
one_liner: Infinite-distance extrapolation uses code distance as a zero-noise-extrapolation parameter to boost fault-tolerant expectation-value estimation.
why_it_matters: It shows a concrete way to combine mitigation and correction so logical observables improve without waiting for arbitrarily large codes.
detailed_summary: This paper reframes zero-noise extrapolation in the language of quantum error correction by treating increasing code distance as the effective reduction of noise, enabling extrapolation to infinite distance. The method is analyzed for rotated surface-code fault-tolerant computations under realistic circuit-level noise and device imperfections. The authors report improved expectation values across several settings, including non-stabilizer input states, and provide analytical guidance for the extrapolation ansatz.
main_result: The work shows that extrapolating in code distance can reduce logical observable errors in realistic surface-code simulations, even for non-stabilizer inputs.
application_angle: This could lower the practical overhead of useful fault-tolerant computations by extracting better logical results from modest-distance codes.

title: QuaNTUM: A Modular Quantum Communication Testbed for Scalable Fiber and Satellite Integration
abstract: Secure communication is essential for modern society, from financial transactions to critical infrastructure. As classical encryption faces threats from advancing computational power, quantum communication provides a fundamentally secure alternative based on physical laws. We present QuaNTUM (Quantum Network at the Technical University of Munich), a modular and extensible quantum communication testbed enabling scalable experiments across fiber-based campus networks and satellite-ground links. The terrestrial network connects research institutions in Garching near Munich via single-mode fibers in a star topology with polarization-maintaining components, multiplexers, and time-synchronized analysis modules. Active polarization control and real-time feedback support stable qubit transmission for high-fidelity quantum key distribution and entanglement distribution. A key feature is the integration of deterministic solid-state single-photon sources, including defects in hexagonal boron nitride and excited erbium atoms, with initial deployments on small satellites to bridge terrestrial and free-space channels. As an open-access platform, QuaNTUM enables protocol development, device benchmarking, and hybrid network research, providing a foundation for scalable quantum communication and future global quantum networks.
one_liner: QuaNTUM is a modular testbed linking fiber campus networks and satellite-ground channels for scalable quantum communication experiments.
why_it_matters: It provides a realistic integration platform for QKD, entanglement distribution, source benchmarking, and hybrid terrestrial-space network development.
detailed_summary: The platform connects research sites in Garching with single-mode fiber links in a star topology using polarization-maintaining components, multiplexing, synchronized analysis modules, and active polarization feedback. It is designed to support stable qubit transmission, high-fidelity QKD, and entanglement-distribution experiments across both terrestrial and satellite-ground channels. A notable feature is planned integration of deterministic solid-state single-photon emitters, including hBN defects and excited erbium atoms, with initial small-satellite deployment paths.
platform_or_system: Hybrid fiber and satellite quantum network testbed
main_result: The paper presents an extensible open-access quantum communication testbed that integrates campus fiber infrastructure with future satellite links and solid-state photon sources.
application_angle: It directly supports deployment-focused testing of components and protocols needed for scalable metro-to-space quantum networks.

title: Saturable absorption in diamond nanophotonics
abstract: Diamond is a leading quantum photonics platform due to its ability to host qubits based on crystal defects such as nitrogen vacancy centres. Fabricating nanophotonic devices from defect-rich diamond, which is central to many quantum sensing technologies, promises to enable enhanced performance and integrability of diamond quantum sensors. Here we demonstrate microdisk cavities fabricated from defect-rich diamond that support optical modes with high quality factor ($Q\sim7\times10^4$ at 1042 nm), and show that they exhibit saturable absorption. Power dependent spectroscopy measurements spanning 979 nm to 1604 nm are used to extract wavelength-dependent absorption coefficients and saturation intensities, which indicate that a hydrogen-related defect is a likely origin of the observed absorption. At 1047 nm, we measure a saturation intensity of 3.3 (1) MW/cm$^2$ and an absorption coefficient of 0.537 (4) cm$^{-1}$. These results provide insight into defect-mediated optical loss in diamond nanophotonics and suggest strategies to harness defect-induced nonlinearities in future diamond photonic devices.
one_liner: Defect-rich diamond microdisk cavities show measurable saturable absorption, exposing nonlinear loss channels relevant to diamond quantum devices.
why_it_matters: Understanding and engineering this defect-mediated absorption is important for improving diamond nanophotonic sensors and exploiting useful optical nonlinearities.
detailed_summary: The authors fabricate defect-rich diamond microdisk cavities with quality factors around 7x10^4 near 1042 nm and observe clear power-dependent saturable absorption. Spectroscopy from 979 nm to 1604 nm yields wavelength-dependent absorption coefficients and saturation intensities, with the data suggesting a hydrogen-related defect origin. At 1047 nm they extract a saturation intensity of 3.3 MW/cm^2 and an absorption coefficient of 0.537 cm^-1, providing quantitative guidance for modeling loss and nonlinearity in diamond photonic structures.
main_result: The study experimentally demonstrates saturable absorption in high-Q diamond nanophotonic microdisks and quantifies its spectral strength and likely defect origin.
application_angle: These measurements inform the design of integrated diamond quantum sensors and may enable purposeful use of defect-induced nonlinear optics in future devices.

title: Miniaturized microscopes to study neural dynamics in freely-behaving animals
abstract: Head-mounted miniaturized microscopes, commonly known as miniscopes, have undergone rapid development and seen widespread adoption over the past two decades, enabling the imaging of neural activity in freely-behaving animals such as rodents, songbirds, and non-human primates. These miniscopes facilitate numerous studies that are not feasible with head-fixed preparations. Recent advancements have enhanced their capabilities, allowing for faster imaging, larger fields of view, and deeper brain penetration. In this review, we examine the latest progress in one-photon and multi-photon miniscopes. We highlight the unique opportunities these devices present for neuroscience research, discuss the current technical challenges, and explore emerging technologies that promise to advance the development of miniscopes.
one_liner: This review surveys one- and multiphoton miniscopes that enable neural imaging in freely behaving animals with improving speed, field of view, and depth.
why_it_matters: Miniaturized optical microscopes expand in vivo brain measurement beyond head-fixed setups, opening more realistic behavioral neuroscience experiments and translational sensing tools.
detailed_summary: This review summarizes the state of head-mounted miniscopes for recording neural dynamics in freely behaving rodents, songbirds, and non-human primates. It compares one-photon and multiphoton architectures, emphasizing technical progress in imaging speed, field of view, and penetration depth. The paper also outlines current engineering bottlenecks and emerging optical technologies likely to shape next-generation miniature neural imaging systems.
main_result: The paper consolidates recent technical advances showing that modern miniscopes can achieve faster, wider-field, and deeper neural imaging in freely moving animals.
application_angle: These systems are directly relevant to portable in vivo biosensing and neuroscience experiments that require natural behavior rather than head-fixed conditions.

title: Impact of Oxygen Vacancies in Josephson Junction on Decoherence of Superconducting Qubits
abstract: Superconducting quantum circuits are promising platforms for scalable quantum computing, where qubit coherence is critically determined by microscopic defects in the oxide tunneling barrier of Josephson junctions. Amorphous Al$_2$O$_3$ is widely used as a barrier material, but under irradiation, oxygen vacancy (V$_O$) defects are readily generated, introducing noise sources that accelerate qubit decoherence. We systematically investigate the structural characteristics and electronic impact of V$_O$ defects in amorphous Al$_2$O$_3$ using first-principles calculations and \textit{ab initio} molecular dynamics. Our results show that both the coordination environment and concentration of V$_O$s strongly influence electrical conductivity. In particular, two- and three-coordinated V$_O$s, unique to the amorphous structure, enhance conductivity more than conventional four-coordinated vacancies. Increasing V$_O$ concentration amplifies conductivity fluctuations, which we link to critical current noise in Josephson junctions. Using a noise model, we estimate that higher V$_O$ densities lead to shorter qubit coherence times. These findings provide insights for radiation-hard design of superconducting quantum devices.
one_liner: First-principles modeling links oxygen vacancies in amorphous Al2O3 Josephson-junction barriers to conductivity noise and reduced superconducting-qubit coherence.
why_it_matters: It identifies a microscopic decoherence pathway in a key quantum-hardware material and informs radiation-hard fabrication strategies for more stable superconducting qubits.
detailed_summary: Using first-principles calculations and ab initio molecular dynamics, the authors study oxygen-vacancy defects in amorphous Al2O3 tunnel barriers used in Josephson junctions. They find that vacancy coordination and density strongly modulate electrical conductivity, with two- and three-coordinated vacancies causing stronger effects than conventional four-coordinated defects. A noise model connects the resulting conductivity fluctuations to critical-current noise and predicts shorter qubit coherence times at higher vacancy densities.
platform_or_system: Superconducting qubits with Al2O3 Josephson junctions
main_result: The study shows that specific oxygen-vacancy configurations in amorphous Al2O3 increase conductivity fluctuations and thereby accelerate superconducting-qubit decoherence.
application_angle: The results guide materials engineering and radiation-hard process design for higher-coherence superconducting quantum processors.

title: Quantum photonic frequency processor on thin-film lithium niobate
abstract: The rapid development of photonic quantum information processing necessitates precise and programmable control over optical frequency, a capability critical not only for achieving photon indistinguishability but also for exploiting a virtually unbounded frequency dimension. However, efficient and scalable processing of frequency-encoded photon states remains challenging, primarily due to the limited nonlinear optical interaction in most photonic materials. Here, by harnessing the high-performance thin-film lithium niobate electro-optic (EO) platform, we demonstrate an integrated quantum photonic frequency processor that enables coherent and programmable control of photon frequency with high precision. We establish a scalable architecture for frequency-encoded quantum information processing. Using a fully integrated photonic chip, we realize a universal set of frequency-encoded quantum logic gates, including arbitrary single-qubit rotation gates and the two-qubit controlled-phase gate. Furthermore, we demonstrate its application in high fidelity characterization of frequency-bin entangled states. Our work reveals the unprecedented potential of utilizing the frequency degree of freedom in integrated quantum photonic systems.
one_liner: An integrated thin-film lithium niobate chip performs programmable quantum frequency processing, including universal frequency-bin gates and entangled-state characterization.
why_it_matters: It moves frequency-encoded photonic quantum processing toward scalable, chip-integrated hardware with precise control over an otherwise underused high-dimensional degree of freedom.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates an electro-optic quantum frequency processor on thin-film lithium niobate for coherent, programmable manipulation of photon frequency states. On a fully integrated chip, the authors implement a universal gate set for frequency encoding, including arbitrary single-qubit rotations and a two-qubit controlled-phase gate. They also use the processor for high-fidelity characterization of frequency-bin entangled states, establishing a scalable architecture for frequency-domain quantum photonics.
platform_or_system: Thin-film lithium niobate integrated photonic chip
main_result: The device realizes universal frequency-encoded quantum logic and high-fidelity frequency-bin entanglement characterization on an integrated EO photonic platform.
application_angle: This supports compact quantum photonic processors and measurement systems for communications, networking, and spectrally multiplexed quantum sensing.

title: Reliability Dynamics in a Two-Site Dissipative Quantum Spin Chain
abstract: As a key index for applications of a device, the device's reliability is its ability to survive (function normally over time) under the influence of some environment. In this paper we present a quantum energy-storing device model with a quantum spin chain, whose environment influence is described by the Lindblad master equation. Here the device survives if the spin system stays in the state with nonzero excitations; otherwise, it fails. Because the Lindblad dynamics enforces one-way energy decay and strict irreversibility of the failure state, we can investigate the reliability of the quantum device directly using classical reliability theory. Focusing on the minimal nontrivial case -- a two-site spin-1/2 chain -- we derive closed-form expressions for the reliability and the hazard rate. The dynamics exhibit an overdamped-underdamped crossover controlled by the competition between coherent exchange and dissipation inhomogeneity. The exact analytical formulas are in excellent agreement with numerical simulations. More importantly, we establish an experimentally accessible protocol for assessing reliability based on first-passage time statistics.
one_liner: An exactly solvable two-site dissipative spin-chain model maps quantum-device survival to classical reliability metrics and proposes first-passage-based assessment.
why_it_matters: It gives a tractable framework for quantifying operational lifetime in open quantum devices, which is important for benchmarking practical quantum components under loss.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze a two-site spin-1/2 chain as a minimal quantum energy-storing device governed by a Lindblad master equation. Defining failure as reaching the zero-excitation state, they derive closed-form expressions for reliability and hazard rate using classical reliability theory enabled by irreversible decay. The model exhibits an overdamped-to-underdamped crossover set by the competition between coherent exchange and dissipation asymmetry, and the analytic formulas match numerical simulations. They further propose an experimentally accessible reliability assessment based on first-passage-time statistics.
main_result: The work derives exact reliability and hazard-rate formulas for a minimal open quantum device and connects them to measurable first-passage-time statistics.
application_angle: It provides a practical framework for lifetime and failure analysis of dissipative quantum hardware where loss and irreversibility dominate performance.

title: Unidirectional exceptional point of reflectionless states in a magnonic mirror array
abstract: Exceptional points (EPs) in non-Hermitian systems are singularities where both eigenvalues and eigenvectors coalesce. In scattering systems, EPs correspond to the merging of scattering states, leading to reflectionless (RL) behavior. A reflectionless exceptional point (RL EP) arises when two RL states further coalesce, yielding an anomalous quartic spectral response. While RL EPs have been explored in bidirectional systems, their unidirectional realization remains elusive. Here, we experimentally demonstrate a unidirectional RL EP by engineering collective states in an anti-Bragg magnonic mirror array. Inversion symmetry is broken using a giant spin ensemble that couples to a waveguide at three spatially separated points, enabling unidirectional reflectionless. At the RL EP, the reflection spectrum flattens and broadens significantly beyond the Lorentzian profile. The observed spectral valleys also expose dark-state behaviors that are typically inaccessible through conventional measurements. Our results provide a route toward controlling collective coherence in open systems and developing broadband unidirectional devices.
one_liner: A magnonic mirror array experimentally realizes a unidirectional reflectionless exceptional point with broadened, flattened reflection spectra.
why_it_matters: This demonstrates non-Hermitian wave control in a hardware platform suited for broadband directional devices and deeper access to collective dark-state physics.
detailed_summary: The experiment engineers a unidirectional reflectionless exceptional point in an anti-Bragg magnonic mirror array by breaking inversion symmetry with a giant spin ensemble coupled to a waveguide at three separated points. At the reflectionless exceptional point, two reflectionless states coalesce, producing a quartic spectral response and a broadened, flattened reflection profile beyond a Lorentzian line shape. The measured spectral valleys also reveal dark-state behavior that conventional probes typically miss.
platform_or_system: Anti-Bragg magnonic mirror array with giant spin ensemble
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate a unidirectional reflectionless exceptional point and observe its distinctive broadened quartic reflection spectrum.
application_angle: The result is relevant to broadband nonreciprocal or directional wave-control components and tunable open-system hardware for signal routing.

title: Back-Action-Evading Measurements and Quantum Non-Demolition Variables via Linear Systems Engineering
abstract: We establish a framework for realizing back-action-evading (BAE) measurements and quantum non-demolition (QND) variables in linear quantum systems. The key condition, a purely imaginary Hamiltonian with a real or imaginary coupling operator, enables BAE measurements of conjugate observables. Symmetric coupling further yields QND variables. For non-compliant systems, coherent feedback can engineer BAE measurements. Crucially, the QND interaction condition simultaneously ensures BAE measurements and promotes the coupling operator to a QND observable. This work provides a unified structural theory for enhancing precision in quantum metrology and sensing.
one_liner: This theory establishes structural conditions for back-action-evading measurements and QND variables in linear quantum systems, including feedback-based engineering.
why_it_matters: It provides a general design rule for suppressing measurement back-action, a central requirement for precision quantum sensing and metrology.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a unified framework for realizing back-action-evading measurements and quantum non-demolition observables in linear quantum systems. It identifies a key structural condition: a purely imaginary Hamiltonian combined with a real or imaginary coupling operator enables BAE measurement of conjugate observables, while symmetric coupling yields QND variables. For systems that do not naturally satisfy these constraints, the authors show how coherent feedback can engineer the required dynamics, and they prove that the QND interaction condition also guarantees BAE behavior.
main_result: The work derives general Hamiltonian and coupling conditions under which linear quantum systems support BAE measurements and QND observables, with coherent-feedback extensions.
application_angle: These rules can be used to design lower-noise readout architectures for quantum sensors, force detectors, and precision metrology platforms.

title: Quantum Error Correction by Purification
abstract: We present a general-purpose quantum error correction primitive based on state purification via the SWAP test, which we refer to as purification quantum error correction (PQEC). This method operates on $N$ noisy copies, requires minimally $O(M\log_2 N)$ data qubits to process the $M$-qubit inputs. In a similar way to standard QEC, the purification steps may be interleaved within a quantum algorithm to suppress the logical error rate. No postselection is performed and no knowledge of the state is required. We analyze its performance under a variety of error channels and find that PQEC is highly effective at boosting fidelity and reducing logical error rates, particularly for the depolarizing channel. Error thresholds for the local depolarizing channel are found to be $ 75 \%$ for any register size. For local dephasing, the error threshold is reduced to $ 50 \% $ but may be boosted using twirling.
one_liner: PQEC uses SWAP-test-based purification on multiple noisy copies to suppress logical errors without postselection or state knowledge.
why_it_matters: It proposes a hardware-agnostic error-correction primitive with high noise thresholds, offering a potentially lighter-weight route to improving logical fidelity.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces purification quantum error correction, a general primitive that consumes N noisy copies of an M-qubit state and performs purification steps using SWAP-test-style comparisons. The scheme requires only O(M log2 N) data qubits, does not rely on postselection, and can be interleaved inside a larger quantum algorithm. Across several noise models, the authors find strong fidelity and logical-error suppression, with a 75% threshold for local depolarizing noise independent of register size and a 50% threshold for local dephasing that can be improved by twirling.
main_result: The method achieves strong logical-error suppression with reported thresholds of 75% for local depolarizing noise and 50% for local dephasing.
application_angle: If implementable efficiently, PQEC could complement conventional codes as a lower-overhead error-mitigation or correction layer in near- and mid-term quantum processors.

title: Subtime: Reversible Information Exchange and the Emergence of Classical Time
abstract: We formalize the concept of subtime -- a reversible mode of information interchange within entangled systems -- and show how classical time emerges as an asymptotic limit through decoherence. Building on the photon clock model, in which a single photon confined between two ideal mirrors creates an alternating causality regime, we develop a process-theoretic formalization using the Oreshkov--Costa--Brukner framework extended with an explicit time-reversal duality condition. We introduce Perfect Information Feedback (PIF) as the information-theoretic realization of this reversibility, demonstrating that mutual information is conserved in any closed causal loop and that entropy quantifies the degree of unreflected causality. We define the Reversible Causal Principle (RCP): every causal relation possesses a conjugate dual, and entropy, energy dissipation, and the classical arrow of time appear only when these alternating components decohere or fail to reflect perfectly. The framework unifies Wheeler--Feynman absorber theory, Bennett's reversible computation, Shannon's communication theory, and the process matrix formalism under a single symmetry principle, and identifies experimentally accessible signatures in reversible digital links and quantum switch experiments. The arrow of time, in this picture, records the universe's imperfect causal echo.
one_liner: A process-theoretic framework links reversible information exchange in entangled photonic systems to the emergence of classical time via decoherence.
why_it_matters: It connects indefinite causal structure, reversible communication, and decoherence in a way that suggests experimentally testable signatures in photonic switch and feedback setups.
detailed_summary: The authors formalize 'subtime' as reversible information exchange inside entangled systems and cast it in an Oreshkov-Costa-Brukner-style process-matrix framework with explicit time-reversal duality. They define Perfect Information Feedback and a Reversible Causal Principle, under which mutual information is conserved in closed causal loops and entropy quantifies imperfect causal reflection. The work is largely theoretical, but it identifies observable signatures in reversible photonic links and quantum-switch experiments where decoherence converts reversible causal alternation into an effective classical time arrow.
main_result: The paper derives a unified reversible-causality framework in which classical time emerges asymptotically from decohering reversible information exchange.
application_angle: It offers a conceptual design language for photonic experiments probing causal nonclassicality, reversible communication, and decoherence-driven time asymmetry.

title: Surpassing Quantum Noise Limits with Nonlinear Amplification
abstract: Linear quantum amplifiers are indispensable tools for quantum technologies, yet their performance is fundamentally limited by quantum noise, precluding any signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) enhancement unless supplemented by post-selection or non-classical resources. To surpass this limitation, we propose a nonlinear quantum amplification strategy that exploits the interplay between a gain-stabilized bright eigenmode of a coupled two-mode bosonic system and Kerr nonlinearity. We demonstrate that this interplay enables the signal gain to surpass the noise gain in a selected quadrature, leading to a net increase in the SNR beyond the quantum limits of conventional linear amplifiers. Our work thus establishes a novel nonlinear amplification paradigm capable of enhancing the SNR, with promising applications across quantum information processing, quantum communications, and quantum metrology.
one_liner: A Kerr-assisted nonlinear bosonic amplifier is predicted to boost signal-to-noise ratio beyond the standard quantum limits of linear amplification.
why_it_matters: True SNR enhancement without postselection or exotic input resources would be valuable for weak-signal readout, quantum links, and precision metrology.
detailed_summary: The work studies a coupled two-mode bosonic system with a gain-stabilized bright eigenmode interacting with Kerr nonlinearity. In this regime, the selected signal quadrature experiences gain that exceeds the corresponding noise gain, unlike standard phase-preserving or phase-sensitive linear amplifiers constrained by quantum noise limits. The analysis establishes a nonlinear amplification mechanism that can increase output SNR directly rather than merely preserving it.
main_result: The proposed nonlinear amplifier yields quadrature-selective gain larger than noise gain, enabling net SNR improvement beyond conventional linear quantum-amplifier limits.
application_angle: Such amplifiers could improve receiver sensitivity in quantum communication and enhance weak-signal detection in metrology and quantum measurement chains.

title: Quantitative 3D imaging of highly distorted micro-crystals using Bragg ptychography
abstract: Bragg coherent diffraction imaging (BCDI) fails to reliably retrieve phases in micro-crystals exhibiting strong strain inhomogeneities, which restricts its applicability. Here we show that three-dimensional Bragg ptychography (3DBP) overcomes this limitation by enabling stable inversion for large lattice distortions. Using a combination of experimental measurements and numerical tests, we compare the performance limits of the two approaches and demonstrate that 3DBP tolerates lattice distortions more than six times larger than BCDI. We also establish the sensitivity of both methods on a weakly distorted crystal, for which 3DBP yields smoother amplitude and phase fields with reduced short-length-scale artifacts. 3DBP thus provides a reliable route for imaging micro-crystals with large lattice distortions, expanding the scope of coherent X-ray Bragg microscopy to strongly deformed systems.
one_liner: Three-dimensional Bragg ptychography robustly reconstructs highly strained micro-crystals where conventional BCDI phase retrieval breaks down.
why_it_matters: It significantly expands coherent X-ray imaging to strongly deformed materials, which is important for device diagnostics and operando materials studies.
detailed_summary: The paper compares 3D Bragg ptychography against Bragg coherent diffraction imaging for reconstructing lattice distortion fields in micro-crystals. Using experiments and simulations, the authors show that 3DBP remains stable for distortion amplitudes more than six times larger than those tolerable by BCDI. On weakly distorted samples, 3DBP also produces smoother amplitude and phase reconstructions with fewer short-scale artifacts.
main_result: 3D Bragg ptychography is shown to tolerate over sixfold larger lattice distortions than BCDI while delivering cleaner 3D amplitude and phase maps.
application_angle: The method is directly relevant for quantitative imaging of strained crystalline materials in microelectronics, energy materials, and other deformation-sensitive systems.

title: Criticality-enhanced global frequency sensing with a monitored Kerr parametric oscillator via extended Kalman filter
abstract: We analyze a global sensing scenario in which the frequency of a monitored Kerr parametric oscillator is estimated assuming limited prior information. The frequency is estimated in real-time by continuously monitoring the oscillator quadrature through homodyne detection and processing the resulting photocurrent with an extended Kalman filter (EKF). Due to the sensor nonlinearity, individual EKF trajectories do not always converge to the true unknown frequency in the long-time limit. However, we show that the statistical distribution of the frequency estimates does exhibit a sharp peak around the true value in the same limit. Leveraging this key statistical property, we develop a global sensing protocol assisted by adaptive control of the sensor parameters to harness critical enhancement. We present numerical evidence that this criticality-enhanced frequency estimation remains robust under low detection efficiency.
one_liner: Real-time homodyne monitoring plus an extended Kalman filter enables globally robust frequency estimation in a Kerr parametric oscillator near criticality.
why_it_matters: It combines nonlinear quantum sensing with practical estimation and adaptive control, pointing to deployable criticality-enhanced sensors under realistic inefficiency.
detailed_summary: The authors consider a monitored Kerr parametric oscillator whose frequency is inferred from continuous homodyne photocurrent using an extended Kalman filter.
