Although single EKF trajectories can fail to converge because of the oscillator's nonlinearity, the long-time distribution of estimated frequencies becomes sharply peaked at the true value. Building on that property, they design an adaptive global-sensing protocol that exploits operation near criticality and show numerically that the enhancement survives even with low detection efficiency.
main_result: The work demonstrates a criticality-assisted, EKF-based frequency-estimation protocol that remains statistically accurate and robust under imperfect detection.
application_angle: It is relevant for real-time precision sensing with nonlinear photonic or superconducting resonators where continuous readout and adaptive tuning are practical.

title: Demonstration of High-Fidelity Gates in a Strongly Anharmonic with Long-Coherence C-Shunt Flux Qubit
abstract: We demonstrate high-fidelity single-qubit gates on a C-shunt flux qubit that simultaneously combines a large anharmonicity ($\mathcal{A}/2π=848~\mathrm{MHz}$) with long relaxation time ($T_1 = 23~μ\text{s}$). The large anharmonicity significantly suppresses leakage to higher energy levels, enabling fast and precise microwave control. Using DRAG pulses and randomized benchmarking, the qubit achieves gate fidelities exceeding 99.9\%, highlighting the capability of C-shunt flux qubits for robust and high-performance quantum operations. These results establish them as a promising platform for scalable quantum information processing.
one_liner: A long-lived, strongly anharmonic C-shunt flux qubit achieves >99.9% single-qubit gate fidelity using DRAG control.
why_it_matters: It shows a superconducting qubit design can simultaneously offer large anharmonicity and long coherence, easing leakage control while preserving performance.
detailed_summary: This experiment reports single-qubit control on a C-shunt flux qubit with anharmonicity A/2pi = 848 MHz and relaxation time T1 = 23 microseconds. The large level spacing suppresses leakage during fast microwave drives, enabling high-quality DRAG pulse shaping. Randomized benchmarking yields gate fidelities above 99.9%, indicating that this flux-qubit variant can combine precise control with strong coherence.
main_result: The device demonstrates >99.9% single-qubit gate fidelity while maintaining 848 MHz anharmonicity and 23 microseconds of energy-relaxation time.
application_angle: This makes the platform attractive for scalable superconducting processors that need fast gates with reduced leakage and calibration overhead.

title: Experimental Demonstrations of Coherence de Broglie Wavelength for Scalable Superresolution with Near-perfect Fringe Visibility
abstract: Quantum sensing and metrology have been intensively studied over the last several decades to surpass the fundamental shot-noise limit of classical systems and approach the Heisenberg limit. However, implementation of N00N-state-based quantum sensing has been severely constrained by the limited order N, intrinsically imperfect fringe visibility, and vulnerability to photon loss. Recently, the coherence de Broglie wavelength (CBW) has been proposed as an alternative method for achieving superresolution in a coherently coupled interferometer architecture, whose characteristics resemble those of photonic de Broglie wavelength (PBW) used in quantum sensing. Here, we experimentally demonstrate scalable CBW superresolution up to N=3, with near-perfect fringe visibility that is invariant to photon loss. The observed CBWs have the potential to enable a superresolution sensing platform even if it remains within the shot-noise limit.
one_liner: A coupled-interferometer experiment demonstrates scalable coherence de Broglie wavelength superresolution up to N=3 with near-unity fringe visibility.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical superresolution route that avoids key N00N-state limitations, especially visibility loss and sensitivity to photon loss.
detailed_summary: The work experimentally realizes coherence de Broglie wavelength superresolution in a coherently coupled interferometer and scales the effect to order N=3. In contrast to N00N-state approaches, the measured fringes retain near-perfect visibility and the visibility is reported to be invariant under photon loss. The architecture reproduces de Broglie-like phase superresolution without relying on high-order path-entangled states, making the scheme experimentally simpler and more robust.
main_result: The experiment shows CBW superresolution up to N=3 with near-perfect, loss-invariant fringe visibility.
application_angle: This could enable robust photonic superresolution sensors where practical stability and tolerance to loss matter more than achieving Heisenberg scaling.

title: Optimal quantum metrology protocols with erasure qubits
abstract: We investigate the precision limits and optimal protocols for sensing single qubit signals in the presence of erasure noise. We study a hierarchy of precision limits achievable with metrological strategies of differing complexity, and identify the optimal protocol for each. The detectability of erasure noise is shown to lead to enhanced precision limits and simplified sensing protocols. For energy gap estimation, we demonstrate that a simple product-state continuous erasure detection strategy yields significant improvements, outperforming optimal entangled protocols even for large numbers of qubits. We show that for other single-qubit signals, quantum error correction provides a substantial advantage by correcting the dominant erasure processes, and can restore Heisenberg-limited precision in certain erasure configurations. As a byproduct of our analysis, we find erasure-conversion schemes for qubits subject to thermal noise that attain the corresponding ultimate precision limits.
one_liner: This paper maps optimal sensing strategies for single-qubit signals under erasure noise, from simple detection schemes to error-corrected protocols.
why_it_matters: Erasure is detectable, so tailored protocols can outperform standard entangled metrology and even recover Heisenberg scaling in favorable cases.
detailed_summary: The authors derive a hierarchy of precision limits for estimating single-qubit parameters when erasure events are present and detectable. For energy-gap estimation, they find that continuous erasure monitoring with product states can beat optimal entangled strategies even at large qubit numbers. For other signals, quantum error correction can suppress dominant erasures and restore Heisenberg-limited scaling in certain configurations. They also show thermal-noise-to-erasure conversion schemes that attain the ultimate precision bounds for the converted model.
main_result: Optimal metrology under erasure noise depends on the signal, with continuous erasure detection or error correction yielding substantial precision gains and sometimes Heisenberg scaling.
application_angle: The results directly inform sensing protocol design for hardware where loss or leakage is detectable, including atomic, photonic, and solid-state qubit platforms.

title: Symbolic Quantum State Representation and its Simulation
abstract: We introduce a symbolic operator framework for simulating quantum photonic systems that works directly with the canonical commutation relations and the Weyl algebra. Unlike existing Fock-space or Gaussian simulators, our method treats temporal wave packets and polarization modes in a continuous setting and does not rely on discretization or Hilbert-space truncation. Device operations are expressed as algebraic rewrite rules acting on creation and annihilation operators, allowing exact evolution of finite-photon states through linear optical networks. As an illustration, we reproduce Hong-Ou-Mandel interference for Gaussian pulses with controlled temporal and spectral mismatch.
one_liner: A symbolic Weyl-algebra simulator models finite-photon linear optics exactly in continuous time, including wave packets and polarization.
why_it_matters: It avoids Fock truncation and discretization, potentially improving simulation fidelity for realistic photonic circuits with temporal-spectral structure.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a symbolic operator formalism that evolves photonic states directly through canonical commutation relations rather than finite-dimensional Fock truncations. Temporal wave packets and polarization modes are treated in a continuous representation, and device actions are implemented as algebraic rewrite rules on creation and annihilation operators. As a concrete validation, the framework reproduces Hong-Ou-Mandel interference for Gaussian pulses with tunable temporal and spectral mismatch. This makes it well suited to modeling realistic few-photon experiments where mode mismatch is central.
main_result: The authors demonstrate exact symbolic simulation of finite-photon linear-optical processes in continuous modes, including mismatched-pulse HOM interference.
application_angle: This can support photonic device design and verification by capturing experimentally relevant pulse and polarization effects without aggressive approximations.

title: Phase Retrieval using Nonlinear Curvature Sensing within Convergent Beams
abstract: Path-length diversity methods may be used for adaptive optics (AO) systems to retrieve phase and amplitude information by measuring intensity across multiple planes. Observations that rely on free-space propagation, such as the nonlinear curvature wavefront sensor (WFS), have been shown to offer excellent sensitivity and robustness to scintillation. However, the default design results in a large opto-mechanical footprint due to unavoidable geometric-optics and wave-optics effects. Measurements recorded in a convergent beam would improve instrument compactness, while concentrating light into smaller detector regions of interest, improving signal-to-noise ratio and possibly wavefront reconstruction speed. In this paper, we study path-length diversity wavefront sensing using four planes of contemporaneous intensity measurements made in a convergent beam. We develop a physical optics propagation model and validate the model by performing wavefront reconstructions in both simulations and lab experiments. The manuscripts core contribution is a practical, intensity-domain, Fourier-transform-based recipe to use a conventional multi-plane Gerchberg-Saxton (or comparable) reconstruction pipeline with convergent-beam measurements, enabling a compact optical layout. We find that this approach offers practical benefits over an equivalent free-space wavefront sensor, in particular reducing size, weight, complexity and cost.
one_liner: A convergent-beam nonlinear curvature sensing method retrieves phase from four intensity planes using a compact Fourier-based reconstruction recipe.
why_it_matters: It shrinks wavefront-sensing hardware while improving light concentration and potentially boosting SNR and reconstruction speed.
detailed_summary: The authors reformulate multi-plane intensity-based phase retrieval for measurements taken in a convergent beam rather than a larger free-space layout. They develop a physical-optics propagation model and show that a conventional Gerchberg-Saxton-style multi-plane reconstruction pipeline can be adapted using an intensity-domain Fourier-transform recipe. The approach is validated in both simulation and laboratory wavefront reconstructions. Compared with free-space implementations, the convergent-beam geometry reduces size, weight, complexity, and cost while concentrating light onto smaller detector regions.
main_result: The study demonstrates practical phase retrieval from four contemporaneous convergent-beam intensity planes using standard iterative reconstruction tools.
application_angle: Compact, efficient wavefront sensing is relevant to free-space optical links and other communication or imaging systems constrained by payload, alignment, and detector efficiency.

title: Optical parametric multi-pass cell amplifier
abstract: Ultrafast lasers with simultaneously high average and peak power have become indispensable for driving a multitude of applications, including high-harmonic generation, strong-field physics, and particle source applications. Both parametric amplifiers and post-compressed Ytterbium lasers have emerged as prime platforms to meet these demands. While multi-pass cell (MPC) based post-compression offers broadband output with high beam quality, it provides limited wavelength tunability and suffers from temporal contrast degradation. Conversely, optical parametric amplifiers (OPAs) provide spectral tunability and high temporal contrast but they are limited by low pump-to-signal conversion efficiency and spatial beam inhomogeneities. Here, we introduce the Optical Parametric Multi-Pass Cell Amplifier (OPMPC), a hybrid architecture that overcomes the limitations of both schemes. Our approach utilizes two non-collinearly intersecting MPCs providing broadband parametric amplification of the seed pulses and complete idler removal after each pass through the crystal, thereby suppressing back-conversion. We experimentally demonstrate a record pump-to-signal power conversion efficiency of 43% using a 1030 nm pump at a 1 kHz repetition rate with a pulse energy of 174 $μ$J. The amplified signal at 1500 nm exhibits excellent beam quality, power and spectral stability and is compressed to 48 fs, demonstrating a new platform for ultrafast pulse generation.
one_liner: A new optical parametric multi-pass cell amplifier reaches 43% pump-to-signal efficiency and delivers compressible 1500 nm femtosecond pulses.
why_it_matters: It combines OPA tunability and contrast with MPC-like beam quality and efficiency, addressing a central bottleneck in ultrafast source engineering.
detailed_summary: The proposed OPMPC architecture uses two non-collinearly intersecting multi-pass cells to achieve broadband parametric amplification while removing the idler after each crystal pass to suppress back-conversion. Experimentally, the system is pumped at 1030 nm and 1 kHz with 174 microjoule pulse energy, producing a record 43% pump-to-signal conversion efficiency. The amplified 1500 nm output shows strong beam quality, spectral and power stability, and is compressed to 48 fs. This positions the design as a high-performance, tunable ultrafast source platform.
main_result: The experiment achieves 43% pump-to-signal conversion efficiency with stable 1500 nm output compressed to 48 fs.
application_angle: Such sources are useful for precision spectroscopy, nonlinear optics, and strong-field experiments that need tunable high-quality femtosecond pulses.

title: Probing the memory of a superconducting qubit environment
abstract: Achieving fault tolerance with superconducting quantum processors requires qubits to operate within the regime of threshold theorems based on the Born-Markov approximation. This approximation, which models dissipation as constant energy decay into a memoryless environment, breaks down when qubits couple to long-lived two-level systems (TLSs) that become polarized during operation and retain memory of past qubit states. Here, we show that non-Poissonian quantum jump traces carry the information required to distinguish long-lived TLSs from the standard Markovian bath. By fitting the Solomon equations to measured quantum jumps dynamics arising naturally due to thermal fluctuations, we can disentangle the coupling of the qubit to the two environments. Sweeping the qubit frequency reveals distinct peaks, each associated with a TLS that outlives the qubit, providing a handle to understand their microscopic origin.
one_liner: Quantum-jump statistics are used to identify long-lived TLS defects and quantify non-Markovian memory in a superconducting qubit environment.
why_it_matters: Directly resolving environmental memory is important for understanding decoherence mechanisms that limit fault-tolerant superconducting processors.
detailed_summary: The work shows that deviations from Poissonian quantum-jump behavior contain enough information to separate a memoryless bath from long-lived two-level systems coupled to a qubit. By fitting Solomon-equation dynamics to thermally driven jump traces, the authors extract couplings to both Markovian and TLS environments. Frequency sweeps reveal discrete peaks associated with TLSs whose lifetimes exceed that of the qubit itself. This provides a spectroscopic handle on microscopic defect-induced memory effects beyond standard Born-Markov assumptions.
platform_or_system: Superconducting qubit coupled to TLS defects
main_result: Measured quantum-jump traces and frequency sweeps reveal long-lived TLS-induced environmental memory and disentangle it from ordinary Markovian decay.
application_angle: The method can guide materials screening, qubit tuning, and mitigation strategies for defect-driven errors in superconducting quantum hardware.

title: Quantum synchronization and chimera states in a programmable quantum many-body system
abstract: Synchronization is a hallmark of collective behavior in classical nonlinear systems, yet its realization as a robust many-body phenomenon in coherent quantum systems remains largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate symmetry-protected quantum synchronization and a quantum chimera state in coherent Floquet dynamics on programmable superconducting quantum processors. By implementing stroboscopic evolution of a two-dimensional Heisenberg model on IBM heavy-hex devices, we observe that initially phase-randomized spins spontaneously self-organize into coherent lattice-wide oscillations. On 28 qubits, synchronization persists even for strongly randomized initial states and is stabilized by SU(2) symmetry, as confirmed by explicit symmetry breaking. Scaling up to 156 qubits reveals a qualitatively distinct regime. For weak initial randomness, global synchronization extends across the device. For strong randomness, the system fails to synchronize globally, yet subsets of qubits exhibit robust local phase coherence under homogeneous unitary dynamics. This coexistence of globally desynchronized and locally synchronized regions constitutes a quantum analogue of a classical chimera state. Statevector and matrix-product-state simulations reproduce both the symmetry-protected synchronization and the chimera coexistence, demonstrating that these phenomena arise from the intrinsic Floquet many-body dynamics. Our results establish symmetry-protected synchronization and quantum chimera states as experimentally accessible nonequilibrium dynamical phases in programable many-body quantum systems.
one_liner: Floquet evolution on IBM heavy-hex processors reveals symmetry-protected quantum synchronization and a many-body quantum chimera regime.
why_it_matters: It shows nonequilibrium collective phases can be engineered and scaled on superconducting hardware, opening a route to controlled dynamical resources beyond static state prep.
detailed_summary: The work implements stroboscopic dynamics of a two-dimensional Heisenberg model on IBM heavy-hex superconducting processors and tracks phase ordering from initially randomized spin configurations. On 28 qubits, lattice-wide synchronization emerges robustly and is stabilized by SU(2) symmetry, with explicit symmetry breaking suppressing the effect. On 156 qubits, the dynamics split into either global synchronization for weak randomness or coexistence of locally synchronized and globally desynchronized regions for strong randomness, constituting a quantum chimera state. Statevector and matrix-product-state simulations reproduce the observed regimes, supporting an intrinsic many-body Floquet origin.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates symmetry-protected quantum synchronization up to 28 qubits and quantum chimera behavior on 156-qubit superconducting hardware.
application_angle: Programmable dynamical phases like these could become useful for robust collective control, benchmarking, and engineered nonequilibrium resources in scalable quantum processors.

title: Second-Harmonic Generation at a Fourth-Order Exceptional Point Degeneracy
abstract: An anomalous flat-band dispersion provided by a degenerate band edge (DBE) of longitudinal optical modes in a double-grating waveguide is used to enhance second-harmonic generation (SHG). The DBE is a fourth-order exceptional point degeneracy (EPD) in a lossless and gainless waveguide, characterized by the coalescence of four eigenmodes that establish a frozen mode in a cavity. At a DBE resonance, the cavity quality factor scales $Q\propto N^5$, where $N$ is the number of unit cells of the grating waveguide. In our numerical experiments, we observe the peak intensity of the fundamental field in the edge-excited cavity scaling as $I_1\propto N^{3.6}$. This leads to a highly efficient SHG process that is radiated vertically from the cavity (i.e., normal to the grating) without requiring collinear phase matching, with a conversion efficiency scaling as $η\propto N^{8.27}$. These results establish DBE-based waveguides as promising platforms for miniaturized efficient nonlinear photonic devices.
one_liner: A double-grating waveguide at a fourth-order exceptional point strongly boosts second-harmonic generation via frozen-mode field buildup.
why_it_matters: Exceptional-point photonic engineering offers a compact path to very high nonlinear conversion efficiency without conventional phase-matching constraints.
detailed_summary: The paper studies SHG in a double-grating waveguide supporting a degenerate band edge, a fourth-order exceptional point degeneracy where four longitudinal modes coalesce. At the DBE resonance, the cavity quality factor scales as N^5 with grating length, and simulations show the fundamental intensity scaling approximately as N^3.6 under edge excitation. The resulting vertically radiated second harmonic avoids the need for collinear phase matching, with conversion efficiency scaling as N^8.27 in the reported numerical experiments. The mechanism relies on anomalously flat dispersion and frozen-mode enhancement near the exceptional point.
main_result: Numerical experiments show DBE resonances can produce strongly superlinear SHG enhancement, with conversion efficiency scaling as eta proportional to N^8.27.
application_angle: This points to miniaturized nonlinear photonic sources and frequency converters with high efficiency in integrated platforms where standard phase matching is difficult.

title: Hybrid Analog Teleportation-Direct Transmission in Noisy Bosonic Channels
abstract: Quantum teleportation uses a shared entangled resource, local operations, and a digitally error-corrected classical channel to transfer quantum states between distant parties. We introduce a hybrid teleportation-direct transmission protocol for state transfer that still exploits entanglement, but replaces classical communication and digital error correction with an analog feedforward through a noisy quantum channel. We show that quantum teleportation outperforms this protocol if the communication channel reduces the entanglement of all bipartite states having the same amount of entanglement as the resource; otherwise, the hybrid protocol is optimal. We apply our result to the state transfer of a uniformly distributed coherent-states codebook, highlighting experimentally relevant scenarios where our protocol is most effective. Our findings are directly relevant to both optical and superconducting microwave channels, where analog feedforward techniques have been recently implemented.
one_liner: A hybrid state-transfer protocol combines entanglement with analog feedforward through a noisy bosonic channel, interpolating between teleportation and direct transmission.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical decision rule for when analog feedforward can outperform standard teleportation in optical or microwave links with realistic noise.
detailed_summary: The authors formulate a hybrid transfer protocol that still uses a shared entangled resource but replaces digitally protected classical communication with analog feedforward sent through a noisy quantum channel. They derive conditions under which standard teleportation is superior and when the hybrid protocol becomes optimal, based on how the channel degrades entanglement for states matched to the resource. The analysis is then specialized to a uniformly distributed coherent-state codebook, giving performance regimes relevant to continuous-variable transfer. The framework directly targets experimentally accessible optical and superconducting microwave channels where analog feedforward is feasible.
platform_or_system: continuous-variable optical or microwave bosonic channels
main_result: The paper identifies the channel-noise regimes where hybrid analog teleportation-direct transmission is optimal and where conventional teleportation remains better.
application_angle: The protocol is relevant for near-term continuous-variable quantum networks and modular superconducting systems that can exploit analog hardware feedforward without full digital correction.

title: Propagation of Two-Photon Zernike States in Atmospheric Turbulence
abstract: We analyze propagation and detection of two-photon states expanded in Zernike modes through atmospheric turbulence using the extended Huygens-Fresnel formalism. For SPDC states prepared with a single Zernike pump mode, we analytically reduce the 8-dimensional continuous propagation integrals to an exact, discrete modal expansion. In the absence of turbulence, Zernike addition enforces conservation of azimuthal index and a strict radial-order bound. Turbulence relaxes these constraints, driving structured azimuthal and radial crosstalk dominated by low-order aberration modes. By explicitly removing the lowest-order terms from the discrete turbulence sum, we demonstrate that partial adaptive optics correcting only up to the sixth radial order is sufficient to heavily suppress this crosstalk and restore near-ideal spatial correlations.
one_liner: An exact modal treatment of SPDC two-photon Zernike states in turbulence shows low-order adaptive optics can largely restore spatial correlations.
why_it_matters: It provides a structured basis for turbulence-resilient free-space quantum links using aberration-aware mode engineering and modest correction complexity.
detailed_summary: Using the extended Huygens-Fresnel formalism, the paper derives an exact discrete modal expansion for two-photon states generated by SPDC with a single Zernike pump mode, reducing an eight-dimensional propagation problem to a tractable basis description. In the turbulence-free case, the addition rules enforce azimuthal-index conservation and a strict radial-order bound. Turbulence relaxes both constraints and produces structured crosstalk dominated by low-order aberrations across azimuthal and radial mode indices. By removing the lowest-order terms in the turbulence sum, the authors show that correcting only up to sixth radial order can strongly suppress crosstalk and recover near-ideal correlations.
platform_or_system: SPDC two-photon spatial modes in atmospheric channels
main_result: The analysis shows that partial adaptive optics targeting low-order aberrations can heavily restore two-photon Zernike-mode correlations after turbulent propagation.
application_angle: This is directly useful for free-space quantum communication and imaging schemes that need robust high-dimensional spatial entanglement under atmospheric distortion.

title: Engineering near-unitary one-axis twisting evolution via a driven Tavis-Cummings model
abstract: One-axis twisting (OAT) interaction is a pivotal resource for manipulating quantum states of atomic ensembles, enabling spin squeezing, atomic-cat-state generation, and weak-phase amplification. Current implementations of OAT dynamics predominantly rely on the Tavis-Cummings model of light-atoms coupling; however, this approach inevitably introduces an additional Stark term that entangles the light with the atoms, which compromises the unitarity of OAT evolution and thereby degrades the OAT-based control precision. Here we propose a scheme based on a driven Tavis-Cummings model to achieve near-unitary OAT evolution. We demonstrate that both constant and time-varying driving of an atoms-cavity hybrid system can realize near-unitary OAT evolution, albeit with distinct coupling strength. Furthermore, when atomic dissipation is taken into account, we find that the time-varying-driving scheme exhibits superior resistance to decoherence. Our approach is broadly applicable to a variety of atomic platforms, including cold atoms, trapped ions, and nitrogen-vacancy centers.
one_liner: Driven Tavis-Cummings control can realize near-unitary one-axis twisting while suppressing detrimental light-atom entanglement from the Stark term.
why_it_matters: Cleaner one-axis twisting improves metrology-oriented spin squeezing and nonclassical state generation across several atomic hardware platforms.
detailed_summary: The work proposes constant-drive and time-varying-drive protocols for an atom-cavity system governed by a driven Tavis-Cummings model to approximate unitary one-axis twisting more closely than the standard implementation. The key idea is to mitigate the extra Stark-induced light-atom entanglement that otherwise spoils ideal OAT dynamics and control fidelity. Both driving strategies generate near-unitary OAT, but with different effective coupling strengths and operating tradeoffs. When atomic dissipation is included, the time-varying-drive protocol is predicted to be more robust against decoherence.
main_result: The paper shows that engineered driving can produce near-unitary OAT evolution and that time-varying driving offers better dissipation resilience.
application_angle: Better OAT directly supports precision sensing, squeezed-state preparation, and cat-state generation in cold atoms, trapped ions, and NV-center ensemble platforms.

title: Frequency downshifting stair for ultra-intense femtosecond lasers through a plasma-photonics structure
abstract: Wavelength-tunable ultra-intense femtosecond lasers may enable breakthroughs in diverse areas of science spanning attosecond science, particle acceleration and beyond. Conventional crystal-based methods are limited by gain bandwidth and damage thresholds, which restrict their wavelength tunability. Plasma-based frequency conversion, unconstrained by material damage, offers a promising alternative. Here, a novel scheme named Frequency Downshifting Stair (FDS) based on plasma bubble filling control is presented. The FDS enables arbitrary frequency down-conversion of ultra-intense femtosecond pulses and yields chirp-free laser pulses. It can achieve near-100% photon conversion efficiency, approaching the physical limit. This is attributed to the linear control by the FDS of laser chirp evolution during the photon deceleration in the plasma wake bubble. For a laser pulse with an arbitrary wavelength λ_0 (e.g., λ_0=800nm), proof-of-concept PIC simulations demonstrate that a single-stage FDS enables continuous wavelength tuning from λ_0 to {2λ}_0 (800-1600nm). Moreover, a three-stage cascaded FDS achieves more than tenfold frequency (10λ_0) downshifting to a central wavelength of 8.5μm. The FDS scheme thus provides a universal pathway for generating high-energy, few-cycle pulses across the broad infrared regime, offering a powerful new tool for wavelength-dependent ultrafast science.
one_liner: A plasma-photonics frequency-downshifting stair enables tunable, chirp-free wavelength conversion of ultra-intense femtosecond lasers with near-unit photon efficiency.
why_it_matters: It addresses a major bottleneck in high-field photonics by offering broadband wavelength agility beyond crystal damage and bandwidth limits.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces the Frequency Downshifting Stair, a plasma-bubble filling control scheme that down-converts the frequency of ultra-intense femtosecond pulses while preserving chirp-free output. The mechanism uses controlled photon deceleration in a plasma wake bubble, with the authors arguing that the staged evolution linearly manages chirp and can approach 100% photon conversion efficiency. Particle-in-cell simulations show a single stage can tune an input around lambda_0 continuously to 2 lambda_0, while three cascaded stages push the output to about 8.5 microns for an 800 nm starting pulse. The approach is material-damage-free and broadly wavelength agnostic in principle.
main_result: Simulations demonstrate continuous single-stage downshifting from 800 to 1600 nm and cascaded conversion to roughly 8.5 microns with high efficiency.
application_angle: The scheme could become a practical route to high-energy few-cycle infrared sources for attosecond science, strong-field experiments, and advanced accelerator applications.

title: Measurement-Induced State transitions in Inductively-Shunted Transmons
abstract: Fast and high-fidelity qubit measurement plays a key role in quantum error correction. In superconducting qubits, measurement is typically performed using a resonant microwave drive on a readout resonator dispersively coupled to the qubit. Shorter measurement times require larger numbers of photons populating the readout resonator, which ultimately leads to undesired measurementinduced state transitions (MIST) of the qubit. MIST can be particularly problematic because these transitions often leave the qubit in a high energy state, and the MIST locations in readout parameter space drift as a function of qubit offset charge. In transmon qubits, these drifts have been avoided using very large qubit-resonator detunings or dedicated offset charge biases. In this work, we take an alternative approach and add an inductive shunt to the transmon to eliminate the offset charge dependence and stabilize the MIST. We experimentally characterize MIST in several different inductively-shunted transmons, in agreement with quantum and semiclassical models for MIST. These results extend to other inductively-shunted qubits.
one_liner: Inductively shunted transmons suppress offset-charge drift of measurement-induced state transitions during fast high-photon readout.
why_it_matters: Stabilizing readout-induced leakage improves calibration robustness and supports faster, higher-fidelity superconducting-qubit measurement for error correction.
detailed_summary: The work studies measurement-induced state transitions (MIST) in dispersively read out superconducting qubits when resonator photon number is pushed high for fast measurement. By adding an inductive shunt to the transmon, the authors remove offset-charge dependence that otherwise causes MIST locations in readout parameter space to drift. Experiments across several devices agree with both quantum and semiclassical models of the transition mechanism. The result provides a hardware-level route to more stable high-power readout without relying on extreme detuning or dedicated charge biasing.
main_result: The authors experimentally show that inductive shunting stabilizes measurement-induced state transitions by eliminating offset-charge-driven drift in transmon readout behavior.
application_angle: This directly supports scalable superconducting processors by making fast qubit measurement more reliable under the high-photon conditions needed for QEC.

title: Single-nanoparticle detection using quasi-bound states in the continuum supported by silicon metasurfaces
abstract: The detection of single particles or molecules represents a critical milestone in the development of biosensing technologies. Recently developed optical sensors based on quasi-bound states in the continuum (qBICs) have primarily focused on detecting global refractive index changes, aiming to simultaneously enhance both refractive index sensitivity and quality ($Q$) factors. However, sensors capable of resolving local refractive index perturbations, such as the binding of a nanometer-sized molecule on a surface, remain elusive and have not yet been demonstrated in BIC geometries due to the limited $Q$ factors and relatively large mode volumes. Here, we demonstrate low-contrast BIC metasurfaces that can perform sensing with a virus-sized single-nanoparticle resolution. The qBIC resonance operating at the critical coupling condition exhibits an experimental $Q$ factor of 4.5 x 10$^4$ in heavy water. The strong interaction between the localized electric field and polystyrene nanoparticles with a diameter of 100 nm enable the experimental observation of step-like resonance wavelength shifts, serving as signatures of individual particle binding events. Furthermore, binding-induced modifications to the qBIC resonance alter the optical confinement and asymmetry factor, inducing changes not only in the resonance wavelength but also in the linewidth and amplitude with single-particle sensitivity. Combined with position-insensitive response and free-space accessible features, low-contrast BIC metasurfaces provide a user-friendly platform for next-generation single-molecule sensing integrated with microfluidic systems.
one_liner: Silicon qBIC metasurfaces detect individual 100 nm nanoparticles via discrete resonance changes in free-space optical readout.
why_it_matters: Single-particle sensitivity in a free-space, microfluidics-compatible metasurface is a strong step toward practical label-free biosensors and optical transducers.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates low-contrast silicon metasurfaces supporting quasi-bound states in the continuum with an experimental Q of 4.5 x 10^4 in heavy water under critical coupling. Individual binding events from 100 nm polystyrene nanoparticles produce step-like changes in resonance wavelength, and also measurably perturb linewidth and amplitude. The response is position-insensitive and accessed in free space, addressing a key limitation of previous BIC sensors that mainly resolved global refractive-index shifts. The combination of high Q, localized fields, and open optical access makes the platform suitable for integration with fluidic sensing architectures.
main_result: The authors experimentally resolve single 100 nm nanoparticle binding events through discrete shifts in qBIC resonance wavelength, linewidth, and amplitude.
application_angle: The approach is well suited to label-free biosensing and chip-scale analyte detection where free-space access and microfluidic integration are practical advantages.

title: Raman relaxation in Yb(III) molecular qubits: non-trivial correlations between spin-phonon coupling and molecular structure
abstract: The coordination complexes of Yb(III) exhibit some of the longest spin coherence times among 4f compounds, making them a promising platform for molecular quantum technologies. While spin-phonon relaxation remains a limiting factor for coherence times even at low temperature, its control through chemical design has the potential to push these spin qubits prototypes beyond current limits. With the aim of providing insights on how to chemically control spin-phonon relaxation, we here present a full ab initio study of spin-phonon dynamics for three Yb(III) molecules exhibiting minimal chemical differences, yet quantitatively different spin relaxation times. Results show that low-temperature relaxation is governed by Raman processes triggered by a small group of largely delocalized low-energy phonons. The analysis of these contributions highlights that the modulation of spin-phonon coupling by molecular structure modifications beyond the first coordination shell are highly non-trivial in nature and hard to rationalize in simple chemical terms. These findings call for a conceptual step change from the attempt to use simple magneto-structural correlations to interpret the effect of molecular structural modifications on spin-phonon relaxation, and present predictive first-principles frameworks as a potential driving force of future chemical design strategies
one_liner: Ab initio modeling shows Raman relaxation in Yb molecular qubits is governed by a small set of delocalized low-energy phonons.
why_it_matters: It reframes molecular-qubit design from simple structural heuristics toward predictive first-principles engineering of spin-phonon decoherence.
detailed_summary: The authors perform a full first-principles analysis of spin-phonon dynamics in three closely related Yb(III) coordination complexes with different relaxation times. They find that low-temperature relaxation is dominated by Raman processes activated by a limited subset of largely delocalized low-energy vibrational modes. Small molecular modifications beyond the first coordination shell affect spin-phonon coupling in ways that are nontrivial and not captured by simple magneto-structural correlations. This indicates that chemically optimizing coherence in molecular qubits requires predictive atomistic modeling rather than intuitive local-structure rules.
main_result: The study identifies delocalized low-energy phonons as the dominant drivers of Raman spin relaxation and shows their dependence on molecular structure is highly nontrivial.
application_angle: The results inform chemistry-driven design of longer-coherence molecular qubits for scalable quantum memories and spin-based quantum devices.

title: Optimal control with flag qubits
abstract: High-fidelity quantum operations are the cornerstone of fault-tolerant quantum computation. In open quantum systems, traditional optimal control only passively resists decoherence, leaving environment-induced uncertainty as a fundamental performance bottleneck. To overcome this, we propose a new optimal control framework with flag ancillas and the Flag-GRAPE algorithm, which can actively tailor the system's noise structure. Through embedding post-selection directly into the objective function, Flag-GRAPE correlates decoherence errors with the ancilla's unexpected state. Subsequent measurement and post-selection effectively expel this uncertainty, circumventing the fidelity bounds of traditional control. Numerical simulations in a superconducting quantum circuit demonstrate a $51\%$ reduction in infidelity compared to traditional closed-system pulses and also show that such enhancement is robust across broad noise regimes. Furthermore, by actively converting unstructured decoherence into heralded erasure errors, Flag-GRAPE is inherently compatible with quantum error correction. We demonstrate this by initializing a logical cat-code state, showing that the combination between Flag-GRAPE and QEC yields immediate state preparation enhancements. This new framework can reduce hardware overhead for fault-tolerant architectures and open up a practical path toward logical state preparation gain in near-term experiments.
one_liner: Flag-GRAPE uses ancilla-assisted post-selection to convert decoherence into heralded errors and boost control fidelity.
why_it_matters: Actively reshaping noise instead of merely resisting it could lower hardware overhead for logical state preparation in near-term superconducting systems.
detailed_summary: This work introduces Flag-GRAPE, an optimal-control framework that embeds flag ancillas and post-selection into the pulse optimization objective. Rather than only minimizing open-system infidelity, the method steers decoherence to correlate with unexpected ancilla outcomes so that noisy trajectories can be rejected. Numerical simulations in a superconducting circuit show a 51% infidelity reduction relative to conventional closed-system pulse design and robustness across broad noise regimes. The method also maps unstructured noise into heralded erasures and improves logical cat-state initialization when combined with QEC.
main_result: Numerical simulations show Flag-GRAPE reduces infidelity by 51% versus standard pulse optimization while converting decoherence into heralded, QEC-compatible errors.
application_angle: The protocol offers a practical route to higher-fidelity state preparation and gate operations with less overhead in fault-tolerant superconducting architectures.

title: Frequency Comb Behavior of Time Crystals in an RF-Driven Dissipative Rydberg System
abstract: Driven nonlinear oscillators constitute a universal paradigm for understanding synchronization, frequency pulling, and frequency comb formation in nonequilibrium systems. Here, we realize such an emergent nonlinear oscillator in strongly interacting cesium Rydberg vapor, where coherent optical excitation, dissipation, and long-range interactions give rise to a driven-dissipative time crystal phase with intrinsic oscillation frequencies. Applying a radio-frequency (RF) field allows controlled tuning of the intrinsic oscillation frequency. Under RF heterodyne conditions, we observe intermodulation, frequency pulling, and, at strong drive, the emergence of a comb-like spectrum in the atomic coherence. We quantitatively capture these observations using a four-level mean-field model and demonstrate a classical analogue with a driven Van der Pol oscillator. Our results establish interacting Rydberg ensembles as a tunable platform for exploring nonequilibrium time-crystalline order, nonlinear synchronization, and frequency comb generation in many-body atomic systems.
one_liner: RF-driven Rydberg vapor time crystals exhibit intermodulation, frequency pulling, and comb-like spectra in atomic coherence.
why_it_matters: It turns dissipative interacting Rydberg media into tunable nonlinear frequency sources for quantum-optical and nonequilibrium-device studies.
detailed_summary: The authors realize a driven-dissipative time-crystal phase in strongly interacting cesium Rydberg vapor and tune its intrinsic oscillation frequency with a radio-frequency field. Under heterodyne-like RF conditions they observe intermodulation products, frequency pulling, and eventually a comb-like spectral structure in the atomic coherence. A four-level mean-field model quantitatively reproduces the observations, and a driven Van der Pol oscillator captures the classical nonlinear analogy. The work establishes Rydberg ensembles as a controllable platform for synchronization and comb generation in many-body atomic media.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates controllable frequency pulling and comb-like spectral generation from a dissipative Rydberg time crystal under RF driving.
application_angle: Such tunable atomic nonlinear oscillators could inform compact comb sources, synchronization elements, and quantum-optical hardware based on strongly interacting vapors.

title: Quantum-Secure-By-Construction (QSC): A Paradigm Shift For Post-Quantum Agentic Intelligence
abstract: As agentic artificial intelligence systems scale across globally distributed and long lived infrastructures, secure and policy compliant communication becomes a fundamental systems challenge. This challenge grows more serious in the quantum era, where the cryptographic assumptions built into today's AI deployments may not remain valid over their operational lifetime. Here, we introduce quantum secure by construction, or QSC, as a design paradigm that treats quantum secure communication as a core architectural property of agentic AI systems rather than an upgrade added later. We realize QSC through a runtime adaptive security model that combines post quantum cryptography, quantum random number generation, and quantum key distribution to secure interactions among autonomous agents operating across heterogeneous cloud, edge, and inter organizational environments. The approach is cryptographically pluggable and guided by policy, allowing the system to adjust its security posture according to infrastructure availability, regulatory constraints, and performance needs. QSC contributes a governance aware orchestration layer that selects and combines link specific cryptographic protections across the full agent lifecycle, including session bootstrap, inter agent coordination, tool invocation, and memory access. Through system level analysis and empirical evaluation, we examine the trade offs between classical and quantum secure mechanisms and show that QSC can reduce the operational complexity and cost of introducing quantum security into deployed agentic AI systems. These results position QSC as a foundational paradigm for post quantum agentic intelligence and establish a principled pathway for designing globally interoperable, resilient, and future ready intelligent systems.
one_liner: QSC proposes a policy-driven architecture that natively combines PQC, QRNG, and QKD for secure agentic AI communication.
why_it_matters: It treats quantum-safe communication as a system property, offering a deployment path for long-lived distributed AI infrastructures facing future cryptographic risk.
detailed_summary: The paper defines Quantum-Secure-by-Construction as an architectural paradigm for agentic AI systems operating across cloud, edge, and organizational boundaries. Its runtime-adaptive security layer composes post-quantum cryptography, quantum random number generation, and quantum key distribution according to policy, infrastructure availability, compliance constraints, and performance needs. The framework covers session bootstrap, inter-agent messaging, tool use, and memory access through cryptographically pluggable orchestration. System-level analysis and empirical evaluation are used to compare the operational tradeoffs of classical and quantum-secure mechanisms.
main_result: The authors present and evaluate a governance-aware orchestration framework that adaptively selects PQC, QRNG, and QKD protections across the agent lifecycle.
application_angle: This is relevant to deployable quantum-safe networking for AI services, especially where long-lived data, regulated environments, and cross-domain interoperability matter.

title: Transition from Statistical to Hardware-Limited Scaling in Photonic Quantum State Reconstruction
abstract: The theoretical efficiency of classical shadow tomography is predicated on a perfect Haar-random unitary ensemble, yet this mathematical ideal remains physically unattainable in near-term hardware. Here, we report the experimental discovery of a fundamental accuracy bound on integrated photonic processors: a ``Hardware Horizon'' where the reconstruction error undergoes a sharp phase transition. While the error initially obeys the predicted statistical scaling $\mathcal{O}(M^{-1/2})$, it abruptly saturates at a floor determined by the spectral distortions of the realized unitary group. By deriving a phenomenological error model, we decouple the competing mechanisms of static coherent spectral distortion and dynamic decoherence, demonstrating that this intrinsic noise floor imposes a hard bound that statistical accumulation cannot overcome. These findings establish that the utility of shadow tomography on NISQ (noisy intermediate-scale quantum) hardware is defined by a specific scaling law involving hardware parameters, necessitating active compensation strategies to bridge the gap between theoretical purity and the noisy reality of integrated photonics.
one_liner: Integrated photonic shadow tomography shows a sharp crossover from ideal statistical scaling to a hardware-imposed error floor.
why_it_matters: It identifies a concrete hardware ceiling for photonic state reconstruction, clarifying when more samples stop helping and compensation becomes necessary.
detailed_summary: The paper experimentally studies classical-shadow quantum state reconstruction on integrated photonic processors and finds a distinct transition between two regimes. At low sample counts, the reconstruction error follows the expected statistical scaling proportional to M^-1/2, but beyond a threshold it saturates at a hardware-limited floor. The authors build a phenomenological error model that separates static coherent spectral distortion of the implemented unitary ensemble from dynamic decoherence. This defines a measurable 'Hardware Horizon' that bounds achievable accuracy even with additional measurements.
main_result: The main result is the experimental observation and modeling of a hardware-limited error floor that sharply interrupts ideal shadow-tomography scaling on photonic hardware.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to practical photonic characterization and verification, since it tells designers when calibration and active compensation matter more than collecting more data.

title: Onset of Ergodicity Across Scales on a Digital Quantum Processor
abstract: Understanding how isolated quantum many-body systems thermalize remains a central question in modern physics. We study the onset of ergodicity in a two-dimensional disordered Heisenberg Floquet model using digital quantum simulation on IBM's Nighthawk superconducting processor, reaching system sizes of up to $10\times10$ qubits. We probe ergodicity across different length scales by coarse-graining the system into spatial patches of varying sizes and introducing a measure based on the collision entropy of each patch, enabling a detailed study of when ergodic behavior emerges across scales. The high sampling rate of superconducting quantum processing units, together with an optimal sample estimator, allow us to access patches of sizes up to $3\times3$. We observe that as the Heisenberg coupling $J$ increases, the noiseless system undergoes a smooth crossover from subergodic to ergodic behavior, with smaller patches approaching their random-matrix-theory values first, thereby revealing a hierarchy across scales. In the region of parameter space where classical tensor-network simulations are reliable, small patches or small values of $J$, we find excellent agreement with the error-mitigated quantum simulation. Beyond this regime, volume-law entanglement and contraction complexity growth causes the cost of classical methods to rise sharply. Our results open new directions for the use of quantum computers in the study of quantum thermalization.
one_liner: A superconducting quantum processor tracks how ergodic behavior emerges first locally and then across larger scales in a 2D Floquet spin model.
why_it_matters: It uses a large digital quantum simulation to probe thermalization where classical methods become costly, highlighting a concrete near-term many-body application.
detailed_summary: Using IBM's Nighthawk superconducting processor, the authors simulate a disordered two-dimensional Heisenberg Floquet model on systems up to 10x10 qubits. They coarse-grain the lattice into spatial patches and use patch collision entropy to quantify when subsystems approach ergodic random-matrix-like behavior. Smaller patches become ergodic at lower coupling J than larger ones, revealing a hierarchy in the onset of thermalization across length scales. Where tensor-network methods remain tractable, the error-mitigated quantum results agree well with classical calculations; beyond that regime, classical cost rises rapidly due to volume-law entanglement.
main_result: The work demonstrates scale-resolved onset of ergodicity on a large digital superconducting quantum processor, with local patches thermalizing before larger subsystems.
application_angle: It positions superconducting processors as useful tools for studying non-equilibrium many-body physics in regimes that increasingly strain classical simulation.

title: Qubit measurement and backaction in a multimode nonreciprocal system
abstract: High fidelity qubit readout is a cornerstone for quantum information protocols. In traditional superconducting qubit readout, a chain of microwave amplifiers and nonreciprocal components aid in detecting the qubit's state with tolerable added noise and backaction. However, the loss, size, and magnetic field of standard nonreciprocal components have sparked a decades-long search for more efficient and scalable alternatives. One prominent approach employs networks of parametrically coupled modes to achieve nonreciprocity. While this class of devices can be directly integrated with the qubit's readout cavity, current understanding of the resulting single quantum system is substantially lacking. Here we provide a first-principles theoretical tool to understand and design networks of linear modes integrated with embedded qubits. We utilize this theory to inform and analyze the experimental implementation of a qubit readout with an integrated three-mode nonreciprocal system. In doing so, we achieve excellent agreement between the experimental and theoretical qubit measurement and dephasing rates. We then theoretically analyze the same system operated as an integrated nonreciprocal amplifier, predicting high efficiency for reasonable experimental parameters.
one_liner: A first-principles theory and experiment quantify qubit readout and backaction in an integrated three-mode nonreciprocal superconducting system.
why_it_matters: It advances compact, scalable qubit readout by replacing bulky ferrite-based nonreciprocal hardware with integrated parametric mode networks.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a theoretical framework for linear multimode nonreciprocal networks coupled to embedded superconducting qubits. The model is used to analyze an experimental three-mode integrated nonreciprocal readout system, reproducing measured qubit readout and dephasing rates with strong agreement. The same framework is then applied to the device operated as an integrated nonreciprocal amplifier, predicting high measurement efficiency for realistic parameters. The work clarifies how gain, directionality, and measurement backaction trade off in a single integrated quantum system.
platform_or_system: superconducting qubit with three-mode nonreciprocal microwave network
main_result: The main result is quantitative theory-experiment agreement for qubit measurement and dephasing in an integrated nonreciprocal readout architecture, plus favorable amplifier predictions.
application_angle: This supports more compact cryogenic readout stacks for scalable superconducting processors by integrating nonreciprocity and amplification closer to the qubit.

title: Absence of Charge Offset Drift in a Transmon Qubit
abstract: Superconducting quantum circuits are sensitive to their electrostatic environment: uncontrolled charges accumulating on the electrodes of a Josephson junction shift the energy levels of a qubit, perturbing its operation and restricting their design. This effect is captured by a single parameter - the charge offset - whose slow, unpredictable drift has proven difficult to eliminate in practice. Here, we report a tantalum-based transmon qubit in which the charge offset remains pinned at zero over nearly three months of measurements, including two thermal cycles, with no observable compromise to the qubit lifetime. This exceptional stability disappears in later cooldowns, indicating a fragile mechanism at play. We attribute it to the inductance of a thin superconducting layer inadvertently formed in parallel with the Josephson junction during fabrication. X-ray surface spectroscopy suggests this layer arises from an incomplete wet-etch of tantalum on sapphire. Deliberately engineering such a layer offers a route to eliminating charge-offset drift in superconducting circuits more broadly.
one_liner: A tantalum transmon exhibits nearly three months of pinned zero charge offset, suppressing the usual slow drift without hurting lifetime.
why_it_matters: Charge-offset drift limits qubit stability and design freedom, so showing a route to eliminate it could materially improve superconducting hardware robustness.
detailed_summary: The authors report a tantalum-based transmon whose charge offset stayed pinned at zero for nearly three months, spanning two thermal cycles, while maintaining qubit lifetime. This behavior contrasts with the usual slow and unpredictable offset drift seen in superconducting circuits. Materials analysis and device modeling point to a thin unintended superconducting layer in parallel with the Josephson junction, likely caused by incomplete wet-etch of tantalum on sapphire. The effect disappears in later cooldowns, indicating a fragile but potentially engineerable mechanism.
main_result: The main result is the observation of essentially drift-free charge offset in a transmon over long timescales without an evident coherence penalty.
application_angle: If deliberately reproducible, this mechanism could reduce calibration instability and broaden viable superconducting qubit designs for larger processors.

title: Hybrid Analog-Digital Simulation of the Abelian Higgs model
abstract: To investigate gauge theories with near-term quantum computers warrants exploration of nontraditional quantum simulators to find resource-efficient simulation protocols and ultimately access exotic features of different field theories, including unexplored regimes of the QCD phase diagram. In this work, using superconducting transmon qutrit processors, we formulate and implement a pulse-based, three-level, hybrid analog-digital simulation protocol of the (1+1) dimensional Abelian Higgs model (AHM) on two sites. Alongside this approach, we experimentally realize a gate-based implementation of the same model. Using the natural mapping of the three-level truncation of the transmon Hilbert space to the spin-1 truncated AHM, we observe real time dynamics of AHM field observables, which are analogous to electric field operators, with both protocols. For the analog-digital protocol, we engineer a Floquet simulation with a combination of local analog drives, driven modification of the natural interaction Hamiltonian of the two transmons, and dynamical decoupling pulses. For the digital protocol, we use a state-of-the-art qutrit processor to implement a Trotterized simulation of the model incorporating advanced error mitigation techniques. We further discuss the scalability of the two approaches, and their potential to be extended to the simulation of other model Hamiltonians. Our experiments demonstrate a viable platform for future studies of spin-1 and SU(3) based gauge theory models on current and near-term transmon qutrit processors.
one_liner: Transmon qutrits realize both analog-digital and gate-based simulations of the 1+1D Abelian Higgs model on two sites.
why_it_matters: It shows that higher-level superconducting hardware can encode lattice gauge models more naturally and with lower resource overhead than qubit-only approaches.
detailed_summary: The work implements a spin-1 truncated Abelian Higgs model on a two-site superconducting transmon-qutrit platform using two complementary methods. In the hybrid analog-digital version, the authors engineer Floquet dynamics through local drives, interaction modulation, and dynamical decoupling; in parallel, they realize a Trotterized digital protocol with error mitigation on a qutrit processor. Both approaches reproduce real-time dynamics of field observables analogous to electric-field operators. The paper also analyzes scalability and extension to broader spin-1 and SU(3)-type gauge-theory simulations.
main_result: The main result is an experimental two-site Abelian Higgs simulation on transmon qutrits using both pulse-level analog-digital and gate-based digital protocols.
application_angle: This provides a resource-efficient route for near-term quantum simulation of gauge theories by exploiting native multilevel superconducting hardware instead of forcing qubit-only encodings.

title: Efficient Quantum Simulation for Nonlinear Stochastic Differential Equations
abstract: Nonlinear stochastic differential equations (NSDEs) are a pillar of mathematical modeling for scientific and engineering applications. Accurate and efficient simulation of large-scale NSDEs is prohibitive on classical computers due to the large number of degrees of freedom, and it is challenging on quantum computers due to the linear and unitary nature of quantum mechanics. We develop a quantum algorithm to tackle nonlinear differential equations driven by the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) stochastic process. The query complexity of our algorithm scales logarithmically with the error tolerance and nearly quadratically with the simulation time. Our algorithmic framework comprises probabilistic Carleman linearization (PCL) to tackle nonlinearity coupled with stochasticity, and stochastic linear combination of Hamiltonian simulations (SLCHS) to simulate stochastic non-unitary dynamics. We obtain probabilistic exponential convergence for the Carleman linearization of Liu et al. [1], provided the NSDE is stable and reaches a steady state. We extend deterministic LCHS to stochastic linear differential equations, retaining near-optimal parameter scaling from An et al. [2] except for the nearly quadratic time scaling. This is achieved by using Monte Carlo integration for time discretization of both the stochastic inhomogeneous term in LCHS and the truncated Dyson series for each Hamiltonian simulation.
one_liner: A quantum algorithm combines probabilistic Carleman linearization and stochastic Hamiltonian simulation to solve nonlinear SDEs efficiently.
why_it_matters: Nonlinear stochastic models are central in science and engineering, so improved quantum scaling for them could broaden quantum advantage beyond physics-native problems.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a quantum algorithm for nonlinear stochastic differential equations driven by Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise. Its framework combines probabilistic Carleman linearization to handle nonlinear stochastic dynamics with a stochastic linear-combination-of-Hamiltonian-simulations method for the resulting non-unitary evolution. Under stability and steady-state assumptions, the authors show probabilistic exponential convergence of the truncation and logarithmic scaling in error tolerance, with near-quadratic dependence on simulation time. They also extend deterministic LCHS ideas to stochastic linear systems via Monte Carlo discretization of both inhomogeneous stochastic terms and truncated Dyson-series simulations.
main_result: The main result is an efficient quantum algorithm for a class of nonlinear stochastic differential equations with logarithmic error scaling and controlled stochastic simulation overhead.
application_angle: The method targets practical simulation workloads in engineering and scientific modeling where large nonlinear stochastic systems are classically expensive to solve.

title: Distributed Quantum Computing via Adaptive Circuit Knitting
abstract: Distributing quantum workloads over many Quantum Processing Units (QPUs) is a crucial step in scaling up quantum computers toward practical quantum advantage due to the limitations in size of a single QPU. In the absence of high-fidelity quantum interconnects, circuit knitting could provide a path to computing certain properties of large quantum systems on many QPUs of limited size in a distributed fashion using only classical communication. Circuit knitting partitions large quantum circuits into manageable sub-circuits, however, reconstructing observables in a straightforward manner comes at an exponential cost in sampling and classical post-processing. To mitigate the overhead this technique incurs, we introduce an Adaptive Circuit Knitting (ACK) method that finds efficient partitions of quantum circuits by discovering regions of minimal entanglement between subsystems. We simulate 1D and 2D disordered mixed-field Ising models up to 60 qubits and show that the ACK approach can reduce circuit knitting sampling overheads by up to four orders of magnitude for observables of interest. We highlight our parallel GPU-accelerated implementation and discuss the need for efficient classical simulators to enable distributed quantum algorithm development. Our techniques could enable efficient distribution of quantum simulation for both near-term and fault-tolerant architectures.
one_liner: Adaptive circuit knitting cuts distributed quantum simulation overhead by up to four orders of magnitude on disordered Ising models up to 60 qubits.
why_it_matters: It makes partition-based use of multiple small QPUs far more practical when quantum interconnects are weak or unavailable.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces Adaptive Circuit Knitting, a method that partitions large circuits by identifying weakly entangled boundaries between subsystems before reconstruction. On 1D and 2D disordered mixed-field Ising models up to 60 qubits, the authors report reductions in sampling and classical post-processing overheads by as much as 10,000x for relevant observables. The workflow is implemented with parallel GPU-accelerated simulation, emphasizing the classical tooling needed to make distributed quantum workloads usable on networks of limited-size QPUs.
main_result: ACK finds low-entanglement circuit cuts that reduce circuit-knitting overheads by up to four orders of magnitude in simulated many-body workloads.
application_angle: The method directly supports scaling useful quantum simulations across multiple small processors without requiring high-fidelity quantum links.

title: Doppler-induced tunable and shape-preserving frequency conversion of microwave wave packets
abstract: In superconducting electronics, the ability to control the frequency of microwave wave packets is crucial for several applications, such as the operation of superconducting quantum processors and the readout of superconducting sensors. We introduce a new approach to microwave frequency conversion harnessing a dynamic Doppler effect induced by a propagating front separating regions of different phase velocities. Employing a high-kinetic-inductance superconducting transmission line in a travelling-wave geometry, we were able to implement frequency shifts of microwave wave packets at 500 MHz and 4 GHz of up to 3.7 % while fully preserving their temporal shape. In contrast to conventional methods based on frequency-mixing, our Doppler-induced frequency-conversion method avoids spurious mixing products, is continuously tunable by a quasi-dc current amplitude, and allows to imprint arbitrary patterns on the instantaneous frequency profile of temporally long wave packets. By engineering transmission lines that allow for larger phase-velocity changes and/or by cascading multiple Doppler-induced frequency conversions, an unlimited amount of frequency shifting is in principle attainable. These features demonstrate the potential of our frequency-conversion technique as a promising tool for advanced control of microwave wave packets for different quantum applications.
one_liner: A travelling-wave superconducting line uses a Doppler front to continuously shift microwave packet frequency while preserving pulse shape.
why_it_matters: Clean, tunable microwave frequency conversion is valuable for superconducting qubit control, interconnects, and sensor readout without mixer spurs.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate microwave frequency conversion using a propagating front between regions of different phase velocity in a high-kinetic-inductance superconducting transmission line. They achieve shape-preserving shifts for wave packets at 500 MHz and 4 GHz, with tunable conversion up to 3.7%, controlled by a quasi-dc current amplitude. Unlike conventional mixing schemes, the approach avoids spurious sidebands and can imprint designed instantaneous frequency patterns on long microwave pulses.
main_result: The device performs continuously tunable, shape-preserving Doppler frequency shifts of microwave packets up to 3.7% without generating mixing products.
application_angle: This offers a practical microwave control primitive for superconducting processors and cryogenic sensors where spectral purity and waveform integrity matter.

title: Entanglement-Assisted Discrimination of Nonlocal Sets of Orthogonal States
abstract: Entanglement-assisted discrimination of orthogonal quantum states exhibiting quantum nonlocality is a frontier topic in quantum information theory. In this paper, we investigate the role of multipartite entanglement and develop resource-efficient LOCC discrimination protocols for nonlocal sets of orthogonal states, including multipartite orthogonal product-state sets and entangled-state sets with different nonlocal features. By incorporating controlled-NOT (CNOT) operations into the discrimination procedure, we construct protocols for genuinely nonlocal GHZ bases in four- and five-qubit systems that require only a single EPR pair. For the same target sets, we compare different entanglement-assisted schemes and identify those with lower entanglement consumption. We further observe that, on average, protocols avoiding teleportation consume fewer resources than teleportation-based approaches. In addition, when higher-partite GHZ-type resources (with $n>3$) are available among suitable subsystems, they can in some cases reduce the overall entanglement cost. Our results highlight the operational significance of multipartite entanglement and provide practical protocols for the local discrimination of orthogonal state sets exhibiting quantum nonlocality.
one_liner: Resource-efficient LOCC protocols discriminate nonlocal orthogonal state sets using minimal shared entanglement, including one EPR pair for 4- and 5-qubit GHZ bases.
why_it_matters: Lower entanglement cost makes nonlocal-state discrimination more realistic for distributed quantum communication and networked-information tasks.
detailed_summary: The work develops entanglement-assisted LOCC protocols for distinguishing orthogonal state sets that exhibit quantum nonlocality, covering multipartite product and entangled-state families. By incorporating CNOT operations, the authors show that genuinely nonlocal four- and five-qubit GHZ bases can be discriminated using only a single EPR pair. They further compare protocol classes and find that non-teleportation schemes typically consume less entanglement, while higher-partite GHZ resources can sometimes reduce total cost further.
main_result: The paper gives explicit low-resource discrimination protocols, including one-EPR-pair schemes for genuinely nonlocal 4- and 5-qubit GHZ bases.
application_angle: Efficient nonlocal-state discrimination is directly relevant to quantum networking, distributed protocols, and communication tasks constrained by scarce shared entanglement.

title: Dynamics of Many-Emitter Ensembles: Probing Cooperative Evolution with Scalable Quantum Circuits
abstract: Many-particle quantum systems often give rise to exotic behaviors in their nonequilibrium dynamics that are rather challenging to reveal with analytical methods or with classical computation. Here, we consider the case of a system of many quantum emitters coupled through a radiation bath. By adopting an efficient mapping of the bosonic modes onto a set of quantum bits, we implement quantum circuits, compatible with NISQ (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum) era systems, that allow us to investigate the dynamics of the ensemble as a function of various parameters, including the number of emitters, the spectral inhomogeneity in the system, the emission lifetime of independent emitters, and the spatial separation between emitters. The quantum algorithms afford us the capacity to precisely track the emergence of cooperative dynamics, manifested through superradiant emission, as the system is tuned towards optimal coupling with respect to various parameters. We are particularly able to characterize superradiant emission in an inhomogeneous ensemble as a function of the linewidth of the individual emitters. These quantum algorithms avoid approximations performed in conventional studies of many-emitter systems and provide a robust and intuitive characterization. Despite being limited to a small number of qubits, the present calculations are found to provide a reliable characterization validated by comparison with analytical solutions and classical computation results in their respective regimes of validity. These findings indicate that the approach can be employed to effectively simulate a broad variety of many-emitter systems.
one_liner: Scalable quantum circuits simulate cooperative emission in many-emitter ensembles and track the onset of superradiance under realistic inhomogeneity.
why_it_matters: It provides a hardware-compatible route to studying complex light-matter dynamics that are difficult for classical methods to capture accurately.
detailed_summary: The authors map bosonic radiation-bath modes onto qubits and construct NISQ-compatible circuits to model emitter ensembles coupled through a common bath. The simulations resolve how cooperative emission depends on emitter count, spectral inhomogeneity, independent-emitter lifetime, and inter-emitter spacing, with particular attention to superradiant behavior. Results are benchmarked against analytical and classical methods where valid, showing that even small-qubit implementations can recover the key cooperative dynamics without the approximations used in conventional treatments.
platform_or_system: many-emitter radiative ensemble on NISQ circuits
main_result: The approach reproduces and characterizes superradiant dynamics in inhomogeneous emitter ensembles using scalable quantum-circuit simulations.
application_angle: It can guide the design and analysis of collective-emission photonic materials and quantum-optical devices where many-emitter effects are operationally important.

title: Pointwise mutual information bounded by stochastic Fisher information
abstract: We derive general upper bounds to pointwise mutual information in terms of stochastic Fisher information and show these bounds average to known results in the literature for bounds to mutual information in terms of Fisher information. These results deepen the connection between information-theoretical quantities and are shown to hold in general cases. We test the bounds in classical systems and provide a quantum generalization. Our results are useful for stochastic dynamics, quantum sensing and quantum communication, providing a less costly way to realize and saturate the bounds.
one_liner: The paper derives general bounds linking pointwise mutual information to stochastic Fisher information, including a quantum extension.
why_it_matters: These bounds offer cheaper information-performance estimates for sensing and communication settings than full mutual-information evaluation.
detailed_summary: This work establishes upper bounds on pointwise mutual information in terms of stochastic Fisher information and shows that averaging recovers known mutual-information bounds. The framework is developed for general classical stochastic systems and then extended to quantum scenarios. Because Fisher-information-like quantities are often easier to compute or estimate experimentally, the results provide operationally useful surrogates for assessing information flow and performance limits.
platform_or_system: general stochastic and quantum information systems
main_result: The authors prove general pointwise mutual-information bounds from stochastic Fisher information and extend the relationship to quantum cases.
application_angle: The theory can simplify performance analysis in quantum sensing and communication protocols by replacing costly information calculations with more accessible Fisher-information estimates.

title: Tunable supercontinuum in multimode fiber via bending-induced dispersion modification
